atmosphereAnalysis.description: A qualitative analysis of the local atmosphere.
atmosphereAnalysis.hints: Technical comments on the chemical composition and general characteristics of the atmospheric mix.

KerbinSrfLandedWater*: The air here is very humid. Water keeps splashing into the sensor, creating inaccuracies and sending sparks flying everywhere.
The air being more terrestrial or marine depends on the time of day and wind direction.
KerbinSrfLandedGrasslands*: There's lots of gaseous nitrogen and methane here - clear evidence of both greenery and cows to eat it.
You decide to do one better and smell the air yourself. Ah, smells like home.
KerbinSrfLandedHighlands*: The analysis shows that it is often rainy and foggy here. There's a faint trace of ethanol mixed with oak.
The thick mist of Kerbin's Highlands is of much interest to the scientists back at Mission Control.
KerbinSrfLandedShores*: The air here is very humid, and seems to have a bit of a fishy smell to it.
A crab decides to wedge itself firmly inside the atmospheric sensor's housing. You end up having to reset the experiment.
KerbinSrfLandedMountains*: Not only is it cold up here, but the air is thin as well. The goat one peak over is shivering.
The sensor tells you that you'll be gasping for breath soon after stepping out of your craft.
KerbinSrfLandedDeserts*: It's very hot and dry out in the desert. The sensors are getting clogged up with dust.
This would be a great place to work on your tan.
This can't be right, the sensor is detecting the atmosphere as solid! Oh wait, it's just dust-clogged again.
KerbinSrfLandedBadlands*: The sensor complains about a mosquito dive-bombing it. Apparently it's carrying malaria.
It's not so bad here. You wonder what all the fuss was about.
The Gooey puddle you just stepped in appears to be eating through the material of your spacesuit. Better get back to the ship before the atmosphere outside is the same as inside your spacesuit.
KerbinSrfLandedTundra*: The atmosphere is very cold and very dry.
The sensors report that the air is cold but otherwise nominal.
KerbinSrfLandedIceCaps*: The polar atmosphere is very, very cold indeed. Some of the sensors have frozen shut, but we're reasonably sure this is still air.
It's a tiny bit nippy here.
The air is dry and cold here. It's almost like you are standing in a walk-in freezer.
KerbinSrfLandedNorthernIceShelf*: The air is mostly cold and dry, with a hint of freezing sea breeze.
An unexpected squall of polar air hits the craft, and fills the analyser with ice crystals.
KerbinSrfLandedSouthernIceShelf*: No hint of any complex organic molecules. Just air and ice crystals.
The freezing polar wind whistles past the craft as the sensor scoops it up. You're glad you're not out there.
KerbinFlyingLowWater*: Spray from the water adds water and salt to the atmospheric composition.
The humidity of the air seems to decrease with altitude.
KerbinFlyingLowGrasslands*: The atmosphere seems calm and peaceful here. Readings are nominal.
Ah so this is what a life-supporting atmosphere is supposed to look like.
KerbinFlyingLowHighlands*: Even at this low altitude the atmosphere seems thin here. Thoughts of a space-sherpa come to mind.
The atmospheric sensor reports higher-than-average humidity. This is supported by the thick fog and the light sheen of wet grass.
KerbinFlyingLowShores*: A nosy seagull gets its beak stuck in the atmosphere sensor's housing. You have to reset the experiment.
Sand gets sucked into your instrument and you are unable to get an accurate reading. Oh well, snack time!
KerbinFlyingLowMountains*: The atmospheric analysis reads "refreshing" in the cool mountain air. You couldn't agree more.
Strong mountain winds buffet your craft. The atmospheric sensor is beside itself with joy.
KerbinFlyingLowDeserts*: You find that the air is much drier over the desert.
Very little signs of precipitation. No wonder the place looks so thirsty.
KerbinFlyingLowBadlands*: The sensor records a sulfurous rotten egg smell in the atmosphere. So this is why they call it the Badlands.
Atmospheric analysis of the surrounding air makes you very glad that you brought your spacesuit.
KerbinFlyingLowTundra*: You try to avoid snowdrifts in order to give the atmosphere sensor a better reading.
The atmosphere analysis sensor gets clogged up with all the dust flying around. You report this back to KSC.
KerbinFlyingLowIceCaps*: The cold air has caused some of the instruments to stop functioning. You send the results you can back to KSC and settle down with a mug of hot cocoa and some snacks.
You feel sorry for the atmospheric sensor, exposed as it is to the subzero temperatures of Kerbin's polar regions.
KerbinFlyingLowNorthernIceShelf*: There are strong traces of windblown snow kicked up by the powerful winds that blow over the icy flats.
At least in the absence of hills you don't have to pay attention to very much while the gadget does its thing.
KerbinFlyingLowSouthernIceShelf*: Water content of the air is low, despite the large amounts of frozen water not far below.
You wonder how cold it would need to be for you to hold a pot out the window and scoop the air up like a solid.
KerbinFlyingHighWater*: No salt an spray up here. Just the normal atmosphere-y....gassy....stuff.
Upon examining the sensor's readouts from up high, you firmly decide against cracking open a window.
KerbinFlyingHighGrasslands*: As you read the atmospheric analysis report you glance down at the surface below. The gentle undulating motion of the breeze through the grass makes you feel sleepy.
You let the atmospheric sensor do its thing. You don't anticipate any earth-shaking discoveries.
KerbinFlyingHighHighlands*: Pfft, Highlands yeah right. Whoever named this place must have pretty low standards.
The atmosphere here seems squeezed between the high altitude of the mountains below, and the invisible barrier of space above. You are somewhere between a rock and no-place.
KerbinFlyingHighShores*: Atmospheric analysis reports humidity and trace amounts of salt in the air.
The scanner registers a faint trace of seaweed and crabs, even way up here.
KerbinFlyingHighMountains*: The air here seems crisp and refreshing. Now time for a refreshing snack!
The air gets progressively thinner as your craft climbs higher. You're happy you didn't have to do this experiment on your own two feet.
KerbinFlyingHighDeserts*: The sensor reads 0 humidity. In fact it looks like the needle is bent as the gauge tries to register negative numbers.
The humidity readings make you want to take off your spacesuit and lather yourself in lotion.
KerbinFlyingHighBadlands*: Analysis of the Badlands' atmosphere makes it difficult to believe that you're still on Kerbin.
Glancing nervously at the sensor's readouts, you sincerely hope that Mission Control doesn't tell you to go lower.
Ozone, check. Carbon Monoxide, check. Hydro-fluorocarbons, check. Yep, we're in the Badlands.
KerbinFlyingHighTundra*: The atmosphere here seems unforgiving. Kind of like you are about eating all of Bill's snacks.
The data from the atmospheric analysis makes you happy that you're wearing layers under your spacesuit.
KerbinFlyingHighIceCaps*: Even this far away the Ice Caps seem to be sucking the heat and moisture from the atmosphere around you. We're not going to land down there, are we?
Atmospheric analysis is complete. Can we go home now? It's cold.
KerbinFlyingHighNorthernIceShelf*: The ice has petered out by now, but there's still enough moisture in the air to glaze the sensor in ice.
The gas mix high above the ice plains seems much the same as over the equator.
KerbinFlyingHighSouthernIceShelf*: Legends of wispy, shimmering clouds have been drifting around the polar kerbal communities for millennia. Turns out they are real, and made of ice crystals.
The high-altitude polar vortex brings cold, dry, thin air from other parts of the poles, mixing their individual gas signatures.
KerbinSrfLandedKSC*: A surprising amount of paint and ink volatiles, combined with ozone from welding and short circuits. About what you'd expect from a thriving space program.
KerbinSrfLandedAdministration*: The atmosphere heats up as Montimer learns about how our funds have been spend lately.
KerbinSrfLandedAstronautComplex*: The sensor picks up fear and excitement in the atmosphere. Don't ask how.
KerbinSrfLandedBaikerbanur*: The air is clear and sweet. For a rocket launch complex, this is a very bad thing.
KerbinSrfLandedBaikerbanurLaunchpad*: There are a few faint traces of an array of toxic hypergols, their chemical secrets long since lost to time.
KerbinSrfLandedCrawlerway*: It's surprisingly windy out in the open.
KerbinSrfLandedFlagPole*: You install the sensor on top of the flag pole.
The sensor tells you that the flag is working as advertised.
KerbinSrfLandedLaunchPad*: Apparently, the atmosphere is made of ash and rocket fuel. But that might be because you're on the launchpad.
I think a fly just got in the research equipment... don't tell the scientists!
The extremely expensive array of sensors, samplers, and detectors has confirmed that today is a very good day to go to space. Which is nice, considering we already have the rocket out and everything.
The sensor detects an explosive atmosphere. Try getting off the launchpad.
KerbinSrfLandedMissionControl*: The atmosphere is always tense here. You didn't need a sensor to figure that out.
KerbinSrfLandedRnD*: The atmosphere here is very contemplative.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDCentralBuilding*: All the blow-hards around here have caused the anemometer to fall off your instrument.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDCornerLab*: The researchers confiscate your atmospheric sensor and disassemble it to see how it works.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDMainBuilding*: Having duct-taped your sensor back together you attempt to get an atmospheric reading, but it looks like your fix will require some bubblegum, superglue, and maybe a coat hanger as well.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDObservatory*: The sensor picks up faint traces of caffeine in the air. Makes sense with the hours that the R&D team puts in.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDSideLab*: The atmosphere here is thick with fumes of unknown chemicals from unknown reactions. Who would have known this R&D stuff could be dangerous?
KerbinSrfLandedRnDSmallLab*: The atmosphere is heavy with an acrid smell. Who knows what those mad scientists are working on.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDTanks*: The strange chemical concoction leaking from the tanks melts your atmospheric sensors into a puddle of goo. Hey look! It's not-as-much-of-a mystery goo!
KerbinSrfLandedRnDWindTunnel*: The sensor blew away. Duh. It's a wind tunnel. At least we got the grant money already.
KerbinSrfLandedRunway*: Wind speed changes slightly as a small insect passes in front of the sensor.
Readings suggest today is as good a time for flying as any.
Atmospheric conditions look good for takeoff. Punch it!
KerbinSrfLandedSPH*: The sensor registers a cool breeze coming from the spaceplane hangar. We always knew this was a cool place.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHMainBuilding*: The atmosphere here is pure awesomesauce. Really. It doesn't get much better.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHRoundTank*: The sensor notes the slight venturi effect of the wind whipping around the SPH tank.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHTanks*: The sensor cannot determine the wind direction with all the tanks around.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHWaterTower*: The sensor reads less wind at the bottom of the tower than at the top.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStation*: The staff at the tracking station shoo you off as you try to take atmospheric readings, saying that your sensor is interfering with operations.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishEast*: You quickly take you readings, hoping that your tests don't interfere with the sensitive electronics here.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishNorth*: You brazenly take your atmospheric readings, not caring less whether or not you are interfering with tracking operations.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishSouth*: Quick, run! The tracking operators are running toward you with pitchforks for interfering with operations.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationHub*: You lock the door of the tracking station from the outside, knowing that the operators inside will be furious when your atmospheric test equipment interferes with tracking operations.
KerbinSrfLandedVAB*: The atmosphere here is abuzz with activity day and night.
KerbinSrfLandedVABMainBuilding*: The atmosphere here is always cool.
KerbinSrfLandedVABRoundTank*: You find yourself circling around the tank looking for a good place to set up your equipment.
KerbinSrfLandedVABSouthComplex*: Metal shavings and offcuts from the VAB's workshops are everywhere in the air here.
KerbinSrfLandedVABTanks*: The tanks block the sensor from any useful analysis.
KerbinSrfLandedIslandAirfield*: The analyser gives you a very accurate breakdown of the sea breeze.
KerbinSrfLandedWoomerang*: You're pretty sure the atmosphere is the same over here as over there.
KerbinFlyingLowKSC*: Atmosphere analysis??? Let's worry about flying this thing first!
KerbinFlyingHighKSC*: The sensor picks up the distinct signature of rocket flight: traces of unburned hypergolics and hydrocarbons.

DunaSrfLanded: Lots of carbon dioxide... and not much else. Extremophile plants could potentially live here.
255. Definitely 255.
DunaSrfLandedPoles: The water ice crystals here contain levels of deuterium several times higher than on Kerbin.
DunaSrfLandedMidlands: You notice the Atmosphere Analyzer says the warranty is void, but you still think using it was a good idea.
After getting some strange readings, you open up the analysis chamber to check the instruments. A large amount of dust pours out onto the ground.
DunaSrfLandedLowlands: The surface pressure seems to vary wildly as the levels of carbon dioxide and water vapour change with the seasons.
The dust here is fine enough that the sensor doesn't get clogged for once! Rejoice!
DunaSrfLandedCraters: Mission Control says there's air here, but you can't breathe it. You wonder why Duna would even bother having one in that case. It's probably making fun of you or something.
The natural bowl in the landscape means the sensor reads higher than average dust concentrations.
DunaSrfLandedHighlands: You fear the dust will never quite come out.
The more rarefied air up here displays marginally greater concentrations of argon and nitrogen over the denser carbon dioxide.
DunaSrfLandedMidlandSea: The sensor beeps, clicks and whirrs as it works. You wish you had a protocol droid handy so you could tell what it was saying to itself.

You put a small crystal from the subsurface ice into the analyser to see what would happen. It melts, and after more heating the analyser reports its vapour contains much more deuterium than Kerbin's.
DunaSrfLandedNorthernBasin: The dust is quite coarse here, and the gas mixtures are fairly Duna-standard.
DunaSrfLandedSouthernBasin: Here the dust of the lowlands mixes with ice crystals blowing off the ice caps, creating reddish snow.
DunaSrfLandedNorthernShelf: The winds blowing down the canyon from the north carry with them the faintest traces of ice crystals.
The detector finds a tiny wisp of krypton-then it's gone. Superkerbal better stay away.
DunaSrfLandedMidlandCanyon: The fine dust here means the analyser actually records the atmospheric composition for once without being filled with sand...let's see...lots of CO2, some Ar and N2...and not much else.
DunaSrfLandedEasternCanyon: There seems to be LOT more dust down here in the thicker canyon air, which acts as a dust sink for the surrounding hills.
DunaSrfLandedPolarHighlands: The sensor registers the large amount of windblown ice crystals and lower dust count.
DunaSrfLandedPolarCraters: Lots of airborne ices-analysis reports they consist of varying ratios of hydroxic acid and carbonic anhydride. Sounds scary.
DunaFlyingLow: The atmosphere appears to be significantly less dense than that of Kerbin.
DunaFlyingLowPoles: Gas geysers firing below fill the atmosphere with dust and ice.
DunaFlyingLowMidlands: The analyser develops a caking of dust, so you jolt the craft from side to side to shake it off.
This air is thin at best. The fine grains of dust that get caught in the intake don't help the scanner.
DunaFlyingLowLowlands: The air is so thin there's hardly any wind. You contemplate how well paper airplanes would work, absent-mindedly folding your mission papers into... *ahem*.
You could get used to this, flying serenely through air-that's-not-really-there while scanning it with a gizmo.
DunaFlyingLowCraters: It's very hard to take readings, as every few minutes you have to clear iron oxide dust from the sensors.
You get distracted by staring at the crater and miss the readings, so you just note down '100% dust'.
DunaFlyingLowHighlands: Despite the dust storms, there's not much wind. The scanner indicates that the dust is incredibly small and easy to blow around.
Not much air up here to carry the dust, but air currents from orographic lift carry the finest of particles over the summits.
DunaFlyingLowMidlandSea: The inlet valve for the air has frozen shut yet again, reminding you that it's still freezing outside.
CO2, Ar, N2 and...Fe2O3? You're pretty sure that last one isn't a gas.
DunaFlyingLowNorthernBasin: The dust seems to stay low to the ground here, as there's not much off it off the ground.
DunaFlyingLowSouthernBasin: The ice crystals appear to stay closer to the ground, up in the air it's mostly dust. And CO2. And N2. And Ar.
DunaFlyingLowNorthernShelf: The analyser bleeps loudly over the noise of the wind, signifying the completion of its analysis.
The air here looks normal, if a little thicker thanks to the low altitude. And it's still really dusty.
DunaFlyingLowMidlandCanyon: Faint traces of ice crystals are detected, perhaps being carried into the canyon by the Northern Shelf winds?
DunaFlyingLowEasternCanyon: Wow, that's a lot of dust down below. So much, it's still detectable in the air above.
DunaFlyingLowWesternCanyon: No significant traces of water vapour up here. Or on the ground. Or anywhere else on Duna, for that matter.
DunaFlyingLowPolarHighlands: Much of the atmosphere is particles that've blown off the surface. That, or we forgot to clean the sensor before departure.
DunaFlyingLowPolarCraters: The wind picks up, and a cloud of water and carbon dioxide ice crystals fills the air.
DunaFlyingHigh: Carbon dioxide, with appreciable amounts of argon and nitrogen and traces of oxygen and carbon monoxide are detected through spectroscopic analysis of the local atmosphere.
DunaFlyingHighPoles: A sharp spike in airborne ice crystals is detected as you fly through one of Duna's wispy polar clouds.
DunaFlyingHighMidlands: The concentrations of argon, nitrogen and other trace gases are significantly higher than the surface up here.
The relative absence of dust up here allows for largely uninterrupted atmospheric sampling.
DunaFlyingHighLowlands: The atmosphere here is largely devoid of the fine dust from the lower regions, but radiation levels are also almost certainly higher.
Still CO2. Come to think of it, we're actually at the same altitude as everywhere else, just further off the ground. You think.
DunaFlyingHighCraters: Looks pretty normal from up here. Ground topography doesn't seem to affect the upper atmosphere much.
Although thin, Duna's atmosphere can be more than thick enough to aerobrake in.
DunaFlyingHighHighlands: Lots of carbon dioxide. The high compressibility and low density might make atmospheric entry heat less intense for landers.
Higher even than the mountaintops, extremely faint and wispy strips of cloud flow behind the mountain. Flying through them shows they are made of ice.
DunaFlyingHighMidlandSea: Duna's rarefied atmospheric gas mixture at these altitudes is starting to separate, and concentrations of gases fluctuate with altitude.
Concentrations of water vapour up here are slightly higher than the rest of the planet, but it's a small enough difference to be a blip.
DunaFlyingHighNorthernBasin: You have to crank the sensitivity on the detector all the way up to get any readings in the extra-thin air.
DunaFlyingHighSouthernBasin: Climate cells and northerly air currents ensure no ice is detected up here.
DunaFlyingHighNorthernShelf: The Northern Shelf and its influence on the atmosphere is far below you.
A dust particle that gets caught in the sensor turns out to be from the Northern Basin-is this evidence of global air currents?
DunaFlyingHighMidlandCanyon: Now, the one time dust isn't an issue, the analyser blows a fuse and stops working anyway. Poxy thing.
DunaFlyingHighEasternCanyon: The canyon is so large, it even causes the streams of upper-atmosphere dust and denser gases to sink into it.
DunaFlyingHighWesternCanyon: The computer asks you rather crossly why you're making it analyse a vacuum. Seems the air really is thin up here.
DunaFlyingHighPolarHighlands: The atmosphere here seems the same as it is anywhere else.
DunaFlyingHighPolarCraters: The inlet on the analyser develops a layer of frost in the lower regions of the upper atmosphere. Curious, considering it didn't have any water on it.

JoolFlyingLow*: It's so incredibly dense.
Engineers scramble to adjust calculations to account for the new, very large crack in the instrument casing.
The drag and pressure almost shuts down the science machinery. The main report reads: "Pressure Warning: Gain altitude immediately!"
The computer running the atmosphere analysis tool crashed. That's probably not good. Upon return, you plan to have a word with the engineers who designed this computer.
The atmosphere here is made of little but hydrogen and helium.
"Do you smell that? That smelly smell... it smells... smelly..."
The atmosphere seems to be very thick here.
The atmosphere is getting much denser now. However, it appears to be unusually transparent.
Don't be silly.
The chlorine content is extremely high here, explaining how the planet got its distinctive green color.
The instrument housing is starting to buckle under the immense pressure.
The instrument says "No refunds if crashed into a gas giant". Darn.
JoolFlyingHigh*: The atmosphere seems to consist mostly of hydrogen and chlorine.
The sensors detect high concentrations of chlorine and helium. You are fascinated, but suspect your findings will cause some headaches for planetary scientists back on Kerbin.
The atmosphere analysis reveals unexpectedly high concentrations of molecular chlorine. You try not to think about what this corrosive gas might be doing to the spacecraft.
Microscopic diamonds, tossed up from liquid carbon far below, are detected in the scanner.
The Joolian clouds contain the expected hydrogen and helium, with substantial amounts of chlorine present.
The atmosphere consists mostly of hydrogen and helium-3 with traces of chlorine. Don't breathe it!

LaytheSrfLanded: The cold temperatures and thin atmosphere appear to have balanced each other out. A layer of high humidity hangs just over the water.
LaytheSrfLandedPoles: There's definitely some oxygen in the atmosphere, but not much. Hypoxia would set in after only a few minutes without a respirator.
LaytheSrfLandedShores: You are amazed by how similar the readings are to Kerbin.
LaytheSrfLandedDunes: The air is thinner here than on the mountain peaks!
LaytheFlyingLow: According to these readings, the air here is not, in fact, breathable. You feel satisfied knowing that you won your bet with the science department.
LaytheFlyingLowPoles: Black smoke, currants, cappuccino, and a hint of buttery beef.
LaytheFlyingLowShores: There's Oxygen, Nitrogen, Hydrogen, and lots of water. You scribble down a note: "Invest in colonization program."
LaytheFlyingLowDunes: The atmosphere appears to be made up of oxygen and nitrogen. You wonder at the high chlorine content, though.
LaytheFlyingHigh: The air has breathable elements, but they're very cold and very thin.
LaytheFlyingHighPoles: The instruments detect hydrogen escaping into space. It would seem that the source of oxygen on Laythe is photo-dissociation of its water, and not photosynthesis.
LaytheFlyingHighShores: The preliminary readings show an atmosphere nearly identical to Kerbin.
LaytheFlyingHighDunes: The atmosphere reminds you of Kerbin; although, you wonder where all the nitrogen came from.

barometerScan.hints: Simple remarks on air density or pressure at the current location. On airless bodies, simple confirmation that there is no atmosphere

EveSrfLandedPoles: The barometer still gives pressure readings, but it may not do so for long.
EveSrfLandedMidlands: As the data from the barometer comes in, you start to feel very unsure about sending any Kerbals there.
The manual for the barometer claims it is rated up to 40atm. You start to doubt the validity of that claim.
EveSrfLandedExplodiumSea: The pressure is crushing down here! You start to worry if you will ever get back up again.
EveSrfLandedLowlands: You start laughing at the abnormally high reading as you realize you're going to be stuck here for a long time.
It's over 9000...m/s! Well, the Delta-V required to escape the atmosphere anyways.
EveSrfLandedHighlands: I tell you what is high, and that's the atmospheric pressure. Some altitude this is.
EveSrfLandedPeaks: The barometer doesn't seem to like it here.
Whatever you think of the place, the barometer works at least. That alone is a win.
EveSrfLandedImpactEjecta: Under pressure, pushing down on me, pushing down on you, pushing down on we.
You hear a hissing sound, like something is leaking.
EveSrfLandedCraterLake: You tap the glass of the barometer. Nope, it's not broken.
EveSrfLandedWesternSea: All this pressure will make it difficult to take off. At least the beach is nearby. A crushed, corrosive, burning hot beach.
EveSrfLandedOlympus: So high above the lower reaches, the atmospheric pressure is less extreme. It's still very high, though.
You tap the gauge on the barometer, and it goes down rather than up for once.
EveSrfLandedEasternSea: The barometer is spitting out overflow errors.
EveSrfLandedFoothills: Wow, that's high. Are you sure they didn't leave something leaning on the sensor?
You calculate the amount of Delta-V just to escape the atmosphere is greater than that it took to both escape Kerbin's and get here!
EveSrfLandedShallows: The instrument shows you an extremely high pressure and then refuses to work anymore.
EveSrfSplashedPoles: Much to your disappointment, the theory of pressure being slightly lower at the poles has been disproven.
EveSrfSplashedExplodiumSea: Atmosphere? Yes! Way too much of it, in fact.
The pressure is very high. You wonder how high would it be at the bottom of this deep purple sea.
EveSrfSplashedLowlands: The needle spins wildly before the device caves in and begins to fizz.
EveSrfSplashedImpactEjecta: The pressure is standard for sea level, which means the liquid level in this crater must be in line with the global sea level.
EveSrfSplashedCraterLake: As you flick the detector on and watch the needle rise, so does your heart rate.
Crater Lake? More like Crusher Lake.
EveSrfSplashedWesternSea: Five atmospheres. What was that about five being a lucky number?
The device logs minute changes in pressure as the craft bobs up and down on the tiny waves.
EveSrfSplashedEasternSea: Judging by how the craft is floating, the pressure would be immense under the surface as well.
You can't tell the exact pressure after the labels burned off. Not like you really need to know the minutiae, anyway.
EveSrfSplashedShallows: Sea level pressure on Eve is the same everywhere, it seems.
You don't want to look at the barometer, so you distract yourself by looking out over the sea instead.
EveFlyingLow: The barometer makes a strange "pinging" sound as its sides bend inwards.
The pressure is high and the atmosphere is thick. You begin to wonder if this is going to be a one-way mission.
The barometer would be broken if not for its ability to move the decimal really far to the right.
You secretly wish your friends and family the best as landing here may be the last landing you'll ever make.
EveFlyingHigh: The barometer is reading the same pressures as sea level on Kerbin. This would be fine, if not for how much of the sky is still below you.
High? You call this high? I tell you what is high, and that's the pressure!
You don't like where you're going, and the data from the barometer only makes you even more concerned.
EveInSpaceLow: A vacuum couldn't be more of a far cry from the conditions at the surface.
The readout from the barometer spells "DONT SEND ME DOWN THERE". You concur.
EveInSpaceHigh: There's no air up here. Not even Eve's mighty atmosphere makes it this far into space.
Eve watches you malevolently as you feel the compulsion to measure the air pressure in space.

GillySrfLandedMidlands: Nil. As though a tiny space potato would ever have an atmosphere.
The tiny gravity isn't even enough to make the needle settle on the bottom of the gauge.
GillySrfLandedLowlands: There's some dust, but it hardly counts as an atmosphere.
You expected more, given how Gilly is so many times bigger than the piddly asteroids flying around.
GillySrfLandedHighlands: You reach for your notepad to write a zero on, but accidentally bat it across the cabin.
Dust kicked up by the spacecraft just doesn't land, and settles in the barometer.
GillyInSpaceLow: You're barely even an orbit, much less landed and surrounded by air.
You could swear tapping the barometer here would perturb your orbit enough to crash.
GillyInSpaceHigh: You're more preoccupied with making sure you don't accidentally break Gilly orbit.
A screw pings out of the barometer housing and drifts slowly down to the surface.

KerbinSrfLandedWater*: Hopefully the folks back at KSC can use this barometer data to warn you of an incoming storm.
The barometer's analysis hints at unusually high humidity in the local atmosphere.
KerbinSrfLandedGrasslands*: A blade of grass gets caught in the outer casing of the sensor, and obstructs your view of the display. Oh well, we'll just make something up. They'll never know anyway.
As you painstakingly jot down the data, you wonder why you couldn't just take it from the morning news weather report.
Looks like a fine day today on Kerbin's plains.
KerbinSrfLandedHighlands*: You make adjustments to the barometer to get an accurate reading at such a high altitude. Hopefully they appreciate your work back at KSC.
Barometer data indicates higher-than-normal humidity and precipitation. You guess that's just what happens when you're closer to the sky.
The barometer indicates an incoming storm! You feel sorry for anyone living in the valleys of the Highlands.
KerbinSrfLandedShores*: The barometric pressure here is high, but you are more interested in sneaking a glance at the bathing suits on the beach.
You send the barometer data back to KSC and reminisce about the good times you have had on these same beaches.
The barometer's dial moves left and right as you watch the surf batter the shores.
KerbinSrfLandedMountains*: The barometer reads very low here. You aren't surprised, as the mountaintop nearly pierces the clouds.
You stand on your tiptoes and try to insert the barometer into a passing cloud. So close...
The air pressure is dropping precipitously. Much like that goat that just fell off the mountain.
KerbinSrfLandedDeserts*: Barometer reads very high. No rain in sight. In fact you wonder if it has EVER rained here.
It's not going to rain for a long time.
Readings here are the same as usual: high. Nothing else to report.
KerbinSrfLandedBadlands*: The sensor looks like it went to the bottom of the Mariana Trench.
You transmit the readings. Mission Control immediately calls you back and demands to know if you are still on Kerbin.
You think that the barometer is broken but you're too scared to go outside and fix it.
KerbinSrfLandedTundra*: You don't think that this would be a nice place to live. The barometer concurs.
The barometer seems extremely high here. You can almost feel the pressure squeezing the ship.
The barometer suggests the area has a prevalence towards blizzards. Might not be a good idea to stay here much longer.
KerbinSrfLandedIceCaps*: The barometer appears to be frozen, or at least extremely steady.
Atmospheric pressure is fairly high in the polar regions. Perhaps the scientists back at KSC can explain why.
KerbinSrfLandedNorthernIceShelf*: The air pressure data spells out "COLD". You concur with the computer.
Pressure is higher here on average, as per usual for polar regions.
KerbinSrfLandedSouthernIceShelf*: Atmospheric pressure is the same as at sea level, even though you're no...wait a minute...
The labels on the barometer are covered with ice, and somebody forgot to install an equipment heater.
KerbinSrfSplashedWater*: The barometer reading isn't very interesting so you look out the window and watch the waves instead.
Surprisingly all the water didn't short out the instrument, and you are able to get a good reading to send back to KSC.
You wish there was a setting on the barometer that warns you of sharks.
KerbinSrfSplashedGrasslands*: A drifting stick bumps repeatedly aginst the sensor and causes the needle to move crazily.
KerbinSrfSplashedShores*: Data from the barometer seems to indicate a storm within the next few hours. You wonder if you'll get to see a shipwreck. You hope it's not yours.
Drops of saltwater seem to have encrusted the barometer and you are unable to get an accurate reading. You make something up randomly, and send it back to KSC.
As you write down the air pressure data you are briefly reminded of your elementary science lessons about "The Water Cycle".
KerbinSrfSplashedDeserts*: Looks like there won't be rain for a long time. You wonder how this water got here.
KerbinSrfSplashedBadlands*: The water really doesn't help the havoc the Badlands plays on the instrumentation.
KerbinSrfSplashedTundra*: The cold water causes air to sink downwards, increasing the air pressure.
KerbinSrfSplashedIceCaps*: The dial is stuck. You're too busy freezing to care.
KerbinSrfSplashedNorthernIceShelf*: Waves of icy water washing over the sensor generate bursts of pressure.
KerbinSrfSplashedSouthernIceShelf*: Looks like a severe polar storm will arrive later.
KerbinFlyingLow*: The pressure is lower here than it was at ground level.
After several minutes of displaying nothing, the sensor briefly registers a value!
One Hundred Million Pascals. That can't be right.
You remember that barometers are often used to forecast the weather. You examine the readings and decide that it might not be a good idea to be here in the next few hours.
The dial keeps spinning round and round. After consulting with Mission Control, you scribble a zero at the end of each of the measurements. The dial immediately points to the largest one.
Instead of sending Mission Control your barometer data, you just send them a copy of this morning's weather report.
Barometric pressure: 300 millibars. Or is that 3000? Whatever....look at the view down there.
Your blood pressure rises as you see just how close you are flying to the terrain below. The barometric pressure, in contrast, remains steady.
Despite the fact that you are flying low over the surface, the reading is no lower than usual.
*singing* "Under pressure. Ba da dum, ba da da dum, Ba da da dum, ba da da dum. Under pressure".
The barometer shakes and shimmies so much in the atmosphere that you are unable to get an accurate reading.
Readings seem nominal. Snack time!
The barometer seems to be questioning its own existence. Well, at least you agree on something.
You wonder what a desert storm would look like.
Air pressure is slightly higher here due to the heat.
Pressure readings are more or less in line with the rest of Kerbin. Much unlike the Highlands themselves.
KerbinFlyingHigh*: There is still atmospheric pressure even this far from the surface.
Did the device malfunction, or is this not a 'true' vacuum?
Glancing at the display, you shake your head and feel sorry for anyone who lives down there.
"The weather will remain pleasant," declares the barometer.
Is it snack time yet? It has to be snack time. No pressure readings till after snack time.
The air is too thin for the barometer to function. Much like how the air is too cold for a kerbal to function.
The barometer is covered in ice crystals at this altitude, and you are unable to get a good reading. Oh well, time for some snacks anyway.
Despite the fact that you are flying high over the surface, the reading is no higher than usual.
In the thin, wispy layers of the upper atmosphere a barometer reading is not particularly useful. You take the reading anyway and send it back to KSC.
The barometer data you transmit back to KSC barely qualifies as a proper reading.
Pressure is nominal. Nothing to see here folks. Move along.
The barometer here seems nominal. What's not nominal is the amount of snacks we haven't eaten yet. You radio KSC about this issue.
The display gives very little indication that you're flying over a desert.
High over the terrain you take a barometric reading. Doesn't matter, as the instrument seems to read the same thing everywhere. Why did we bring this stupid instrument anyway?
High above the shore you scribble yet another note on the barometric pressure, thinking about all the Kerbals sunning themselves below.
KerbinInSpaceLow*: You don't bother to look at the display. The fact that you're in space tells you all you need to know.
It reads -3. Huh, that's funny. Oh well, they can figure it out back at KSC.
At this altitude, a barometer scan is almost purely a formality.
You have discovered that there is no air in space.
KerbinInSpaceHigh*: Whatever vestiges of the atmosphere that might have lingered in low orbit are long gone.
Why are you wasting your time doing barometry in a vacuum when you could be doing something fun instead, like a spacewalk?
Aw hell, this is useless.
The needle is above zero. Tapping it, it climbs higher. You make a note to request the barometer be calibrated properly next time.
KerbinSrfLandedKSC*: The Barometer pressure is dropping. According to the weather station that means higher chance of rain later.
There's a storm coming, and its not your typical Kerbstorm.
KerbinSrfLandedAdministration*: The pressure drops as Montimer grows older.
KerbinSrfLandedAstronautComplex*: Since you are just checking the 'lowly barometer' the new recruits don't recognize you and walk right past.
KerbinSrfLandedBaikerbanur*: When Baikerbanur was decommissioned, a weather station was set up here. You simply pull the logs from that instead.
KerbinSrfLandedBaikerbanurLaunchpad*: Mission Control thinks you're still at KSC's launchpad and chews you out.
KerbinSrfLandedCrawlerway*: The reading is pretty low right now since the crawler is not actively driving over the instrument. NOT FOR LONG THOUGH!
KerbinSrfLandedFlagPole*: You realize that the pressure drops the higher you climb the pole.
KerbinSrfLandedLaunchPad*: Mission Control checks the readings against the local weather report to calibrate the Barometer.
No signs of adverse weather incoming, we can launch at any time.
KerbinSrfLandedMissionControl*: Mission control is glad to hear that the reading indicates favorable conditions for a launch. And for lunch too!
KerbinSrfLandedRnD*: As you attempt to record your observation, one of the research scientists comes over to explain that a millibar is not where those little umbrellas that come in drinks come from.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDCentralBuilding*: The folks at R&D disassemble your barometer before you have a chance to take a reading. What did you expect?
KerbinSrfLandedRnDCornerLab*: You hope to get a reading before your instrument is disassembled for parts by the staff here, by hiding in this corner.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDMainBuilding*: You point at an imaginary flying purple elephant to distract the staff here from disassembling your instrument just long enough to get a reading.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDObservatory*: How fitting to take your barometric pressure observation here. Must have been fate.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDSideLab*: OK, how many labs are there here?? You're tired of barometric pressures, and just want some snacks.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDSmallLab*: The smallness of the lab constricts the atmosphere, causing a higher reading.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDTanks*: You get distracted listening to your voice echo in the tanks and forget to take a barometric reading.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDWindTunnel*: The barometer blows away before you are able to record the results. Oh well, it was worth a try.
KerbinSrfLandedRunway*: No storm fronts detected. Perfect day for a flight!
KerbinSrfLandedSPH*: Another beautiful day at the SPH, and the barometer confirms it.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHMainBuilding*: You eye the menacing clouds on the horizon as the barometer plummets. Maybe we should hold off on that launch after all.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHRoundTank*: The barometer detects a small leak. You consider informing Gus Kerman but refrain from it, realizing that he probably doesn't care.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHTanks*: The barometer detects a small leak. You consider informing Gus Kerman but refrain from it, realizing that he probably doesn't care.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHWaterTower*: The barometer defers to the gravity sensor on this one.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStation*: The staff at the tracking station are unimpressed with your lowly barometer since it can't predict space-storms.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishEast*: You place the barometer right in the center of the dish just to aggravate those annoying Kerbals at the tracking station.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishNorth*: You take a reading at the focal point of the dish, hoping that somehow it will focus the atmospheric pressure.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishSouth*: The mercury from the barometer leaks all over the dish as you take your reading. Oh well, you never liked the guys at the tracking station anyway.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationHub*: Well, here you are, at the center of all those annoying, weaselly little....oh yeah barometer: seems normal.
KerbinSrfLandedVAB*: You are surprised to find a normal reading here. Probably about the only thing that IS normal here.
KerbinSrfLandedVABMainBuilding*: I don't know about the atmosphere, but the people here are definitely under a lot of pressure.
KerbinSrfLandedVABPodMemorial*: Looking at the pod you consider how much more -and less- pressure it endured than your barometer could ever hope to.
KerbinSrfLandedVABRoundTank*: The barometer detects a small leak. You consider informing Gus Kerman but refrain from it, realizing that he probably doesn't care.
KerbinSrfLandedVABSouthComplex*: The engineers inside are under pressure to get things done on time.
KerbinSrfLandedVABTanks*: The barometer detects a small leak. You consider informing Gus Kerman but refrain from it, realizing that he probably doesn't care.
KerbinSrfLandedIslandAirfield*: The runway on the island appears to be at a slightly higher altitude than The KSC Runway.
KerbinSrfLandedWoomerang*: In the foothills of the mountains, air pressure is a bit lower at this pad.

DunaSrfLanded: This data will help to determine the pressure gradient of Duna's atmosphere.
There isn't much atmosphere here. You consider lighting candles to create more.
DunaSrfLandedPoles: The atmosphere appears slightly denser here.
The atmosphere is slightly denser due to the gases escaping the ice beneath.
A blast of pressure from a nearby gas geyser is detected.
DunaSrfLandedMidlands: There's air here, but not much of it.
The pressure here is average and seems to consist of mostly dust.
DunaSrfLandedLowlands: The pressure in this basin is a whole notch higher on the scale.
The pressure here is the highest you've recorded, but it's still as low as in the upper atmosphere back home.
The pressure is a little higher here on the valley floor.
DunaSrfLandedCraters: The lower altitude of the craters results in slightly higher than average pressures.
There's just enough air here to fill a parachute, but don't even think about trying to breathe it.
DunaSrfLandedHighlands: The air here is so thin, you feel like you could reach up and touch space.
Duna's air is thin normally, and it's even thinner here on the mountaintops.
DunaSrfLandedMidlandSea: We just broke the Duna pressure record for this spot! Well, the record was "N/A" before now.
The unusually high pressure drops ever so slightly as a dust vortex passes over the craft.
DunaSrfLandedNorthernBasin: The atmosphere is a bit denser here from the low altitude.
Diurnal pressure variations are smaller than expected, possibly thanks to the canyon outlet to the south.
DunaSrfLandedSouthernBasin: The atmospheric pressure is largely average save for occasional gusts sweeping in off the ice.
Atmospheric pressure here shows larger diurnal fluctuations than elsewhere due to its proximity to the ice cap.
DunaSrfLandedNorthernShelf: Low altitude makes the air a bit more dense here. Just a bit.
Winds howling through the canyon from the north generate regular pressure spikes.
DunaSrfLandedMidlandCanyon: The altitude here is very low, so the atmosphere is pretty thick (for Duna).
Pressure here shows less diurnal variation than other places on Duna.
DunaSrfLandedEasternCanyon: There's more air here, but even more dust. That's not good news for the barometer you've already cleaned out twice today.
Air pressure increases substantially at night here as the atmosphere cools and sinks.
DunaSrfLandedWesternCanyon: There's a little bit more gas down here than some other places.
Wanting to have something to report, you tap the barometer and the needle rises slightly. Result!
DunaSrfLandedPolarHighlands: Evaporating ice from the polar cap raises the pressure by a tiny fraction.
Ice subliming off the polar cap raises the pressure very slightly.
Atmospheric pressure seems to experience measurable diurnal fluctuations.
DunaSrfLandedPolarCraters: The air here is pretty dense and pressure is higher thanks to the cold temperatures.
Pressure increases greatly as the temperature drops at nights and cold, dense air sinks into the depression.
DunaFlyingLow: This measurement will provide more information about the composition and pressure gradient of Duna's atmosphere.
The atmosphere seems to be quite thin here, but it's still preferable to the hard vacuum of space.
Not much pressure, and even less when flying at altitude.
DunaFlyingHigh: Even the barometer isn't sure how to interpret the readings.
There's some gas up here, but not much. What little pressure there is remains far below.
Pressure reads: "Gasping for air". You add an addendum about more numbers and less wisecracking.
DunaInSpaceLow: You're pretty sure this is a vacuum, but given how thin the atmosphere is you can't really be sure.
You're the one under pressure here, not the barometer. It quite clearly shows.
DunaInSpaceHigh: The surface all the way down there must surely have more atmosphere than up here.
There's probably a few atoms out there, but you can't detect them with a simple barometer.

JoolFlyingLow*: You notice the barometer has a crack in it shaped like a doughnut. There's also a reading about the pressure, but you've stopped caring.

The barometer's hand switched from "You sure we're in an atmosphere and not underwater?" to "Look, I wasn't built to record pressures in kbar". It suddenly dawns on you that you might have aerobraked a bit too deep.
The sensor starts breaking under pressure.
The immense pressure causes the sensor to implode.
Pressure increases quite rapidly, then stabilizes on "-65535". It occurs to you that the barometer may be broken.
The barometer reads lower than expected. Perhaps you are in the eye of a storm?
The barometer lets out some steam and fails to give a proper reading of the immensely thick atmosphere.
The scanner only has time to say "I hate you!" before the voice module implodes.
The gas chamber just cracked. Any deeper and this whole craft might implode.
Jool seems to be under a lot of pressure right now.
The barometer is calling you an idiot.
JoolFlyingHigh*: Cracks start to appear as you enter deeper into the atmosphere.
The pressures start rising in comparison to the surrounding area. You take this as a good sign - Joolian storms are nothing to sneeze at.
A massive blast of wind shakes the spacecraft. Of course, you didn't exactly need a barometer to tell you this.
The atmosphere here is very dense. The glass components appear to be weakening. Let's blame the engineers if anything breaks!

LaytheSrfLanded: Atmospheric pressure is lower than on Kerbin, but you don't think seasoned mountaineers would have any trouble adapting to it.
LaytheSrfLandedPoles: The readings are almost the same as on a sunny beach back home. It makes you feel like going for a swim.
LaytheSrfLandedShores: Atmospheric pressure is a bit lower than on Kerbin. You are unsure if that makes it more fun or not.
LaytheSrfLandedDunes: For a couple of seconds you think the device is still displaying readings taken on Kerbin.
LaytheSrfLandedTheSagenSea: The atmosphere here is unusually dense.
LaytheSrfSplashed: Atmospheric pressure at sea level is quite high, but still less than on Kerbin. Well, it's a moon, give it a break!
LaytheSrfSplashedCrescentBay: You contemplate dunking the scanner to see if it can measure depth, but Mission Control says that will void the warranty.
LaytheSrfSplashedTheSagenSea: The pressure seems higher over the water.
LaytheFlyingLow: The numbers are high... intriguingly high. Throw a bit more oxygen in this mix and you could live here.
If this atmosphere had oxygen, you could take off your helmet and crack a window.
LaytheFlyingHigh: The air is thin and rarefied, but if you're getting readings this high up, there's a chance it gets dense enough for planes and parachutes closer to the surface.

crewReport.description: Crewmember observations recorded from inside the vessel.
crewReport.hints: Visual impressions from the porthole, comments on flight conditions or the state of the craft.

SunInSpaceLow*: You wonder if you could fry an egg here.
You decide that Mission Control decided to send you here because they wanted to see how good fried kerbonaut is.
You are almost more amazed that you got here than at the fact that you haven't melted yet.
You close your eyes. The sun is still clearly visible!
All your snacks are getting crispy and brown around the edges. Luckily, you prefer them well done.
As the craft gets closer to Kerbol, you start to feel the effects of the heat on the craft. You break out the extra water, and make a note.
You ask Mission Control why they put you so close to Kerbol. They respond: "Science isn't about WHY. It's about WHY NOT." You can only wonder what that's supposed to mean.
You put on your spacesuit and activate the air conditioning.
Your personal freezer isn't working particularly well anymore and all your ice cream has melted.
You are pretty sure being this close to the sun isn't safe.
You look out the window trying to see something. Log: "Breath makes window foggy. Momentarily dissipates. Causes dizziness if repeated in rapid succession."
"Is it just me, or is it getting really hot in here?"
Theories predict that, at this distance, neutrino radiation is so high that you might even detect a collision.
You feel nervous as the sun's radiation blasts the windows. It's like a hand reaching out towards your spacecraft.
Your very expensive sunglasses seem to have no use here.
You decide that Kerbolar wind won't be a good candidate for exhaustive atmospheric analysis.
Why me?
You look through the window. And then you realize that the only thing you can see is a giant ball of plasma.
Looking through your cockpit window, you now understand the expression "Light at the end of the tunnel".
How did I... get here?
"'Go to space,' they said. 'It'll be fun,' they said..."
"Ohhh, the Sun. I'm gonna meet the Sun. Oh no! What'll I say? 'Hi! Hi, Sun!' Oh, boy!"
You question the safety of this mission.
Mission Control questions your choice of orbit, but at least you will get a nice tan.
You record the lack of sunglasses on the spacecraft.
Good thing we brought sunglasses.
You might want to put on some sunglasses.
Since you're really close to the Sun, you decide to put on your sunglasses.
You're starting to think getting this close to the Sun was a very bad idea.
You are going to have some really weird tan lines when you get back.
It's so shiny, you wanna touch it!
The radiation causes a tingly sensation.
You wonder how long mission control wishes to fry you for.
A large curlicue of plasma comes off the sun. You wonder what it is.
You see a few large sunspots. They look like really big dents.
You realize that the sun is very hot, maybe you should come back at night.
You tell Mission Control that no, your stupid plan to "go to the sun at night" did not work.
All the sunglasses we brought don't work. We should demand a refund, if we survive.
SunInSpaceHigh*: You stare up at space. And to the left at space. And to the right. You slowly realize that pretty much everywhere you look at is space.
The crew seems to have forgotten their sunscreen.
The future's so bright, I gotta wear shades. Also the sun.
You check your snack stores again, hoping they will last through the mission.
You are feeling extremely lost and lonely.
You realize the sun won't be setting anytime soon and wish you had installed some window shades.
You are bored, as nothing of interest is happening.
You press your face against the window and gaze at the wonders of space... only to be cut off by Mission Control reminding you to write a report.
You close your eyes. That seems to be the only way to make the sun disappear.
You gaze into open space, and you just can't believe how vastly, hugely, mind-bogglingly big it is.
You realize that you forgot to pack some snacks. You wonder if it is too late to turn back.
You decide that now would be a good time to take another nap.
Are we there yet? Huh? HUH? ARE WE THERE YET?!?
You see a bright, shiny object in the sky... and wonder to yourself... did I leave the stove on?
You don't much feel like going outside.
"I wish I'd packed that sun tan lotion."
You perceive a small flash of white light at the edge of your vision.
Are we there yet?
It's actually really boring out here.
Did something move past the capsule's window, or was that just a radiation-induced hallucination?
Wow, what a beautiful galaxy! Wait... that's a smudge on the window. A beautiful smudge.
The crew contemplates the rocket's infinite cosmic potential... and its cramped living space.
Interplanetary space, with nothing to look at but the stars, makes you feel lonely.
You can't believe leaving your hot dog outside didn't heat it up.

MohoSrfLanded*: Brownish red or reddish brown? It's good to think about the big questions.
MohoSrfLandedNorthPole*: Grey rocks and grey dirt. What a *great* location!
MohoSrfLandedNorthernSinkholeRidge*: You gaze in amazement at the sinkhole and wonder how it formed. You decide it was probably just an oversight.
MohoSrfLandedNorthernSinkhole*: You ponder the mystery of where Moho's atmosphere went. You're glad it doesn't have one anymore, because that would make coming back really hard.
MohoSrfLandedWesternLowlands*: You decide that it's hard to be an explorer.
MohoSrfLandedCentralLowlands*: The crew considers which snacks would taste best if cooked.
MohoSrfLandedHighlands*: You haven't been this hot since that day in training when your suit caught fire. You worry about it happening again.
MohoSrfLandedMinorCraters*: Jeb wants to know if a rover would powerslide here. Badly.
MohoSrfLandedSouthWesternLowlands*: You wonder how much ore could be mined here. You tell mission control that the mining rights are yours.
MohoSrfLandedSouthEasternLowlands*: Sure is lonely here on Moho.
MohoSrfLandedCanyon*: It appears the heat shielding is melting. The Kerbals are sweating and hot.
MohoSrfLandedSouthPole*: This pole is much less interesting.
You look for a big hole in the ground. Is this the wrong pole?
MohoInSpaceLow*: You watch the fuel gauge anxiously.
After some arguments with mission control you come to the conclusion that Moho is a giant cookie.
MohoInSpaceHigh*: You discover that it is possible to fry an egg on the console by the viewport. You note it down as a possible economic reason to go into space.
You hear the structure of the spacecraft creaking and groaning due to thermal expansion. You hope the person who designed it knew what they were doing.
Moho is beautiful. You take a picture, then realize you're out of batteries.

EveSrfLandedPoles: Maybe smuggling some snacks into the command module wasn't a bad idea, after all.
The Poles seem every bit as inhospitable as the rest of this pressure-cooker of a planet.
EveSrfLandedMidlands: You have a feeling that landing here may have been a bad idea.
EveSrfLandedExplodiumSea: You still cherish the hope that you packed enough fuel.
EveSrfLandedLowlands: The ship computer starts playing the tune to the song Purple Rain all on its own.
After hearing the creaks of your capsule, you regret visiting Eve.
EveSrfLandedHighlands: We have successfully landed on Eve! However, the crew is expressing doubts about the lander's ability to leave.
The purple souposphere swirls menacingly around the craft out of the windows.
EveSrfLandedPeaks: The gravity is getting us down. Hopefully that doesn't include down the mountain.
There's nowhere flat around here, and the high gravity means it's a struggle to walk around the cabin and makes you constantly fall out of your chair.
EveSrfLandedImpactEjecta: Your crew asks Mission Control if snacks can be turned into fuel.
You feel that your ship is under a lot of pressure.
EveSrfLandedCraterLake: The purple sea sparkles nearby, looking innocent enough to those not in it.
EveSrfLandedWesternSea: You don't fancy going outside, so you have a snack instead.
EveSrfLandedOlympus: There's a lot of sky out of the window. Even the terrain is intimidating here on Eve.
That altimeter is still reading awful high. Are you sure we've actually landed?
EveSrfLandedEasternSea: Purple sea, purple sky, purple ground. You rub your eyes and search in vain for something not purple.
EveSrfLandedFoothills: The more you look at them, the more the rolling purple hills start to blend together as a blur.
Even Eve has baby mountains. Naturally, they're bigger than Kerbin's.
EveSrfLandedShallows: Flat coastal plains provide an ideal landing spot for craft, but not one for taking off from.
EveSrfSplashedPoles: The ocean is feeling a bit too cold...
EveSrfSplashedExplodiumSea: Explodium, you say? Can we turn this stuff into rocket fuel?.....it's just metaphorical? Aww.
Mission Control assures you that it's grape juice outside and you can drink it without harm. You, however, become skeptical when it begins peeling the paint off the ship.
EveSrfSplashedImpactEjecta: You catch a whiff of something chemical-y. Are you sure that hatch is closed properly?
EveSrfSplashedCraterLake: You see the purple liquid that is Eve's ocean leak into the capsule. You get the suspicion that you may be in big trouble.
This reminds you of a time you saw a man get trapped in a vat of grape soda. Unfortunately, you can't drink your way out of this one.
EveSrfSplashedWesternSea: Even though your suit should be airtight, you swear that the capsule reeks faintly of nail polish remover.
A small wave slaps against the craft, causing you to lose your balance in the high gravity and fall over again.
EveSrfSplashedEasternSea: As you see the purple ocean surrounding you, you start to wonder how you'll ever get back home.
This stuff probably has so many uses, and you could make a killing off of it if you could only figure out how to bring it back with you.
EveSrfSplashedShallows: We're bouncing a bit too much here.
You're pretty sure this (isn't) grape juice.
EveFlyingLowPoles: The wind and crew are screaming.
You start having second thoughts about landing...
EveFlyingLowMidlands: They said that the crew would be getting a raise when they returned, but you've never heard of anyone getting a raise. Ever. We're not even paid!
EveFlyingLowExplodiumSea: After looking through the window, you inform Mission Control you figured out where all the grape soda from the break room fridge keeps going.
EveFlyingLowLowlands: You get a sudden urge for grape juice.
Flying through the soupy air is a slow process.
EveFlyingLowHighlands: The crew wonders if there are enough snacks.
Small air currents are amplified in power by the high atmospheric density, and the craft rocks as it flies.
EveFlyingLowPeaks: Mountains rise all around you to stab into the heavy sky.
The communications system crackles with unknown interference.
EveFlyingLowImpactEjecta: The equipment is strained to the limits. We should be fine, as long as we don't make any sudden movements.
A blob of molten exterior paint slowly crawls up the window glass.
EveFlyingLowCraterLake: The Crater Lake extends for miles towards the horizon. Why isn't it called the Crater Sea?
There's no alien sea monsters down there, right?
EveFlyingLowWesternSea: The vile lake shimmers beneath the crushing atmosphere.
You try not to imagine what getting stuck in that liquid would be like.
EveFlyingLowOlympus: Please don't try and land here. We don't want to end up as debris on a mountainside.
The disconnect between the sea-level and radar altimeters is scarily large.
EveFlyingLowEasternSea: The presence of a sluggish gust of wind moving across the sea is betrayed by a patch of ripples.
The capsule window glass feels uncomfortably warm to the touch.
EveFlyingLowFoothills: You can't see the edge of the hills, even from here.
The manufacturer offered us our money back if the capsule gets crushed, so there's that at least.
EveFlyingLowShallows: You could swear you see shapes lurking under the purple surface.
As you look out at the sea, its purple color reminds you of your favorite soft drink.
EveFlyingHigh: After various tests you can confirm that it is indeed "very purple".
A tendril of glowing plasma streams off of a protruding rivet.
Atmospheric entry is dangerous here. Praying to your relevant deity is recommended to alleviate stress.
High above the toxic clouds and crushing pressure, things don't seem quite so bad.
EveInSpaceLow: Well, purple is your favorite color. Maybe there are purple flowers you can take home?
That is definitely a deep commitment to purple.
The world looks incredibly toxic and inhospitable.
EveInSpaceHigh: You think you see Gilly, but it turns out to be a spare peanut.
Eve's sky glows a beautiful green on the terminator, between day and night.
The atmosphere shimmers like rocket fuel.
Eve looks beautiful from up here. You suspect this to be a ruse to lull you into a false sense of security.

GillySrfLandedMidlands: This landing spot is definitely not flat, but the gravity is so low it barely matters.
A shallow slope can be vertical and vice versa. Gilly has some very confusing geometries from down here.
GillySrfLandedLowlands: You contemplate running around the circular walls of the crew capsule like a caffeinated hamster, to see if it would generate enough force to tip over the lander.
You feel no change in gravity.
GillySrfLandedHighlands: You consider going for a hike, but you are afraid that your first step will launch you right off the surface.
You feel a surge in gravity. Mentioning nothing to Mission Control, you put on your brown pants.
GillyInSpaceLow: "I didn't know they made them this small."
You wonder if anything would have a TWR of less than one here.
What is this, a moon for ants?
This place looks a bit boring. You start calculating what it would take to deorbit it into Eve, until your calculator gives you a NaN error. Mission Control refuses to do calculations for you.
It looks small and not very tasty.
You look down to the surface. It's so close! Of course you don't want to end up lithobraking on those hills by accident, but at this speed it probably wouldn't be that bad.
You think you can stick your arm out and touch the surface.
GillyInSpaceHigh: Somehow your foot has become stuck in the controls. Who has been chewing gum?
In retrospect, you imagined it to be bigger.
It looks like a potato. You are starting to feel very hungry.
A rock so little must have very little gravity. Next time, you'll bring a golf club.
How cute! Despite its potato-like appearance, Gilly is an adorable little asteroid.
The color and highly irregular shape of Gilly remind you of a potato. You suddenly wonder where the chips are.

KerbinSrfLandedWater*: Does our craft float? Maybe we'll get to test it.
KerbinSrfLandedGrasslands*: You look forward to opening the capsule door and smelling the fresh earthy smell of grass. Oh yeah, we have to wear these stupid spacesuits even on Kerbin. Who's great idea was that?
The crew is embroiled in an argument over whether the grass is in fact greener on the other side.
KerbinSrfLandedHighlands*: KSC, we have landed. I think. The altimeter still shows way too high for us to be on the ground, but our velocity is zero. So there you have it. Maybe.
Landing in the Highlands woohoo! Break out the celebratory kilts and bagpipes!
KerbinSrfLandedShores*: You want to throw a beach party but Mission Control orders you to focus on the mission.
The shores look inviting as you watch the waves roll in to the coast.
The shores appear to have a blue liquid nearby.
KerbinSrfLandedMountains*: You feel like the king of the world standing on this mountain. Or at least the king of the mountain.
As your craft settles precipitously near a sharp drop, you reflect on the need to attach cleats to all future landing gear.
KerbinSrfLandedDeserts*: You imagine how hot it would be if the capsule did not have the a/c on.
The crew complains about the heat.
KerbinSrfLandedBadlands*: You start to feel very nervous about all the bad things you said about the Badlands.
You became a kerbonaut specifically to get away from the Badlands. And now here you are. Congratulations.
You try to have a good time. You fail.
KerbinSrfLandedTundra*: As you shiver even inside a heated capsule, you find it hard to believe that your ancestors once lived in such conditions.
You peer out the spacecraft window at the stunted plant growth, and wonder if this is where the first Kerbal came from.
Maybe you'll see a bear! Or a wolf! Or an Eskimo!
KerbinSrfLandedIceCaps*: You wish you’d brought your ice skates.
You think that you'd appreciate the stark beauty of the pristine poles more if it wasn't so cold.
KerbinSrfLandedICeCaps*: Ooh, that aurora is pretty! Wonder what it looks like from space?
KerbinSrfLandedNorthernIceShelf*: You've heard the legends of Santa Klaus living at the North Pole. Quite why he would live in such a freezing, desolate place is beyond you.
We're here in this terrible place, and there's not even an igloo to be seen.
KerbinSrfLandedSouthernIceShelf*: The freezing, windswept and featureless ice plains stretch over the horizon.
The crew is disappointed at the distinct lack of tuxedo-wearing birds.
KerbinSrfSplashedWater*: Arr mateys! Let's show those landlubbers at KSC who the masters of the high seas truly are!
You don't need to worry about pirates, right? Surely they're only after regular ships not spaceships.
I hope the hatch doesn't break and flood the pod.
“I can't swim!”
Hey little guy! I shall call him Squishy and he shall be mine, and he shall be my Squishy. Come here Squishy...
KerbinSrfSplashedGrasslands*: You're bobbing around in a muddy pond in a field. Surely there's more glamorous places for a kerbonaut to be.
KerbinSrfSplashedShores*: Who needs a swimsuit? Chicks dig spacesuits.
Your ship is wet. You suspect that if you exited the ship you would be too.
The crew highly doubts the floating capabilities of this craft.
You see a fish swimming by the window. It looks like you are under water.
KerbinSrfSplashedDeserts*: Waves lapping against the window are one of the last things you would expect to find in a desert, behind “penguins” and “a Yeti” but ahead of “Mortimer's underwear”.
KerbinSrfSplashedBadlands*: The water must be hot, since the capsule interior is getting rather warm.
KerbinSrfSplashedTundra*: That water sure looks chilly. You're glad you're in here and not in it.
KerbinSrfSplashedIceCaps*: A small piece of ice clacks against the window. You can feel the cold through the thickened glass.
KerbinSrfSplashedNorthernIceShelf*: You watch a large iceberg accompanied by a swarm of growlers make its stately way past.
KerbinSrfSplashedSouthernIceShelf*: You feel naming your ship the Unsinkable may have been a bad idea in this environment.
KerbinFlyingLowWater*: You see a spray of water whipped up by the air rushing out of your way.
You are flying so low you notice disturbances in the water from your craft.
From this perspective you can see the underwater topology.
You wonder if it's safe to drink the water.
The water looks so peaceful from here.
You imagine all the wonderful creatures that live below the surface that would probably eat you. Or at least brush up against your leg while swimming.
KerbinFlyingLowGrasslands*: There's something on the wing, some.....thing....
It's very comforting to see this much green below you.
The ground here has been highly manicured. You note the number of groundskeepers the planet must have.
KerbinFlyingLowHighlands*: You get a great view of the highlands.
There can be only one report from the Highlands!
It’s pretty boring out here. This must be why there aren’t many cities on Kerbin.
KerbinFlyingLowShores*: The crew is disappointed by the absence of bikinis in the area.
You look out the window and see a piece of your rocket fly off. It wasn't important anyways.
“Crew reporting in. We'll get back to you… about to eat lunch.”
KerbinFlyingLowMountains*: You calculate the chance of impact at this altitude. It does not make you feel safe.
You keep a sharp lookout for sudden mountains.
As you hurtle past the mountainside at a reckless speed, you almost hit a very surprised mountain goat.
KerbinFlyingLowDeserts*: You look out the window hoping to find a tasty cake until you realize somebody made a slight spelling error in the flight plan.
The heat rising from the sand causes the desert to move and shimmer.
You look around and see no water for miles. You begin to wonder why you didn't bring more.
You wonder if crashing here would create a glass crater. Then you realize how cool that would be.
In anticipation of the heat, you turn on the air conditioning.
KerbinFlyingLowBadlands*: You start to share scary stories about the strange things found in the Badlands, but Mission Control shushes you.
This looks like a bad place to land.
You bet this is where all the cool kids come to land.
You have a bad feeling about this.
Crashing here would not be good.
KerbinFlyingLowTundra*: You look at the ground below you and see the frosted hills. It reminds you of the cake you had for breakfast.
You look down and see a glimmer. There's gold in these hills!
You see patches of frozen grass. It looks like it's pretty cold out there.
Please don't land here, please don't land here, please don't land here...
KerbinFlyingLowIceCaps*: The snowy ice caps look so fluffy and soft to land on.
You look down and are blinded by the white snow.
KerbinFlyingLowNorthernIceShelf*: Even flying above the cold, white plains, you're not free of its icy grip.
You think you see some land on the horizon from your aerial vantage point, but the glare makes it difficult to see.
KerbinFlyingLowSouthernIceShelf*: You look down and see a sheet of shining white ice. If you stare at it too long, your eyes start to water.
The ice sheet is strangely uniform from the air, with a total lack of major crevasses and joints.
KerbinFlyingHigh*: From this high up, the Badlands doesn't look so bad. You still don't want to crash there though.
Whatever lives in the Badlands can't get you up here. You hope.
You are almost in an orbit with your home planet.
You wonder what is down there on the surface. If only you had some binoculars.
A golf ball pings against the side of the spaceship. How the heck did someone hit a ball that hard??
You can't smell the cows anymore.
*singing* “Colorado Rocky Mountain high. I've seen it rainin' fire in the sky”. Ah, good 'ol John Denver.
You feel a certain satisfaction at being higher than the Highlands.
Is that the desert down there? It looks awful dry and sandy.
Wow that desert is big. You squint your eyes and try to count the palm trees.
Over the ice and through the atmosphere, to KSC we go! You wonder if grandmothers house is on the way.
You discover that you are the only one of the entire crew who still believes that your presents come from Santa.
The mountains rise up from the grasslands of Kerbin. They seem so much smaller from up here.
Even from up here, Kerbin's mountains remain an imposing sight.
You mock the seagulls with their ugly faces, funny voices, and low flight ceiling.
You squint through the window, hoping to catch a glance of the Kerbals sunning themselves on the beach below.
Nothing down there looks like it's enjoying itself.
The stunted trees below make it seem like you are further away than you already are. Are we in space yet? And when do we break out the snacks?
What's that big shadow? Is that...a kraken?
KerbinInSpaceLow*: Did you remember to pack your Agoraphobia medication?
You float through the air, eating the levitating chips.
Alarmingly, one side of the ship feels really hot. The other side doesn't, though, so it should be fine.
You stare out the window in deep thought, pondering the answer to the ultimate question, before being rudely interrupted by the Mission Control.
You marvel at the weightless nuts and bolts as they dance around the cabin. You briefly wonder what parts of your craft they had once held in place.
You're in space and not dead. The crew and Mission Control have agreed that it's indeed a big step forward.
Much to your chagrin, Scott Kerman gets on the radio and begins educating the crew about proper bathroom procedures. Again.
You attempt to perform a juggling act, only to have the balls fly off in various directions due to micro-gravity. You hope they didn't hit any important buttons this time.
LOOK! Another star! And hey... there's another! Wow, space is awesome.
After rigorous testing, the crew has determined that micro-gravity increases the fun of bouncy balls by 200%.
“This is why we need fancy suits, huh?”
Even from space the vast swaths of grass appear a technicolor green. You feel proud to have come from such a beautiful place.
You marvel at how beautifully-manicured kerbalkind's lawn is.
They didn't fly into space, storm a beach or bring back gold! No, sir, we did! It's you and me against the world, son!
This is Major Kerman to Mission Control, I'm feeling very still. And I think my spaceship knows which way to go.
It's time to leave the capsule... right?
Are we there yet?
We should have fixed the GPS before taking off so we'd know what biome we're in. Oh well, rocket fuel is expensive.
From this altitude, you realize that you'll pass over the Badlands several times per hour. This worries you more than it should.
Hey, it looks like my globe!
“Hey, I can see Jeb's Ego from here!”
“TO INFINITY, AND BEYOND!”
Yep, we're in space alright, but you bet that if we left the landing gear down it would hit that mountaintop.
You feel dizzy, unable to keep your head from spinning. If you are going to build a larger crew pod, a web of ropes inside might be of help.
Planet Kerbin is blue and there is nothing I can do.
You reflect on the meaningful events it took you to get here, warmly recalling the moment you first decided to become an astronaut. It was just this morning.

You are chatting to Mission Control when your radio channel is interrupted by some guy named Major Tom frantically saying something has gone wrong. You tell him take a protein pill and put his helmet on.
You kill some time chatting with a random radio operator.
The command console reads: “Welcome to space. Please enjoy your stay.”
The crew is reading books to pass the time.
Just a little bit further to go!
Bail out!
Don't bail out!
Do we have enough fuel to make it into real space?
If you rotate the craft, we might end up aerobraking...
As you gaze down on your home planet, you are simultaneously filled with wonder and amazed that you made it this far without exploding.
Here you are sitting in a tin can, far above the world.
“Wheeeeeeeeeeeeeee!”
“Space? SPACE! SPAAACCCCCE!”
You never get tired of the view from space, even flying over this part of the planet.
You think that KSC may be somewhere below the clouds.
If you were in charge of creating Kerbin you wouldn't have wasted so much space on an empty desert. They could at least give it a swimming pool or something.
You gaze in wonder at the auroras and wonder if they are related to rainbows.
You contemplate the vast swaths of ice below, wondering if any Kerbals have ever been there.
You peer down at the mountains below, wondering if there is anyone down there looking up at you.
You tell Mission Control that some of those mountains seem perfectly suited for an observatory.
The beach looks inviting even from space. Too bad we forgot to pack cocktails.
You prepare your bathing suit in case Mission Control wants you to land there.
From this height, you can clearly see where hard ice meets harsh rock meets cold snow. Not a very nice place to live.
From this distance the already tiny plants growing on the tundra below are so small you can barely see them.
KerbinInSpaceHigh*: You almost have a panic attack as your instrumentation warns that you are directly over the Badlands. Then you laugh as you remember that you're over a quarter million kilometers away.
You have mixed feelings about being this far away from Kerbin. On the one hand you're pretty far from home, but on the other hand you're pretty far from the Badlands.
Almost back to KSC. Just an atmosphere and a few thousand kilometers between us.
From up here, the desert and the oceans look like one giant beach! Beaches are fun!
The spacecraft's crew and some of the guys at Mission Control are playing chess over the radio.
You look out the window only to realize you forgot your keys at home.
You can see the Badlands from orbit. Ew.
“Log: The alien planet seems remarkably similar to our own. Oh wait...”
You wonder at the fact that Kerbin is mostly green.
Agoraphobia sets in...
You really are high as a kite, rocket-man. Maybe higher.
Bound for the stars.
You think about all the great times you had hanging out on the shores below. Anyway, hasn't it been a while since we had some snacks??
From space, everything looks like a giant beach! Especially the actual beaches!
You observe the Highlands from orbit, marveling at how tiny they look.
You become homesick thinking about the mountains of Kerbin being the closest part of the planet to you at this altitude. Then you have some snacks, and it's all better.
You wonder if you will make it home safe.
You count the snowstorms passing over the Tundra below and are suddenly glad you don't have to be down there.
Is that Tundra below? You are so far from the planet that you can only make a wild guess. So you do, and report it as solid fact back to KSC.
You briefly contemplate your navel as you watch the planet pass below you.
Although the planet is indeed very round, you wonder if perhaps its roundness has been exaggerated.
Your slide rule breaks, and you begin to search for your backup before realizing that you don't know how to use a slide rule anyway.
You swear you saw a blue police box flashing away on a retrograde orbit.
Let's engage Warp Drive... wait, we don't have a Warp Drive?
