Everything looks so small from up here.
KSC, I hope you know where I am, cause I just spilled coffee on the roadmap.
Ooh that aurora is pretty! Wonder what it looks like from the ground?
You really want to get out and take a walk, but there's nothing to walk on.
You make an observation, but the lack of gravity stops your pen from working. Perhaps you should have brought a pencil.
The surface of Kerbin is the shore of the cosmic ocean. Recently you've waded a little way out, maybe ankle-deep, and the water seems inviting.
“Crew reporting in. Everything is nominal here.”
The planet appears to be round! It's not flat like we thought!
You strain to see detail on the surface below, but all that you can see are the broad strokes of biomes below.
You hope that when you come back, you'll land in the Grasslands.
You can barely make out the ridges of Kerbin's mountains from this altitude, but enjoy a few snacks with the view.
From this distance, Kerbin's mountain ranges blend into one big blob.
You look down over the vast ocean below and wonder how many glasses it would take to empty all that water.
From here Kerbin looks like a smooth blue marble. You never were good at that game.
As you watch the great blue seas of Kerbin pass by you wonder what lays hidden in the depths.
Wow, that ocean really is big. You're glad you aren't trying to sail across it.
As you watch Kerbin's oceans from the depths of outer space you ask KSC if there are space pirates. KSC assures you that pirate ships have sails instead of rockets and thus cannot achieve orbit.
You can't see any plant life below, and can't decide whether it's because you are too far away, or that nothing grows here.
Is it still the Highlands if you're over a quarter million meters above it?
KerbinSrfLandedKSC*: You look out the window and can no longer remember if you've already returned from a mission or are yet to depart.
You think you should get inside the VAB and discuss if the rocket is good enough.
As you sit in the capsule, you can't help but wonder why you haven't taken off yet.
This is our Space Center. We're home.
Was a rocket really necessary to get here?
Deja Vu!
You should probably climb the VAB!
Um, did we actually go anywhere? This looks awfully familiar.
KerbinSrfLandedAdministration*: You watch Montimer swing a golf ball into the pond.
KerbinSrfLandedAstronautComplex*: You are just happy that we didn't land on any of the new recruits.
KerbinSrfLandedBaikerbanur*: This old, neglected spaceport really is a sorry sight nowadays, a grave insult to its proud history.
KerbinSrfLandedBaikerbanurLaunchpad*: You can't help but feel reverence at sitting where the first pioneers of Kerbal spaceflight sat, before the KSC was even lines on paper.
KerbinSrfLandedCrawlerway*: You pretend to be the Crawler driver. After a few minutes you realize that you'd rather fly to space.
KerbinSrfLandedFlagPole*: You wonder if this will be the site of your grave marker.
KerbinSrfLandedLaunchPad*: You look up at the stars and begin to count. You realize you should take off before you fall asleep.
You regret wearing a red shirt to work today.
You're not sure this is the right place to be.
You are waiting impatiently.
"Are we going to launch? If not, I want cookies."
You pause for a moment to rethink your life.
"Are we there yet?"
You pick up your pencil, but get distracted by the thought of space.
This place looks very familiar.
"Ignition, check. Fuel gauge, check. Parachute, check. Microwave, check. Wait, microwave?"
You mutter a personal version of Shepard's Prayer.
You get impatient and try to find the launch button so you can go to space already.
The launchpad... very interesting terrain we have here.
You wonder why you experienced over 3 gs when the rocket was moved to the pad.

Just a heads' up: We're gonna have a superconductor turned up full blast and pointed at you for the duration of the flight. Honestly, we're throwing science at the wall here. Best case scenario, you gain some superpowers. Worst case, you get commemorated.
We don't seem to be moving very fast right now.
Are we there yet?
“Wait! Let me out, I changed my mind!"
Check ignition and may Von Kerman's love be with you.
You wonder what that nasty smell is. Either there is a serious propellant leak or Bob had burritos for lunch. Again.
You examine the blinking lights and stare intensely at the fuel gauges. Looks like we are ready to go.
Mission Control reminds you that getting out and pushing is often a viable tactic.

You furtively pull out the corned beef sandwich you smuggled on board and take a bite. Some crumbs drop into the flight controls, but no worries - they should float right back out once you're in space.
You wonder where Gunther Wendt.
Flipping trough the Prelaunch Checklist you open a page that simply reads "Your Commercials could be here". You finally realize that Montimer is overtaxing his authority - literary.
KerbinSrfLandedMissionControl*: Mission report: we didn't go very far. Can't you see me waving at you?
KerbinSrfLandedRnD*: The R&D folks start disassembling your ship as soon as it lands.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDCentralBuilding*: Quick, fire the engines back up before the R&D folks disassemble them!
KerbinSrfLandedRnDCornerLab*: The R&D techs are concentrating so hard that they didn't even see us land.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDMainBuilding*: You would send your report out, but the R&D techs already disassembled the comms antenna.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDObservatory*: You hope the R&D folks were watching that landing. Textbook perfect, if I do say so myself.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDSideLab*: The R&D folks are awestruck at the sight of your ship, having never seen one fully assembled before.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDSmallLab*: We landed ON TOP of the building (you think). Now that is a pinpoint landing!
KerbinSrfLandedRnDTanks*: You hope the rocket exhaust doesn't catch whatever strange substance is in those tanks on fire.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDWindTunnel*: You catch the complex on fire trying to counteract the wind with your rocket engines. Oh well, snack time!
KerbinSrfLandedRunway*: You think that you should be flying the craft instead of making boring logs.
You take a piece of paper from your notepad and draw a spaceplane on it.
Despite its name, the runway does not actually run.
The runway seems to run a long way.
There's a nice view of mission control from here. You can see them waving back.
You take a sip of a soft drink before igniting the engines. Or was that after?
Don't buzz the tower. The guy paying your salary is standing there and he doesn't want his coffee spilled.
“Clear Right. Clear Left. We're good here. Ground control come in. Over."
Reporting from the Runway. Good thing there's not a lot of traffic, because I don't think we ever got the clearance to be here.
This runway needs a ramp.
KerbinSrfLandedSPH*: You hope they can still roll the planes out, having landed this close to the SPH.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHMainBuilding*: Whew, that was a close one, we almost hit the SPH!
KerbinSrfLandedSPHRoundTank*: You notice that your ship looks strangely similar to the tanks over there.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHTanks*: You feel inadequate in your little ship surrounded by all these huge tanks.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHWaterTower*: You consider jumping out of the spacecraft and climbing the water tower.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStation*: You sure hope they can track you from here.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishEast*: You make loud noises into your comms headset, wondering if there will be a feedback loop this close to the tracking dish.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishNorth*: So, what was the point of this flight again?
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishSouth*: All the way to the south tracking dish. Now we're exploring the universe (one small step at a time).
KerbinSrfLandedVAB*: There seem to be a lot of strange green lifeforms building something here. Oh yeah, we're still at the VAB.
KerbinSrfLandedVABMainBuilding*: Wow, that was a short trip!
KerbinSrfLandedVABPodMemorial*: Take THAT memorial! This is a REAL ship!
KerbinSrfLandedVABRoundTank*: We really should have landed INSIDE the tank.
KerbinSrfLandedVABSouthComplex*: You're not really sure what goes on in this building. Then again, that applies to most KSC facilities.
KerbinSrfLandedVABTanks*: Nice piloting. We made it back to KSC in one(ish) piece.
KerbinSrfLandedIslandAirfield*: The crumbling control tower looks like it's ready to fall and squash you and your ship any second now.
KerbinSrfLandedWoomerang*: Welcome to Woomerang. Is this it? You expected more.
KerbinFlyingLowKSC*: Woohoo! We're doin' it! Space b****es!!
KerbinFlyingHighKSC*: You look out the window and think that you can just make out KSC below.

MunSrfLanded: You feel awestruck at the beaten up nature of the Mun.
MunSrfLandedMidlands: Ahh, the Midlands. The only flat place on this not-made-of-cheese rock.
MunSrfLandedNorthernBasin: Another place to gain the 'First snack ever eaten here in the history of Kerbalkind' achievement. Let's get to it!
MunSrfLandedEastCrater: One small step for man, one giant bill for mankind.
MunSrfLandedNorthwestCrater: This would be an awesome spot to film a movie. "North by Northwest Crater".
MunSrfLandedSouthwestCrater: This would be a great spot for the first Munar music festival. 'South by Southwest Crater'
MunSrfLandedFarsideCrater: Boy, the lighting up here sure looks weird. Almost as if you were on a sound stage or something.
MunSrfLandedCanyons: Seeing the dark canyon makes you wonder what wonders lie down there.
MunSrfLandedPolarCrater: It feels strangely dangerous out there. Mission Control insists it's safe, but you don't think they would know very well.
MunSrfLandedPolarLowlands: You put on your polarized aviator sunglasses. Now you can look cool AND be situationally appropriate.
MunSrfLandedHighlands: You survey the vista that spreads out before you as you munch on some snacks.
MunSrfLandedHighlandCraters: Struck by a sudden urge to say something witty, you nearly choke on your snack.
MunSrfLandedMidlandCraters: You muse on the chances of having landed in this specific crater. Oh yeah, we picked it from data from a pre-scan mission. There was a pre-scan mission, right?!
MunSrfLandedEastFarsideCrater: You begin to indicate concerns regarding whether or not the landing was simply an elaborate hoax, but the communications are interrupted by a strange voice telling you to keep quiet.
MunSrfLandedTwinCraters: The twin craters remind you of some other twin things. But we won't get into that here.
MunInSpaceLow: You resist the urge to take your craft down even lower and 'thread the needle'.
You glance out your window at the surface... Whoa! Who put mountains this high on a rock this small?!
Your ships suffers a minor depressurization, but quick thinking and lots of bubble gum manage to solve the problem before it gets out of hand.
Looking down at the Mun's surface, you suddenly feel tempted to eat cheese.
"Crew reporting in. We are currently near the Mun. It turns out it's much bigger than we thought it was."
Great, we've made it this far! Now, how to land?
Wasn't there an opera written about this once?
You don't know if the crater rims would be a good landing site. Probably not, but they would certainly be... interesting.
Ah the "East Crater". Yes, now that you're up close you can definitely tell which one it is. You totally didn't look at the map.
ANOTHER crater?! Does this place have ANY flat land??
You wonder if your report can even make it back to Kerbin from here - you are on the Farside of the Mun, after all.
You report to Mission Control that you have found no evidence of the long-standing claim that a cow jumped over the Mun.
You try to decide which area would make the best landing spot. Then you make a back-up list of places that would make a good crash-landing spot.
Those highland craters think they're so much better than the other craters. Well they don't look so great from up here!
Crew report begins. I'm hungry. Will eat snacks. Crew report ends.
Without atmosphere, weather, or major seismic activity, the Mun's craters are remarkably well-preserved. You make sure to take plenty of pictures and note this in your report.
You remember watching a documentary on one of the leading theories explaining the formation of the Mun. It's hard to believe that the sterile surface below was once a literal sea of fire.
In addition to the rather round craters, there also appear to be areas that are far more irregular in shape. The scientists are calling these 'basins'.
You report that the ship makes scary noises at night and you can't sleep. Mission Control advises leaving on the night light.
You thank Mission Control for helping you get this far.
The Mun's Polar Lowlands don't seem that different from the rest of the Mun.
The crew is incredibly impressed with the engineering of this spacecraft. They especially admire the cleverly-recessed, climate-controlled snack bar.
Nothing to report Mission Control! Except for the fact that we're flying over the Mun but you already know that.
Those Twin Craters don't look very similar. Perhaps they are fraternal twins.
MunInSpaceHigh: From this high up, the canyons look like scars across the Mun's face. Which is appropriate considering how much the Mun has survived millennia after millennia.
You are very proud of yourself for reaching this far!
You see a great sphere of moldy cheese... Oh, no wait, its just the Mun.
What kind of geologic formation is THAT?
Hey, that looks sort of like the arch from McKerbal's.
You look back at Kerbin. I looks rather small.
It seems that the Mun is one big giant rock with no atmosphere.
Those craters... what could they be caused by?
"Here I am floating in a tin can, far above the Mun."
You wonder if those craters were caused by the impacts of launches before you. Mission Control laughs nervously and urges you otherwise.
You sketch a map of the munar surface with the intention of taking it home to frame and hang on your wall. And, because you have time, you make a few autographed copies to sell on kBay.
You gaze down at the crater below as crumbs from the snack you are eating fall out of your mouth.
Wow the Mun looks pretty beat up. Look at all those holes.
The crater below reminds you of snacks. Of course everything reminds you of snacks.
You request a bedtime story from Mission Control.
Huh. The Mun kinda looks like a giant golf ball. You nervously keep an eye out for a giant golf club.
Staring at the crater-filled surface of the Mun you are suddenly thankful for Kerbin's thick atmosphere.
Ha the Mun is so small and adorab--holy crap, it's getting big really fast.
All systems nominal. The ship is happy so you're happy.
Watching the pockmarked munar surface get larger and larger as you get closer and closer is a sight you'll treasure forever.
You try to imagine the sheer kinetic energy released when whatever made those giant craters impacted. Must've been quite a sight.
You and Mission Control are playing Battleship over the radio. Curses! There goes your kruiser.
You take out your sketchbook and make a drawing of the munar surface. Then you give it a smiley face so it looks happy to see you!
As you approach the Mun, you take the time to rehearse several contingency plans in case of an emergency.
You take the time to study the schematics of your spacecraft. You are greatly impressed and slightly worried at how many corners were cut during its design and construction.
The Twin Craters remind you of a cell dividing.

MinmusSrfLanded*: You try jumping into the air and strafing at the same time. Now, if only you had a water pistol...
MinmusSrfLandedHighlands*: The curvature of Minmus is easily visible from here.
MinmusSrfLandedMidlands*: Low gravity and interesting terrain visible from the window invite you to come outside.
MinmusSrfLandedLowlands*: You wonder why are you still sitting in your capsule while there's so much fun to have outside.
MinmusSrfLandedFlats*: You look up at Kerbin and it looks smaller than ever. You think you can just about make out the Mun, too.
MinmusSrfLandedGreatFlats*: You note that landing on a perfectly flat surface is somewhat boring.
MinmusSrfLandedGreaterFlats*: The ground here is completely flat. It's really boring.
MinmusSrfLandedLesserFlats*: "I could be bounded in a nutshell, and count myself a king of infinite space."
MinmusSrfLandedPoles*: Well, it looks no different from here than it did from far away.
MinmusSrfLandedSlopes*: Don't fall over, don't fall over, don't fall over...
It's completely beyond your understanding why you decided to land here and not on the perfectly flat surface of the frozen lake below.
MinmusInSpaceLow*: You wonder if it's possible to EVA to the surface and back.
You wonder if landing on the top of a mountain will consume less fuel. It would be harder, though.
Jet-packing down there would probably be fun. You doubt you could get back up, though.
You wonder if Minmus tastes of mint, and if you could make a fortune selling tiny edible planets.
You watch Minmus' surface shimmer beneath and wonder if your tongue would stick to the ground upon a thorough licking.
Here we are. The giant mint in space.
You look out of the window and suddenly feel homesick.
Let's go back, I saw a cool rock down there.
I know there is no atmosphere, but it still feels stinky, like overripe cheese.
MinmusInSpaceHigh*: You note that your radio fails to transmit when set to the OFF position.
Kerbin looks small. The Mun looks small. Even the Sun looks small. You turn the binoculars the right way round.
Please let it be a sea of lime sherbet.
Well, it's certainly much smaller in person.
Kerbin's smaller moon isn't as impressive as the other one. However, it does look much more yummy.
At this distance, Kerbin is little more than a pale blue dot, floating the the inky vastness of space.
Hm, how much debris could you kick up if you jumped into one of those lakes from here?

DunaSrfLanded: It is quite dusty outside.
DunaSrfLandedPoles: Why did we land here again? There's nothing here but ice in all directions.
A spiderweb of ice crystals spreads across the pod window as it cools.
DunaSrfLandedMidlands: Very small piles of fine red dust are being deposited on the windows by the thin atmosphere.
You look through the thickened glass and see small hills to the horizon.
DunaSrfLandedLowlands: Dark spots on the surface of Duna were once thought to be the darkness of vegetation growing near the poles as the seasons change. It is now known that the dark coloration is in fact caused by seasonal dust storms.
A monster dust devil winds its way across the plains in the distance, kicking up a trail of fine dust in its wake.
DunaSrfLandedCraters: Duna's surface seems to be covered with dunes. You remark that the planet was aptly-named.
You look out and see the crater floor filled with sand sculpted into patterns by the wind currents.
DunaSrfLandedHighlands: "It's extremely red; over."
I spy with my little eye something beginning with "h".. yes, it's "hill".
You write down 'red', 'bouldered', and 'dusty' in the logbook.
DunaSrfLandedMidlandSea: The gentle winds on the lowland plains deposit a fine coating of dust on the window, turning the world even redder.

"Goooood morning ladies and gentlekerbs, the weather outlook for today features highs of a nippy -5 degrees with the dust storm hazard level at medium-high. If you're going out, be sure to bundle up warm and always carry a respirator. This is Jeb, for KSWN."
DunaSrfLandedNorthernBasin: The red landscape outside the window doesn't look particularly inviting. Do you have to go out there?
Sand starts to pile up on the window ledge. That's right, sand! Not dust for once!
DunaSrfLandedSouthernBasin: The ice is slowly creeping over the ancient lava field. You hope it doesn't creep over you, too.
One side of the basin is mostly ice, the other is mostly rock. You check the map to see which side you landed on.
DunaSrfLandedNorthernShelf: You squint through the dust-are those mountains off in the distance? ...Those aren't mountains-they're canyon walls!
With the craft now still, the red Dunaian sand starts to pile up on the window. You try to tap the window to knock it off, until a wind gust does that for you.
DunaSrfLandedMidlandCanyon: The canyon walls here look...curvy. Odd.
The chasm looks ominous from down here, like any self-respecting canyon should.
DunaSrfLandedEasternCanyon: The canyon is so huge and dusty you can't see the other wall. It makes you feel perticularly small. Well... OK, stop rubbing it in, Duna!
The air is thick with dust down here, and the haze reduces visibility to a couple of hundred metres or so.
DunaSrfLandedWesternCanyon: This canyon looks pretty narrow and relatively dust free, letting you get a clear view of the walls.
You imagine the canyon as a groove in a giant, red vinyl record. Actually, maybe music-loving aliens built this?
DunaSrfLandedPolarHighlands: You look out and see a network of tunnels running beneath the ice to a nearby hole, and try not to think about what this says about the ice.
DunaSrfLandedPoalrHighlands: The craft briefly slides and judders over the ice before coming to a stop. It takes a while to let go of the grip handles.
DunaSrfLandedPolarCraters: You look out at the vast icy bowl in the landscape and dearly hope the EVA suits are climate controlled.
The cabin radiator clicks on, dispelling the cold seeping into the craft.
DunaFlyingLow: You really like Duna. It reminds you of chocolate pancakes.
DunaFlyingLowPoles: A scattering of tiny ice crystals flows past the window like dust particles.
The pod window is cold to the touch. Better hope there's some mittens in the cupboard.
DunaFlyingLowMidlands: The low red hills start to blend into one another in the distance. You rub your eyes.

The planet looks very red, which reminds you of the red chicken you left in the astronaut complex fridge. You dearly hope it will still be there when you come back, because you ARE coming back. Right, Mission Control?
DunaFlyingLowLowlands: The plains below are criscrossed with lines of disturbed sand, like sand-based crazy paving.
You gaze at the planet, and realize you'll have no problems keeping your night vision.
DunaFlyingLowCraters: You shiver in your seat. Your thoughts turn to the glowing warmth of re-entry into Kerbin's atmosphere and you start to miss your family.
You see the crater walls looming imposingly off in the distance. Whatver made this, it must have been a big ol' wallop.
DunaFlyingLowHighlands: You think you see a face. Maybe it was a trick of the light... No, no... that's a face.
You can get a good enough vantage point at the surface, why bother with coming up that little bit higher?
DunaFlyingLowMidlandSea: You spot a towering column of dust far off in the distance. Wonder what that could be?
There's a monster dust storm bearing down on your landing spot. Might not want to spend too much time there.
DunaFlyingLowNorthernBasin: The smell of the snacks you put on to warm up earlier starts to make you hungry at a bad time.
In the low Duna gravity, things fly further. You know that because you just got hit in the face by a boot thrown at you by turbulence.
DunaFlyingLowSouthernBasin: A sudden gust of freezing wind almost blows the ship off course. Such are the hazards of being near the icy zones.
You want to do a barrel roll, until you realise KSC is closely monitoring all craft telemetry and you'd rather keep your job when you return.
DunaFlyingLowNorthernShelf: The huge scar scraped out of the surface stretches all the way to the reddish, hazy horizon.
A powerful gust of wind shakes the capsule violently and a long-forgotten snack falls out of the overhead locker.
DunaFlyingLowMidlandCanyon: This one will take some landing skill. You try to pick out a spot that isn't too steeply sloping.
You can't see much more from here, as the canyon curves out of your view.
DunaFlyingLowEasternCanyon: There's so much dust you can barely see the ground. You're flying blind with only the altimeter to tell you of the nearby ground.
The dust clouds roil past the window, as though the very air is alive with sand.
DunaFlyingLowWesternCanyon: You look down, and hope you don't land on one of the steep walls. That would be... problematic.
The canyon stretches off to the hazy horizon in both directions. You feel like you've said something similar before.
DunaFlyingLowPolarHighlands: The icy hills stretch out in all directions beneath you. The ice glare makes it difficult to look down.
You find a page in the safety manual on how to deal with space polar bear attacks. Unfortunately, all it says is "run away".
DunaFlyingLowPolarCraters: All you can see is pinkish ice to the horizon, albeit lacking the dust haze of the lower latitudes.
You fumble around the glovebox for a pair of sunglasses to protect from the ice glare, which is strong even in the weak Dunaian sun.
DunaFlyingHigh: There seems to be something resembling a face on the surface of a planet, but it's only a play of light and shadow.
The faint whistle of the the thin air around the capsule is almost relaxing.
You look outside to try and spot Duna's version of the "thin blue line", but there is none to be seen.
DunaInSpaceLow: Duna. Kerbin's small, failed twin. The thought of what it could have been makes you sad.
As you orbit the planet, you pick up what appears to be a TV signal from Kerbin. It's so badly distorted that you can't make neither head nor tail of it.
DunaInSpaceHigh: The crew is concerned that the planet is "too red". Mission Control does not believe this is a good reason to go home.
Hopefully wherever you are is out of the grasping graviational claws of Ike.

DresSrfLanded*: You were right... it does, in fact, seem to be a giant potato.
DresSrfLandedHighlands*: It is exactly as gray and icy as expected. Not a bit of gray less, not a bit of ice more.
DresSrfLandedImpactEjecta*: You see scorch marks and are reminded of back home at the KSC.
DresSrfLandedCanyons*: You bask in the knowledge that you have successfully landed on the Mun. Wait.
DresInSpaceLow*: It's very gray and icy down there. The VAB team is going to use this to lobby for better gray-resistant landing legs.
This looks too small to be a planet.
You see debris from what looks like an impact...
DresInSpaceHigh*: You see what appears to be burn marks, and suddenly feel bad for Dres.
After noticing a large cut across Dres' surface, you wonder if any band-aids in the first aid kit are big enough.
Watching the planet turn beneath you, you miss home.

JoolFlyingLow*: You can feel the green all around you now. This is awesome.
This seems like a situation Jebediah would like to be in, just with more SRBs and explosions.
As you look up through the stormy green clouds, it strikes you that maybe flying that low in Jool's atmosphere wasn't such a good idea.
The craft is shaking and feels very unstable.
You realize you are not a probe.
Your aerobraking attempt has turned into an aero landing. Was this part of the plan?
Mission Control asks for a report, but all you can picture is a giant valedictory explosion.
You may have gotten yourself into a bit of a pickle.
Landing on Jool, eh? Why not?
Still no sign of a surface. Perhaps if you flew even lower?
JoolFlyingHigh*: While observing from the window you get startled as Jool seems to look back at you! After a moment of confusion you find out that it was your face reflecting from the window and comment on the screams to have been "only radio chatter".
As the craft flies through the dense green clouds, it slows down dramatically. Just don't get stuck.
It's more than disconcerting to see clouds in all directions.
You see a massive lightning bolt in the far off clouds. You nervously ask Mission Control if the ship is rated for that kind of discharge.
The planet before you resembles nothing so much as a giant tennis ball. Perhaps it has fuzz that can be harvested to build a space elevator?
You look out the window and are amazed by the view.
You wonder if you will live to see the next hour.
You hope the aerobrake won’t be an aero landing.
JoolInSpaceLow*: You wonder if orange juice here is green.
The green color of the atmosphere reminds you of the soft grass of Kerbin. Sort of.
You note just how big Jool is, and try not to think about how small it makes you feel.
The massive size of the Van Allen radiation belts is ripping electrons from the ship's hull. You carefully avoid the walls, avoiding to be zapped with the mother of all static discharges.
Maybe you'd be able to get a sample of metallic hydrogen before the inevitable crunch?
The green orb is looking very big now.
As you look down upon the great green planet, you think you can see a stirring of the clouds far, far below.
JoolInSpaceHigh*: You see three big moons and two little ones. You want to land on them all.
Captain's log: "We are out of snacks."
Glancing out the viewport, you see a gray disc blitz by. You start to think you are not alone.
As you ponder at the planet's hue you are reminded that green is the color preferred most by geniuses.
You think about Jool's radiation belts and are wondering if you're still alive.
Suprisingly, the radiation here is only 1.5 MR/hr. Oh wait, that's a capital M...

LaytheSrfLanded: You miss your family. You begin to write a postcard.
LaytheSrfLandedPoles: One day we will have a permanent base here. One day.
LaytheSrfLandedShores: It appears that we are very much on the ground right now.
LaytheSrfLandedDunes: You wonder if you just could sit on the beach, relax, and have a margarita.
LaytheSrfLandedCrescentBay: After hearing the air is breathable, you open your space helmet, only to find that breathable does not mean odorless or pleasant.
LaytheSrfLandedTheSagenSea: As you look out the window, you see something glowing below you. You decide it's probably best to get to shore as soon as possible.
LaytheSrfSplashed: Looking at the map of Laythe to figure out which of the islands is the closest, you know it'll be a long swim for any chance of rescue.
LaytheSrfSplashedCrescentBay: You suddenly realize that you forgot to bring your towel.
LaytheSrfSplashedTheSagenSea: You wish you had brought a boat.
You thought you saw something moving in the water. Leaving the capsule might not be a good idea.
LaytheFlyingLow: Are you sure we aren't on Kerbin?
LaytheFlyingLowPoles: You started daydreaming about being the captain of the Space Battleship 'Spirit of Kerbin', until being woken up by a beeping sound.
LaytheFlyingLowShores: The ocean looks inviting. You realize that you forgot your swimsuit. Oh well, Mission Control would never have approved.
LaytheFlyingLowDunes: The crew deliberates heavily on the correct way to pronounce 'Laythe'.
LaytheFlyingLowCrescentBay: The atmosphere appears to have blocked most of Jool's radiation at this altitude. You hope your tan doesn't suffer as a result.
LaytheFlyingLowTheSagenSea: You look at the moon below and feel homesick.
LaytheFlyingHigh: Readings for alpha and beta radiation are low here, but there are still elevated readings for X-Rays and gamma radiation.
LaytheInSpaceLow: Your radiation instruments are showing some strange results. Perhaps Laythe has a magnetic field that is interacting with Jool's.
You start to think how lucky you are that the Radiation Belt DLC for life isn't out yet.
LaytheInSpaceHigh: The radiation here must be intense. You are glad the cockpit of your spacecraft is heavily shielded.
As you gaze at Laythe, you begin to wonder whether Kerbin is the only planet harboring life.
As the crew look down upon Laythe, they are reminded of home and the huge distance they have traveled.
You know this blue liquid can't be water. This moon is outside the habatible zone.

VallSrfLanded*: Quakes rattle tools and loose gear every few minutes.
VallSrfLandedMidlands*: You note that Vall is "Cold, desolate... lonely." Mission Control tells you to quit being so melodramatic.
VallSrfLandedHighlands*: You can't wait to go outside. It looks so beautiful!
VallSrfLandedLowlands*: Everything in the ship just bounced, that was one heck of a quake!
VallInSpaceLow*: Staring out the window, you realize how blue the moon is.
The surface looks broken by eons of geological activity.
As you stare, something stirs inside you. You make a note to cook your food more carefully next time.
VallInSpaceHigh*: As you take your notes, you notice that Vall can fit in the hole of your clipboard.
You can swear you saw a black formation near the south pole, but you lost it before you could grab the binoculars.
The moon is a beautiful mosaic of blue and white.
You wonder why this moon’s inclination is exactly the same as Vall's.

TyloSrfLandedHighlands: The gravity is quite heavy and reminds you of home.
TyloSrfLandedLowlands: You can't walk a few meters without your face becoming friends with the surface. It is a little agitating.
TyloSrfLandedMara: You congratulate yourself on getting down here without excessive lithobraking. You then begin calculating your delta-V to see if you can get home.
TyloInSpaceLow: You wonder why, despite its size, Tylo doesn't have an atmosphere.
The crew wonders why they even need to land on this miserable rock.
TyloInSpaceHigh: Was this orbit intentional, or is Mission Control hiding something from you?
It's like Kerbin, but without all the cool stuff like an atmosphere, oceans, or tectonic plates. Okay, so it's not like Kerbin at all.

BopSrfLanded: The landing legs sink into the surface and make a squelching sound.
BopSrfLandedPeaks: I can see Kerbin from here! I wish I had a telescope...
Mission Control tells you that the amazing vista you see is just an optical illusion. You take some picture anyway.
BopSrfLandedRidges: You hear an odd noise on the radio, most probably caused by radiation from the host planet.
BopSrfLandedPoles: You're not sure why, but this place gives you an eerie feeling.
BopSrfLandedSlopes: From here Bop looks entirely untrustworthy.
BopSrfLandedValley: You smell squid. The pilot must be eating sushi. What else could it be?
BopInSpaceLow: You wonder if Twist and Pull are around here somewhere?
You stare out of the window, only to see some rough, jagged terrain that fails to please the eye.
Bop looks unremarkable. TOO unremarkable. Surely it's hiding some dark secret...
That mountain high enough to hit you. You hope you are landing.
Last time we went to Gilly, we did not come back... oh? This is Bop?
BopInSpaceHigh: This rock looks completely unremarkable. You have an uneasy feeling about it, most probably because you’ve been out of tasty snacks for the last six months.
The captured asteroid looks like a big brown potato. You can't help but wonder if changing inclination was worth the trouble.
Stuck in an eccentric, inclined orbit, this rock offers nothing to look at. A complete waste of time.
Bop looks remarkably like a potato from this height.
Mission Control informs you that that no, that is not a potato outside your window.
You realize that that mountain is remarkably high.

PolSrfLanded: The crew has been reduced to excessive sneezing.
PolSrfLandedPoles: You can't help but giggle realizing that you've landed on the pole of Pol. Mission Control is not amused.
PolSrfLandedLowlands: You are happy that landing is over.
PolSrfLandedHighlands: You wonder if you can bring back some pol for your garden on Kerbin.
PolInSpaceLow: Turns out Pol is real, and not just a speck of pollen on the lens of Galileo Kerman's telescope. Who knew?
Just looking at the surface makes you sneeze.
PolInSpaceHigh: You wipe off the window to make sure that speck you see is not a piece of pollen.

EelooSrfLanded*: You wonder about the ship that got you here. Are you really sure you can get back?
As you trip and fall on a very small rock, you begin to lose feeling in your arms. Terrified of the sub zero temperatures, you look down and realize you've been laying on them.
You feel cold.
You feel very cold.
You feel very hot. The physician diagnoses you with extreme hypothermia. Then you realize you hallucinated the physician.
EelooSrfLandedPoles*: You look out the windows and see ice. Lots and lots of ice. And some rocks here and there. Icy rocks.
EelooSrfLandedNorthernGlaciers*: From this far away, the radio seems to be a bit patchy. You can hear static blocking out your next instructions. Oh well, it probably wasn't important anyway.
EelooSrfLandedLowlands*: You report that Eeloo is "distant and lonely". Mission Control is not happy and demands more information.
EelooSrfLandedHighlands*: You take notes on how barren and lonely Eeloo is. You then try to calculate how long it will take for the signal to get to and from Kerbin.
EelooSrfLandedIceCanyons*: It seems you have eaten all of your snacks. You start weeping quietly in the corner.
EelooSrfLandedCraters*: You look out of the capsule and can't find Kerbin. What has mission control gotten you into this time?
EelooSrfLandedMidlands*: You wonder what's beneath the ice...
EelooInSpaceLow*: You note that the capsule is not equipped with central heating, and Mission Control informs you this is because you were meant to go to Moho. This does not instill you with confidence.
For a moment, you can't quite remember which star is your sun.
I feel like we could go anywhere. Let's go. Like, now. Really, anywhere.

The crew look out towards the frozen ice ball Eeloo, and are momentarily lost for words. Few of Kerbal-kind have made it this far from home, and the courage, determination, and drive their species has to put them here dawns on everyone. Their thoughts are profound and humble, that so few are supported by so many.
Eeloo's sleek surface reminds you of home's ice caps. After some thinking about your distance from home, you go to sleep.
You're beginning to regret not bringing along the extra bag of snacks.
EelooInSpaceHigh*: The orbit of Eeloo being so odd, you begin to ponder the existence of a planet beyond it. You think a good name for this planet would be "Planet Y".
You look out the window and notice beige stripes and spots on the surface. You immediately wonder if it's cookie dough.
It reminds you of a snowball. Is it winter already?
It looks like a great big spherical ice cube!
Now this is an icy planet. You start imagining epic snowball fights in micro-gravity.
Eeloo looks very white; maybe someone covered it in a giant bed sheet?
As you stare down at the splintered, lifeless surface, a question forms in your mind: "Was this really worth it?"

evaReport.description: Observations made by a Kerbal while outside the spacecraft.
evaReport.hints: Visual impressions focused on the surrounding environment, terrain, atmosphere, or immediate conditions.

SunInSpaceLow*: You release a gray rock from your spacesuit pocket and make a resolution to come back in a year to find out if it looks like Moho.
You think its becoming sort of hot... You hope you will be allowed back inside soon.
Your wax wings begin to melt.
You sincerely hope that the guys back home made this suit hardened against radiation.
You suddenly regret not bringing your SPF-10000 sun cream with you.
You think sunglasses would have been a good idea.
You think your new tan will impress everyone back in the Space Center.
You smell a faint burning coming from your spacesuit.
As you stare at Kerbol, you manage to resist the urge to take off your helmet to have some toasted marshmallows.
Your thermometer blew up inside the cabin. That can't be a good sign.
It's a dry heat.
You really wish you'd brought marshmallows. You bet you could make S'mores from here.
You can't wait to tell your friends about flying near the Sun with nothing but your spacesuit on.
Good thing you brought your 4000+ sunscreen.
The suit feels a bit warm.
Outside the ship, you can really see the power of the sun. As you watch, you see a flash on the surface and a tongue of plasma shoots off.
You wonder if the radiation from Kerbol is harming you in any way.
Your spacesuit is starting to heat up now. Maybe it's a good idea to get back to your spacecraft.
Is it just me, or is it hot in here?
You are starting to sweat profusely and wonder why you had left the capsule in the first place.
You feel as if you should have brought a stronger sunscreen.
It feels a bit warm.
A massive, luminous sphere of plasma held together by its own gravity! This one appears to be undergoing thermonuclear fusion of hydrogen into helium in its core.
Kerbol is bigger then you have ever could have seen it on Kerbin. You wish you'd brought your sunglasses.
You're feeling really hot and can't stand the unbearable heat.
You crack a bad joke about landing on the sun at night to Mission Control.
Since the solar panels produce more energy, you crank up the air conditioner.
You realize that this is not where you want to be right now.
Turns out jet-pack fuel does not like being exposed to this kind of temperature.
You realize things would go pretty badly if you strayed away from the vessel.
Your geiger counter is maxed out at 3.6 Roentgen.
SunInSpaceHigh*: All you can remember while glaring at the empty void is the tale of the Space Kraken. You really want to return to the ship.
You gaze down at the majestic fireball that is Kerbol, only to realize that you have temporarily blinded yourself.
You're suddenly alarmed at how empty space is. Just you, the sun, and... your ship. Mission Control questions why your heartbeat just spiked.
As you look toward Kerbol, you realize you forgot your sunglasses and Jeb has the only pair anyways.
You wonder if that burning smell is real or just your imagination.
Finally some peace and quiet!
You attempt to become the first Kerbal to finish a crossword puzzle in space, but your pencil floats away before you can even start.
"Am I stupid or just upside-down?"
It's empty. It goes on forever, and... Oh my gosh, it's full of stars!
You decide to have a staring contest with Kerbol. You lose.
The sun appears... Yellow?
Having spent time in stellar orbit, you request that you be declared a new planet. Mission Control promises to think about it.
Looking around, you notice lots of sparkly things. One of them is REALLY sparkly.
They should have sent a poet. It's so beautiful.
You look out into the universe and wonder if you're alone, if Kerbin is a cradle of life in an otherwise sterile universe. Mission Control tells you to stop thinking so much.
It would have been better if it was green.
You search in every direction, but there is no Kraken to be seen.
Suddenly you hear a deep growling. Is that the space Kraken?
Staring into the void, you ponder your place in the universe.

MohoSrfLanded*: At least you will have a nice tan when you get home.
MohoSrfLandedNorthPole*: The thermal systems of your suit protest as you move around in the bright sunlight. It's really hot out here.
MohoSrfLandedMidlands*: You realize you forgot to bring your sunglasses.
MohoSrfLandedWesternLowlands*: You trip over a small rock. Fortunately the gravity is low enough that you aren't hurt.
MohoSrfLandedCentralLowlands*: You are relieved to see that spacesuits have an SPF of one million.
MohoSrfLandedHighlands*: You contemplate sun bathing for your return publicity shots.
MohoSrfLandedMinorCraters*: There's a hole around here somewhere.
Look! There's water on Moho! Oh, wait... that's just sweat that got on your visor.
MohoSrfLandedSouthWesternLowlands*: Gene was skeptical, but you're glad you brought an umbrella.
MohoSrfLandedSouthEasternLowlands*: Suddenly, you really want some ice cream. You ask mission control for the fastest transfer window to Minmus.
MohoSrfLandedCanyon*: You can hear the coolant rushing through the tubes in your suit as fast as it can, and decide that you'd really like to go back inside.
MohoSrfLandedSouthPole*: You hope to find the exact point of the south pole. Then you can plant a flag. But mission control might have other plans....
MohoInSpaceLow*: You can actually feel the radiation.
MohoInSpaceLowNorthPole*: You pull out a energy bar, before realizing that you can't get it through your helmet. You compromise by smearing it on the visor.
MohoInSpaceLowNorthernSinkholeRidge*: The ridge looks like the lips of an enormous mouth.
MohoInSpaceLowNorthernSinkhole*: That's a deep, dark hole down there. You've heard legends of a Kraken, and wonder if this is its den.
MohoInSpaceLowMidlands*: It's really bright this close to Kerbol. You wonder if Dyson Spheres could be made by Kerbalkind.
MohoInSpaceLowWesternLowlands*: You feel dehydrated, but your suit says you're fine.
MohoInSpaceLowCentralLowlands*: The scientists back home want to know if life could exist here. You decide that they should've come instead.
MohoInSpaceLowHighlands*: You draw a house in the dust. You're pretty sure the rocket will cover it up during launch, though.
You hope you're high enough that you don't hit any mountains.
MohoInSpaceLowMinorCraters*: Are there many asteroids around? Any asteroid that made those craters could do a real number on a Kerbal.
MohoInSpaceLowSouthWesternLowlands*: It looks like the ship is covered in pollen. No, wait, it's just dust.
MohoInSpaceLowCanyon*: You can almost feel the radiation.
MohoInSpaceLowSouthPole*: Surely you're safe from the Kraken here.
MohoInSpaceHigh*: Stepping out of the shadow is like walking into a furnace.

EveSrfLandedPoles: Why can't the R&D eggheads invent a suit with movement-assisting servomotors already?
Poles, schmoles, this the planetary oven all right.
EveSrfLandedMidlands: You should have done more exercise back on Kerbin.
"Welcome to hell, weather's great here. Pull up a chair, have a cold beer....."
EveSrfLandedExplodiumSea: You decide to take a short walk, but think better of it after the first few steps.
EveSrfLandedLowlands: You wonder what jerk put lead weights in your suit.
The toss of a pebble suggests that the atmosphere is REALLY, REALLY thick.
EveSrfLandedHighlands: You feel like you may have put on some weight.
"Should I take the left foot or the right foot?" The decision weighs heavy on your shoulders - literally.
EveSrfLandedPeaks: "Help! I've fallen and I can't get up!"
The steep, gravelly ground makes walking up the slope torturously slow as you slide back constantly.
EveSrfLandedImpactEjecta: The extreme pressure is making it hard to move. Thankfully, you can still wiggle your toes.
You step on a loose stone and fall hard on your face. That hurts here!
EveSrfLandedImpactEJecta: The extreme pressure is making it hard to move. Thankfully, you can still wiggle your toes.
You step on a loose stone and fall hard on your face. That hurts here!
EveSrfLandedCraterLake: That water nearby would be looking quite inviting were it not for the fact that it is almost certainly not water or healthy for you.
EveSrfLandedWesternSea: Everything about this place makes you want to get back into the capsule and lie down.
EveSrfLandedOlympus: If I have seen further, it is by standing on the shoulders of giants! Or gods.
EveSrfLandedEasternSea: The liquid in the seas has contaminated the soil around here. You know this because your boot has been bleached of colour.
EveSrfLandedFoothills: This planet was certainly not made with kerbals in mind, and vice versa.
You push some dirt into a small pile with your feet. There, a foothill on a foothill!
EveSrfLandedShallows: You've come all this way to Eve's coast, only for your bucket and spade to melt and dissolve instantly.
EveSrfSplashedPoles: You pour some water from a bottle in your pocket onto the surface, and it forms a steaming sheen on top.
EveSrfSplashedExplodiumSea: You realize swimming in a spacesuit is harder than you thought. You regret the decision of leaving the ship.
The liquid is stripping the colour off your spacesuit alarmingly quickly.
EveSrfSplashedLowlands: The sea is hot. Very hot. You want to get out of it now.
EveSrfSplashedImpactEjecta: The crater walls curve out of sight around the ocean you find yourself floating in.
EveSrfSplashedCraterLake: You hope dearly that your suit has been properly sealed and corrosion-proofed.
The extremely dense liquid is hard to get your hands and legs into to swim with.
EveSrfSplashedWesternSea: You have the idea to use your suit to dive with. You can only just get your head below the surface, and you can't see anything.
The "water" is thick and soupy. It drips in large blobs off your hand.
EveSrfSplashedEasternSea: Whose idea was it to make these helmets so large? You can't even look down.
You had a dream like this once. Unlike the dream, however, this isn't grape soda, and robot dolphins are not swimming in it.
EveSrfSplashedShallows: Mission Control assures you that there are definitely no monsters in the sea.
You try and light the explodium on fire, forgetting you're currently swimming in it. Fortunately, there's no oxygen to allow it to ignite.
EveFlyingLowPoles: If this place is hell, you feel like an infernal Santa Klaus, flying through the hot polar air.
The temperatures are slightly less burning at these latitudes, and the rushing air makes it slightly cooler still.
EveFlyingLowMidlands: You wonder, supposing you let go, if the atmosphere is thick enough to allow you to fall gently to the ground like a feather.
You'd say it looks boring, but in a place like Eve, nothing is ever boring.
EveFlyingLowExplodiumSea: You start to think that it's entirely possible to swim in this atmosphere as well as the oceans.
The dense not-water probably wouldn't break your fall if you needed it to. Hopefully you won't.
EveFlyingLowLowlands: The atmosphere is so thick you have a sneaking suspicion it may be made out of some kind of soup. Well, it wouldn't hurt to taste it... right?
Flying in the thickest parts of the atmosphere is hard, with the very air opposing your progress.
EveFlyingLowHighlands: The purple behemoth dares you to slip.
The mountains are the surface's way of spitting defiance in the face of the crushing atmosphere and gravity. Be more mountain.
EveFlyingLowPeaks: "I will not give up, I will not give in! I will land boots down, I will stand again."
Spiky peaks rise from the surface, as if trying to snag you in midair.
EveFlyingLowImpactEjecta: The miles of impact debris must only for a small part of the crater.
EveFlyingLowImpactEjects: The miles of impact debris must only for a small part of the crater.
EveFlyingLowCraterLake: The thick atmosphere makes it difficult to move around. You swear that if not for the high gravity, you could hover a bit just by flapping your arms.
You wonder how far down the shimmering liquid filling the impact basin goes.
EveFlyingLowWesternSea: You're more concerned with the climate control warning light that just appeared.
You would rather not end up in the drink, thank you very much.
EveFlyingLowOlympus: You're not sure you needed to be flying for this. You could just have climbed a bit higher up the mountain.
The view from the ground was amazing and this one's even better.
EveFlyingLowEasternSea: What's that floating down there? It better not be piece of the ship.
When you were sent to Eve, you didn't anticipate trying not to tumble through the air over a toxic ocean
EveFlyingLowFoothills: You feel like your helmet is dissolving. Oh wait... IT IS! Get me back in, now!
The suit starts to feel awful warm. You crank the suit thermostat down another notch.
EveFlyingLowShallows: The set of tools you had clipped to your belt fall off and plummet out of sight.
You're not sure if you're near or far from the ocean surface. All the purple is playing havoc with your depth perception.
EveFlyingHigh: You deliberate whether you'd rather fall on the ground or into that nasty liquid.
Even all the way up here the atmosphere still buffets you around.
You know how dangerous Eve atmospheric entry is, and wish to lodge a formal objection to this highly dangerous course of action.
EveInSpaceLowPoles: Nope. No ice at these poles. Just more purple water and dust.
EveInSpaceLowMidlands: If purple is the color of royalty, Eve is certainly regal!
Some of the rolling hills are large enough to be picked out from orbit.
EveInSpaceLowExplodiumSea: You wonder if you could light the ocean on fire.
EveInSpaceLowLowlands: The Lowlands are the closest thing Eve has to some coastal plains.
Most low-altitude areas are on the east and western shores of the primary ocean.
EveInSpaceLowHighlands: You wonder if you will ever be returning home. Probably not.
Most of Eve's land surface is covered in these low hills.
EveInSpaceLowPeaks: These are some proper mountains. You feel proud of them.
Can we please not land on those big, spiky mountains?
EveInSpaceLowImpactEjecta: You trace the outlines of the craters' ejecta systems to take your mind off the landing.
EveInSpaceLowCraterLake: The Crater is double the size of Kerbin's biggest. Everything is bigger on Eve, even the cosmic gunshot scars.
The rebound peak in the middle of the lake that fills the basin makes a prominent island in the centre.
EveInSpaceLowWesternSea: A thin, elongated, scraggly inland sea carving a slash through the landmass.
It doesn't look very impressive, but no doubt it seems much bigger from downstairs.
EveInSpaceLowOlympus: The highest peaks on Eve. You're torn between thinking they would make a good landing site for their altitude, or bad for their spikiness.
You feel like a god, looking down on your domain from the heavens.
EveInSpaceLowEasternSea: If you squint, it looks like a stretched trilobite. That's just me, isn't it?
The ocean sparkles and shines as light dapples off the water.
EveInSpaceLowFoothills: Lumpy land ahoy! Such pretender mountains.
A burst of noise over comms breaks your concentration and makes you forget the notes you were making.
EveInSpaceLowShallows: Those oceans look flammable. You'd like to see the explosion if you dropped a match in.
Those shapes under the surface must be giant sea monsters. Mission Control gives their end of the radio a dope-slap.
EveInSpaceHigh: From this position you can clearly make out lakes and oceans of some kind on the surface below.
Eve looks almost inviting from up here. You, however, know better than to listen to its siren call.
A vivid green aurora blossoms over Eve's poles. Eve can be beautiful when it wants to be.

GillySrfLandedMidlands: You become concerned that the rock you threw away may loop all the way around Gilly and hit you in the back of your head.
You try to walk, but instead go several meters into the air. It takes minutes before you float back down.
The seemingly conflictory orientations of things confuse you, and you fall over in very slow motion.
If that was 'one small step', you don't want to imagine how big a 'giant leap' would be in this gravity.
You balance yourself on your pinky finger and start doing push-ups, only to find yourself slowly floating away.
GillySrfLandedLowlands: Walk very slowly... Oh no! You tripped.
It takes a while to get used to the idea of kilometer-long jumps.
Using your RCS systems for balance, you manage to walk along the surface using only two fingers.
We could really use some anchors here.
GillySrfLandedHighlands: If only the launchpad had been here instead of KSC, this whole space thing would've been so much easier. You make a note to berate the administrators back home over their poor choice.
One small step for Kerbal, one giant leap for WHAAAAA!
That rock you threw is still going...
You make a mental note of how light you feel and then deduce that it may be best to keep both feet on the surface.
You probably shouldn't jump. You might not come back down.
GillyInSpaceLowMidlands: It's easy to fly away from things, but just as easy to jet back over.
GillyInSpaceLowLowlands: You think you can jet-pack into orbit ten times over.
GilleInSpaceLowMidlands: You ask Mission Control if you could pleeeeease be allowed to jetpack between the surface and orbit?
GillyInSpaceLowHighlands: You wonder how awesome it would be to make a jump with a rover from the hills here.
You try to touch a passing peak. Mission Control tells you to go back into your capsule and raise your damn orbit.
GillyInSpaceHigh: Looking down, you see the two moons of Eve. As you are about to record the new satellite, you realize that one of the two is, in fact, a speck of dust.
"Wait... there's a planet below me? Does it even have gravity?"
The terrain grades in colour between tan and light brown.

KerbinSrfLanded*: Finally. Time to stretch your legs!
I wish I had brought pants.
It seems we are on our home planet.
KerbinSrfLandedWater*: You think you see a shark swimming in the water nearby. Wait, no, that's just a landing leg.
You consider taking your helmet off to smell the fresh ocean breeze, but the frantic yelling over the comms from KSC convinces you otherwise.
You wonder if a shark can bite through your spacesuit. You try it yourself and fail. You hope that shark teeth aren't any sharper than yours.
KerbinSrfLandedGrasslands*: As you go trudging through the grass you hope you don't step on a cow-patty. The spaceship will stink for the rest of the trip.
You free your potted plant and hope it does well in the wild.
You want to take your helmet off, and breathe the sweet air of your home planet.
KerbinSrfLandedHighlands*: You catch yourself as you nearly step into a 100 meter crevasse and slide down a massive glacier. No wonder they call this the Highlands.
The hills are steep and the weather is miserable. No wonder people from the Highlands are always so cranky.
KerbinSrfLandedShores*: You take some sand from the desert and compare it with beach sand. They're actually fairly different!
A Girlbal approaches you with a problem. It seems she has too many seashells. You try to tell her that she should sell seashells on the sea shore but find it difficult to finish your sentence.
The wet consistency of beach sand makes excellent building material and you immediately set about building a small shrine to your glory.
KerbinSrfLandedMountains*: You slowly inch your way away from the spacecraft, just to tell KSC you did, hoping that you don't get blown off the mountaintop by the winds.
A little bit closer and you'll either be the first Kerbal on Mt. Keverest or a green stain on the side of the mountain.
You look around at the mountains around you, taking everything in. You suddenly think to yourself, "What am I still doing here, space is up there, and it's so close!"
KerbinSrfLandedDeserts*: It is so hot here that your feet are burning even through the spacesuit.
You pour your juice onto a small cactus. You decide to call it Mr. Prick and hope it grows up to be a beautiful palm tree. That's how it works, right?
KerbinSrfLandedBadlands*: Your thoughts turn to running back to the capsule and hiding from whatever might live in the Badlands.
You place some snacks on a makeshift altar. You hope that your offering pleases the denizens of the Badlands.
Badlands, Shmadlands. It takes more than a bad name to scare this kerbonaut!
KerbinSrfLandedTundra*: You wander around sort of hoping you could find a moose to ride on.
You wander around the stunted trees noting that you are FINALLY taller than something.
KerbinSrfLandedIceCaps*: The ice crystals spider across the visor of your spacesuit. You hope that the suit was made with more care than the parts that went into your rocket.
You eagerly place a magnet on the ground. Nothing observable happens and you walk away disappointed.
You remembered an old tale of explorers who froze to death here and hope you don't end up the same way.
KerbinSrfLandedNorthernIceShelf*: Light from the ship and sky gleams off the shining white ground.
The ground is white. The sky is white. Even the notepad paper is white.
KerbinSrfLandedSouthernIceShelf*: You're so close to the pole you could swear the compass needle changes orientation with every step.
The ice here isn't snow, and what little loose stuff there is is no good for making snowballs.
KerbinSrfSplashed*: Jeb... is that you biting my leg?
This is not the same thing as the blue stuff above us.
This suit was obviously not intended for scuba diving.
You see a fish swimming in front of your helmet and you start asking yourself: "What would happen if my suit wasn't waterproof?"
Another water landing. You feel fortunate that the EVA suits double as emergency flotation devices.
Good thing these spacesuits act like flotation devices.
It's like training in the pool.
As the water rushes to your face, you begin to thrash and wail and gasp, and then remember your suit is airtight.
What are those creatures circling below?
Now I really wish I knew how to swim!
KerbinSrfSplashedWater*: You hope they made this spacesuit waterproof as well as space-proof.
You calculate the number of snacks you should bring to be able to swim to KSP.
Oh look a fishy! Then you remember what happened to your last goldfish and decide to let this one go.
You have trouble swimming because your helmet is more buoyant than the rest of your spacesuit.
Is that a shark over there?
Something nibbles on your foot.
Help! Help! Where's a lifeguard when you need them?
KerbinSrfSplashedGrasslands*: You peer down into the murky water. What could be lurking down there?
KerbinSrfSplashedShores*: You look at the inviting waves and realize that you forgot your swimsuit!
Ew! Something just touched your foot. Oh wait, it's just seaweed.
KerbinSrfSplashedDeserts*: It's not a mirage! There's actally water under you for once!
KerbinSrfSplashedBadlands*: You can feel the heat of the water through your suit. You don't really want to swim for much longer.
KerbinSrfSplashedTundra*: The cold from the water starts to seep into your suit. It's not pleasant.
KerbinSrfSplashedIceCaps*: The freezing cold of the water seeps right into your bones almost immediately.
KerbinSrfSplashedNorthernIceShelf*: You keep watch for the nearby icebergs to make sure none of them run you over as you swim.
KerbinSrfSplashedSouthernIceShelf*: An iridescent sheen of ice forms on your helmet visor as water splashes freeze.
KerbinFlyingLow*: I'm free! Free falling.
Get back in! Quick!
This isn't that high up after all...
I wonder if I can buzz the tower while hanging off the side of the cockpit...
KerbinFlyingLowWater*: You hope that sharks can't jump this high.
You are so close to the waves that you can almost touch them. KSC recommends against this, though.
You wonder if you can skip yourself like a rock if you end up falling off.
KerbinFlyingLowGrasslands*: "Why did I go outside? I really want to get back into the capsule!"
You suddenly realize that your space suit doesn't include a parachute.
KerbinFlyingLowHighlands*: Yep, those are the Highlands. Can I go back in now?
You are afraid that your helmet will hit the peak of one of the mountains as you fly by. Who thought an EVA was a good idea here, anyway?
You're pretty sure you can reach out and grab a surface sample from the top of one of these hills.
KerbinFlyingLowShores*: You hang on to the craft one-handed as you use your other hand to fight off a particularly persistent seagull.
You wave at all the Kerbals on the beach below. Surely this will make the news reports.
Flying this close to the ground is definitely not over-rated!
KerbinFlyingLowMountains*: Uhhhh...It's cold, windy, and we're really close to a mountain. Why are we doing this EVA again?
It could be worse. You could be doing this over an active volcano.
The ground is looking awful close. Did you pack a parachute?
KerbinFlyingLowDeserts*: You can see the cactus and scrub brush clearly from here. Too clearly for comfort.
The air is dusty and dry. Each breath leaves you wanting.
KerbinFlyingLowBadlands*: Mission Control assures you reckless displays of bravery are well-received in the Badlands.
As you hang on for dear life, you start to question your career choice.
Are you really sure now is a good time?
KerbinFlyingLowTundra*: Dude, seriously. You know what happens when we try to EVA here. It never ends well.
Those snowdrifts look soft enough to catch you safely. You still don't want to try though.
Your spacesuit keeps you warm even as the brutal winds of the Tundra buffet you outside your craft.
KerbinFlyingLowIceCaps*: You know there is a joke some where in here about an ice hole, but you can't think of it. Must be snack time!
If you fall, would you break through the ice? Probably best not to try.
KerbinFlyingLowNorthernIceShelf*: Even from this vantage point you can't see anything over the horizon.
The solid white, flat surface makes it difficult to judge altitude. Don't think about the prospect you could be dangerously low.
KerbinFlyingLowSouthernIceShelf*: The rushing air somehow makes it even colder. Can we get back in now?
Looking down at the flat ice you can't tell how fast you're moving, or even if you're moving at all. It's unnerving.
KerbinFlyingHigh*: You might be OK for a few minutes.
The clouds look so pretty from up here.
Did you remember to pack a parachute?
Can I go back in now? Please?
Uh, why did we think this EVA was a good idea, again?
More like EXTREME Vehicular Activity, am I right?
You imagine yourself as an intrepid explorer, daring to push back the boundaries of the unknown...then you look out the window and see your mother. (Sigh) Hi mom.
The wind blowing through the vast hills and valleys of grass reminds you of waves on the surface of the ocean.
Great swaths of brown and tan stretch out before you. You wonder how anything can live in such a dry environment. Including you as you hurtle through the atmosphere.
You begin to have an appreciation for the saying 'as right as rain'.
You hold on for dear life as you hurtle through the atmosphere above some of Kerbin's highest mountains. Too much adrenaline.
Why? Just why? Of all the places to do an EVA why did we have to choose here?
Nothing but ice as far as the eye can see...you really want to go back in now.
Is it just you or do those mountaintops seem...sharp? You want to go back inside now.
You are now too high to worry about seagulls and can devote all your to attention to hanging on for dear life.
Kinda cold and windy out here. Not at all like being on the surface at the shores. You wish you hadn't smeared sunscreen all over your EVA suit now.
KerbinInSpaceLow*: You've successfully played hide and seek with Mission Control. Crap.
Oh my gosh, oh my gosh, oh my gosh! I'M IN SPACE!
"Why isn't there any noise? Is something wrong with my spacesuit?"
WHEEEEEEE!
Mom calls, wondering where you are and when you'll be home for dinner.
You wonder why you left your hot dog at Mission Control.
I wonder how the lights turn on and off?
You think you see a something moving on the Mun's surface. You are officially terrified.
This never gets old. It kinda makes you want to... break into song.
KerbinInSpaceLowWater*: You are reminded of the myriad sailors who have been adrift in the ocean below. And here you are, the first one adrift in space.
Why are krakens such a popular topic back at KSC? Don't they live down there in the water?
Planet Kerbin is blue, and there's nothing you can do.
KerbinInSpaceLowGrasslands*: The hills and valleys of the grasslands seem even closer looking through a spacesuit visor. You begin to long for home. And snacks. Need more snacks.
If you squint really hard you can see some of the scattered debris from your discarded booster stages.
It's so big. You wonder if you can see your house from here. Probably not.
KerbinInSpaceLowHighlands*: Yup, that's a mountain down there. What's of greater concern is the mountain of snacks that need to be eaten *nom, nom, nom*.
The Highlands are baby mountains. You wonder if you'll ever get to see them grow up.
You realize you forgot to eat lunch, and hunt in the cupboards for a snack.
KerbinInSpaceLowShores*: You chuckle at the idea that you are blocking someone's sunlight as they try to tan themselves on the beach below.
You are mesmerized by the glint of the sunshine off the waves below as you stare through the thin visor of your helmet.
KerbinInSpaceLowMountains*: You feel like you are in a dream just floating above the mountains below. Hey, you ARE floating above the mountains below!
As you float outside your craft, you decide to count mountaintops to help pass the time.
The craggy mountains below look even closer now that there is no spaceship between you and them.
KerbinInSpaceLowDeserts*: You look down and contemplate the desert below. Then you contemplate having a snack when you return to the ship, because really, snacks are far more interesting than a dusty desert.
Just looking down is making you thirsty.
You contemplate the serene beauty of Kerbin's deserts, and long for solid ground.
KerbinInSpaceLowBadlands*: The badlands don't look so bad from up here. YOU HEAR THAT BADLANDS?! YOU DON'T SCARE US!!.....we're not going down there, are we?
As you sigh in relief at the distance separating you from the Badlands, you find yourself thankful that there's no such thing as Badspace.
Somehow you get the feeling that something down there is watching you.
KerbinInSpaceLowTundra*: Even with only a thin spacesuit and small amount of space separating you from the landscape below the plant life still looks tiny and stunted.
You marvel at how many plants can look so small as you consider letting go of the ship to float freely through space.
Oh look it's the Tundra! It looks cold and inhospitable even from up here.
KerbinInSpaceLowIceCaps*: You make some notes about different shades of white. You conclude that the spectrum ranges from white to extremely white.
Your try to touch an aurora but it remains fiendishly out of reach.
KerbinInSpaceLowNorthernIceShelf*: It looks flat, white and boring from up here.
You squint, and struggle to tell where the land ends and sea ice begins.
KerbinInSpaceLowSouthernIceShelf*: The ice caps gleam brilliantly in the sun. They have a splendent beauty from space, but you know how bad they are on the ground.
The great ice platform below traces an outline through the sea that could easily be mistaken for a true continent.
KerbinInSpaceHigh*: Pssht. Badlands. Whatever. Next they're gonna say Eeloo is a giant snowball and Laythe is populated by another race of Kerbals.
"If you're hearing this, it means you're taking a long time on this spacewalk. The lab boys say that might be a fear reaction."
The lone Kerbal loves it out here.
It crosses your mind that a thin layer of Mylar and glass is the only thing separating you from nothing.
Staring down at tiny Kerbin, it strikes you as a planet that looks mostly harmless.
I can see my house from here!
You look down over the frosty peaks of the planet below, and contemplate the meaning of life. And the integrity of your spacesuit.
The Highlands must be so jealous of you right now.
You wonder why so much ice is built up around each pole...then again, you can't really think of a better place for it.
All of Kerbin stretches out before you. You wonder what is happening far away on the rolling hills below.
As you conduct your EVA you use the Grasslands of Kerbin as a visual reference point to keep from getting confused.
If you squint really hard and press your thumb against the glass, you can almost pretend that the Badlands don't exist!
As you gaze at the beautiful blue ball that is your home-world, you wonder who screwed up and made the Badlands.
Memories of Kerbin's deserts keep you warm as you work in the cold depths of outer space.
As you float peacefully high above the great mountains of Kerbin, you feel content in your choice of careers.
We're in space! Take that Kraken! Who's laughing now, huh???
The coldness of outer space is a sharp contrast to the warmth-filled memories of your time on Kerbin's shores.
You do a somersault and a back-flip from the hatch onto the side of the ship. How do you like THAT KSC?
Here you are, crossing the gulf between the stars. Much like the ancient sailors who braved the journey across the seas.
Wow, look at that view. Now if only we could figure out how to pack snacks in these suits.
As you float above the bleak terrain in your EVA suit your mind wanders, as it often does, to snacks. Yummy, yummy snacks!
You wonder why so much ice is built up around each pole...then again, you can't really think of a better place for it.
The view of the mountains below is much clearer when you're not looking through the window of a spaceship.
You wave in the general direction of Kerbin. Hi Tundra! Bye Tundra!
KerbinSrfLandedKSC*: Truly a very tiny step for Kerbalkind.
You wonder if you can get back in a t-shirt and shorts.
You wave at mission control, they tell you to get back to work.
Mission Control would like to remind you that while the suit is not, in fact, designed for jogging around on Kerbin, there are several more appropriate suits inside.
The surface here is made of concrete. Very interesting...
If a spacesuit was really necessary to get here, I will eat my helmet!
KerbinSrfLandedAdministration*: KSC tour stop number 1 of 31: Welcome to Administration.
You wonder what the briefing will be about.
KerbinSrfLandedAstronautComplex*: KSC tour stop number 2 of 31: Welcome to the Astronaut Complex.
KerbinSrfLandedBaikerbanur*: As you walk, you jot down the things that need restoration work. You run out of space on the notepad.
KerbinSrfLandedBaikerbanurLaunchpad*: You wonder how they ever got to space with technology so much older and more primitive than your already questionable set.
KerbinSrfLandedCrawlerway*: KSC tour stop number 3 of 31: Welcome to the Crawlerway.
You wonder if you can re-purpose the Crawlerway and the Launchpad as a horizontal launcher with a ramp. What could go wrong?
You imagine being the driver of the crawler. It must be the second best job on Kerbin.
KerbinSrfLandedFlagPole*: KSC tour stop number 4 of 31: Welcome to the Flag Pole.
You stumble as you try to get to the base of the pole.
You put on a red cap and try to make a handstand on the poles top.
KerbinSrfLandedLaunchPad*: KSC tour stop number 5 of 31: Welcome to the Launch Pad.
You love the smell of rocket fuel in the morning.
You suspect that you'd better move if you don't want to be fried during a rocket launch.
You are eager to keep things up and running.
You feel compelled to stare at the sky.
We don't seem to have gone very far... yet.
KerbinSrfLandedMissionControl*: KSC tour stop number 6 of 31: Welcome to Mission Control.
You hear Gene Kerman barking "FAILURE is NOT an OPTION!"
You join Walt Kerman in cleaning up the Visitors Lounge for the next launch.
You join Walt Kerman in cleaning up the Visitors Lounge from the latest launch mishap.
KerbinSrfLandedRnD*: KSC tour stop number 23 of 31: Welcome to R&D.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDCentralBuilding*: KSC tour stop number 24 of 31: Welcome to the R&D Central Building.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDCornerLab*: KSC tour stop number 25 of 31: Welcome to the R&D Corner Lab.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDMainBuilding*: KSC tour stop number 26 of 31: Welcome to the R&D Main Building.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDObservatory*: KSC tour stop number 27 of 31: Welcome to the R&D Observatory.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDSideLab*: KSC tour stop number 28 of 31: Welcome to the R&D Side Lab.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDSmallLab*: KSC tour stop number 29 of 31: Welcome to the R&D Small Lab.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDTanks*: KSC tour stop number 30 of 31: Welcome to the R&D Tanks.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDWindTunnel*: KSC tour stop number 31 of 31: Welcome to the R&D Wind Tunnel. You made it to the last stop! Congratulations!
KerbinSrfLandedRunway*: Ah, the smell of asphalt.
KSC tour stop number 7 of 31: Welcome to the Runway.
KerbinSrfLandedSPH*: KSC tour stop number 8 of 31: Welcome to the SPH.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHMainBuilding*: KSC tour stop number 9 of 31: Welcome to the SPH Main Building.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHRoundTank*: KSC tour stop number 10 of 31: Welcome to the SPH Round Tank.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHTanks*: KSC tour stop number 11 of 31: Welcome to the SPH Tanks.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHWaterTower*: KSC tour stop number 12 of 31: Welcome to the SPH Water Tower.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStation*: KSC tour stop number 13 of 31: Welcome to the Tracking Station.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishEast*: KSC tour stop number 14 of 31: Welcome to the Tracking Stations East Dish.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishNorth*: KSC tour stop number 15 of 31: Welcome to the Tracking Stations North Dish.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishSouth*: KSC tour stop number 16 of 31: Welcome to the Tracking Stations South Dish.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationHub*: KSC tour stop number 17 of 31: Welcome to the Tracking Station Hub.
KerbinSrfLandedVAB*: KSC tour stop number 18 of 31: Welcome to the VAB. Somehow I think we have been here before.
KerbinSrfLandedVABMainBuilding*: KSC tour stop number 19 of 31: Welcome to the VAB Main Building.
KerbinSrfLandedVABPodMemorial*: KSC tour stop number 20 of 31: Welcome to the VAB Pod Memorial.
KerbinSrfLandedVABRoundTank*: KSC tour stop number 21 of 31: Welcome to the VAB Round Tank.
KerbinSrfLandedVABSouthComplex*: You scratch at the layer of dust that has built up on the building, added to by every failed launch's smoke cloud.
KerbinSrfLandedVABTanks*: KSC tour stop number 22 of 31: Welcome to the VAB Tanks.
KerbinSrfLandedIslandAirfield*: The surface is uneven and full of concrete chunks. You watch your step, lest you bite the dust.
KerbinSrfLandedWoomerang*: You wander around the steel frame, and see all there is to see.
KerbinFlyingLowKSC*: You are really just showboating for the guys at KSC, but isn't that what huge rockets are for??
KerbinFlyingHighKSC*: You wonder if the guys back at KSC can see you out the window, and not just on their comms display. You wave just in case.

MunSrfLanded: That's one small step fo- the situation is nominal.
That's one small step for a Kerbal, and one big cupboard of snacks for said Kerbal.
You notice some weird rocks in the distance.
You're in a most peculiar way, and the stars look very different today.
A small rock cracks your visor. You begin to question EVA on a surface with no atmosphere.
MunSrfLandedMidlands: As you step off your lander, saying the usual dramatic speech, Mission Control calls up, tells you to cut the chatter and to take the all-important surface sample.
You look up and hope that the rocket scientists back on Kerbin did their math correctly.
MunSrfLandedNorthernBasin: "Did I leave the keys in the ignition?"
MunSrfLandedEastCrater: You feel a peculiar quake. When you look behind you, there is a fresh meteor impacted where you were just walking.
MunSrfLandedNorthwestCrater: You should have brought your golf club.
MunSrfLandedSouthwestCrater: That dust is getting into everything! Now stop staring and help me clean my visor!
MunSrfLandedFarsideCrater: You wonder how that rock got into your spacesuit.
MunSrfLandedCanyons: You spot a narrow part of the canyon, and wonder if you could make the jump across.

You hold your hand up against Kerbin, and use your thumb to blot out the sun. After thinking briefly about pale blue dots, the puniness of kerbalkind, and philosophy, you go back to wondering how sweet it would be to ramp off those dunes back there.
MunSrfLandedPolarCrater: Feeling slightly bored, you play peek-a-boo with Kerbin over the crater edges.
MunSrfLandedPoles: You hum "You never make me be like you/I'm master of the moon."
You are amazed at how mystifying the moon scenery looks like from the pole.
MunSrfLandedPolarLowlands: You confirm that the Mun is not made out of cheese. Mission Control is very sad.
MunSrfLandedHighlands: As it turns out, there's no cheese!
MunSrfLandedHighlandCraters: It doesn't -taste- like it's made of dairy...
MunSrfLandedMidlandCraters: Yep, it's a crater all right. You've noted this down on your checklist with a smiley face. Science is fun!
MunSrfLandedEastFarsideCrater: There was something you were going to say, and even though you spent the entire trip over here perfecting your delivery, you've somehow managed to forget it.
