MunSrfLandedTwinCraters: You really want to jump again, but last time Mission Control yelled at you.
MunInSpaceLow: "I wonder if it tastes like cheese? Nah. Minmus though..."
MunInSpaceLowMidlands: Despite being relatively flat, the Midlands is still littered with debris from impact ejecta, making choosing a landing site a non-trivial matter.
MunInSpaceLowNorthernBasin: As you study the Northern Basin from low-orbit EVA you suddenly feel the an overwhelming urge to pee...
MunInSpaceLowEastCrater: You stare at the Mun amazed at how fast you are moving over the surface; maybe going out of the capsule was a bad decision.
It's hard to tell from outside the cockpit, but you think that you might be really low. You're not... in the crater, are you?
You Stare at the Mun amazed at how fast you are moving over the surface; maybe going out of the capsule was a bad decision.
MunInSpaceLowNorthwestCrater: Hmm... this Northwest Crater doesn't appear to be as round as some of the other ones. Perhaps the lava cooled unevenly. Or something.
MunInSpaceLowSouthwestCrater: The Southwest Crater is really rather round. The impact must have hit almost perpendicular to the surface.
MunInSpaceLowFarsideCrater: Hmm not bad. Still, you'd like to see the West Nearside Crater before making the final decision on which crater is the fairest of them all.
MunInSpaceLowCanyons: There are visible striations in the walls of the canyons.
The canyons below look particularly fun to climb. If only you weren't moving t such a high relative velocity...
MunInSpaceLowPolarCrater: You wish you could've witnessed the devastating impact that created the Mun's vast Polar Crater. From a safe distance of course.
MunInSpaceLowPoles: Aw, the Mun's Poles don't have auroras... Oh hey, you can see Kerbin's from here!
MunInSpaceLowPolarLowlands: If this was Kerbin, you'd be seeing ice sheets by now. Oh well, EVAs are still fun.
MunInSpaceLowHighlands: The scientists have explained that the munar highlands are likely plagioclase cumulates. You're not quite sure what that means.
The Mun's Highlands look alright. But you still wish there was some kind of munar mountain!
MunInSpaceLowHighlandCraters: After you complete your allotted activities, you let go of the ship and float freely over the Mun's Highland Craters.
MunInSpaceLowMidlandCraters: Holy crap there's a lot of them... which means there's plenty to go around when they start naming things after people!
MunInSpaceLowEastFarsideCrater: You're not a fan of the name "East Farside Crater". You really hope you land down there so they can name it after you instead.
You wish you brought a skateboard. Those crater walls would make an awesome halfpipe.
MunInSpaceLowTwinCraters: What an oddly-shaped crater. Are they two different impacts? Or perhaps the result of an impactor hitting at an angle and skipping across the surface.
MunInSpaceHigh: Say are those the Munar Canyons down below? They sure are impressive. Nothing like Kerbin's of course.
It looks like a really big rock.
All you can think about is potatoes...
You're trying to decide how to describe this moment to the folks back home. You probably shouldn't mention the part where you peed inside your suit.
You can see the ridge of the crater below much more clearly through just the visor of your spacesuit. Why did we bring a spaceship anyway?
You gave up counting the craters when you ran out of fingers for the ninth time.
You stare through the not-so-vast expanse between you and the Mun. Who knew it was this big.
Ahhhh. No one can hear you rip one loose way out here.
Entering the dark side of the Mun and losing contact with Mission Control makes you realize just how isolated you are from the rest of your kind.
Well there she is. You should probably rehearse your awesome, historic speech before you land.
You wonder if there's someone back on Kerbin watching the Mun through a telescope right now. You wave a few times in case they can see you.

Mission Control insists on guiding you step-by-step through this spacewalk. As a small act of rebellion, you do everything upside-down. They'll never know! Even you can't tell if you're really upside-down or not.
As you carry out your EVA you feel your nose get progressively itchier. The resulting sneeze almost spins you out of control.
Huh the Mun looks surprisingly like the one in your high school textbook. Probably the end result of hundreds of years of Kerbal curiosity. And telescopes. Really big telescopes.
You hear a sudden hissing from your space suit. Thankfully, it turns out it was just the snacks from last night.
You roam around the outside of your craft, marvelling at how such a contraption managed to get you this far.
You inspect your craft from the outside, going through the maintenance checklist provided by Mission Control.
As you carry out your EVA, you look over your spacecraft to make sure you're not missing too many pieces.
You could get used to peeing in this suit. Perhaps you should wear it all the time like a badass diaper.

MinmusSrfLanded*: You have confirmed that the surface does not taste like mint ice cream.
The powdery substance on the surface gets kicked up with every step, you swat at it as it pelts your visor.
You order the engineers to start building a "wicked fast rover" to race on the Flats at once.
MinmusSrfLandedHighlands*: It is hard to control your movements in the low gravity.
The ground beneath you has the perfect consistency for snowballs. Sadly, you cannot find a worthy target.
MinmusSrfLandedMidlands*: You spend several minutes contemplating how to taste the ground without removing your space suit's helmet before giving up.
MinmusSrfLandedLowlands*: You look up at the highlands and wonder out loud whether you could jump over them. Mission Control advises you not to try.
MinmusSrfLandedFlats*: As far as you can tell in the low gravity, the ground is firm underfoot, and not as slippery as you had expected.
MinmusSrfLandedGreatFlats*: You think you could drill some holes here and play golf... space golf, of course.
MinmusSrfLandedGreaterFlats*: As you exit your craft, you utter the words; "It's one small step for Kerbals, one giant leap for Kerbalkind."
MinmusSrfLandedLesserFlats*: Engine's exhaust seems to have melted the ice underneath.
MinmusSrfLandedPoles*: You can feel the planet spinning beneath you. Suddenly, you feel a little green around the gills.
MinmusSrfLandedSlopes*: You wonder how far you could slide down this hill...
You start to jury-rig a sled from spare parts, only to be interrupted by orders from Mission Control.
You could have sworn you saw a little boy and a tiger sledding down the hill in a toboggan. You check your O2 supply... Ah! Just hypoxia.
MinmusInSpaceLow*: The mesmerizing aquamarine flats almost make you forget to keep one hand on the ship's handle at all times.
MinmusInSpaceLowHighlands*: The surface seems to be a mixture of rock and ice... you wonder if it could be mined for fuel.
MinmusInSpaceLowLowlands*: You are craving mint ice cream right about now.
MinmusInSpaceLowFlats*: These ice lakes are so flat and smooth, you suspect they would make an excellent drag strip.
MinmusInSpaceLowGreatFlats*: If the spaceship fails, you could probably use the jetpack to get back into orbit.
MinmusInSpaceLowGreaterFlats*: You need to do something about the lack of snacks here.
MinmusInSpaceLowLesserFlats*: The low gravity would make driving around difficult.
MinmusInSpaceHigh*: It looks so small, and yet so delicious.

DunaSrfLanded: You see red. A LOT of red.
The air is a little thin up here. Down here?
Would this be a good place to have giant sand worms for pets?
A gust of wind creates a small red dust storm, it's hard to see.
DunaSrfLandedPoles: It feels as though a stiff breeze is tugging at your ankles. You suspect that this means gas is escaping from the ice caps, and this makes you slightly nervous.
A sudden puff of gas makes you jump back from the geyser hole you were peering into.
DunaSrfLandedMidlands: I'm hungry. Where's my pizza? What do you mean, you have a 37,000-Fund charge for Duna doorstep delivery? 300 days or it's free!
It looks boring out here. You flip your sun visor down and up again as if it might make it more interesting.
Duna's low gravity would lend itself well to an exciting game of basketball! You consider adding a hoop to your next lander design.
DunaSrfLandedLowlands: You feel like a true hero as you march across the red planet, until you realize you're a long, long way from home.

The sand here is much too fine to make a sandcastle with so you decide to bound around for a bit. Mission Control orders you back to your tasks-you've come all this way, why can't you just have a few minutes of FUN!?
DunaSrfLandedCraters: You've proven the alien conspiracy theorists wrong. There are no tall, pink men anywhere to be seen.
You can't see the edges of the crater from the ground. Are you sure this is even a crater?
DunaSrfLandedHighlands: There's no wind. The slight force that buffets you around everywhere else is unnervingly absent.
Hills, schmills, I'm on top of the world up here! I can see forever!
DunaSrfLandedMidlandSea: You look up at Ike overhead, and it reminds you of Dres. It's just as dull, too.
You trudge on through the fine red sand, dust cascading off your boots with every step.
DunaSrfLandedNorthernBasin: You can't see the other side of the basin. Whatever sort of basin it is, it's massive!
Your suit does a pretty good job of keeping you warm in the cold, but your toes are getting a bit chilly.
DunaSrfLandedSouthernBasin: The ground is slushy underfoot, as though it can't make its mind up over whether it wants to be ice or rock.
A squall of freezing wind blows off the ice cap, briefly making you very cold indeed.
DunaSrfLandedNorthernShelf: A blast of howling wind almost blows you off your feet as sand clatters against your visor plate.
The even-more-freezing-than-usual wind bites into your toes. Where could it possibly be coming from?

The sand grains flying past swirl and rub against each other as an even more huge dust storm front bears down on you from the north. The air around you crackles with static electricity that makes your hair stand up.
DunaSrfLandedMidlandCanyon: On one side you can see rock face, on the other you can see the vast expanse of the Midland Sea.
You look up at the walls, but lose your balance and tip over, landing on your back in the dust.
DunaSrfLandedEasternCanyon: You want to stand on a rock and strike a dramatic pose, but you can't find a big enough one in the thick haze.
Dust-that's all you can see. You daren't wander too far from the ship in case you can't find it again.
DunaSrfLandedWesternCanyon: You look down one end of the canyon, and then look down the other end. It's very disorienting, and you'd hate to get lost here.
You pull out a pair of binoculars and look at the wall just in time to see a huge boulder fall from the precipice and crash to the canyon floor. The sound doesn't make it to your position.
DunaSrfLandedPolarHighlands: From this elevated vantage point you can see the dazzling pinkish hills stretching into the distance.
You look back at your ship and see large amounts of vapour hissing from under it. You hope it isn't about to sink into the ice.
DunaSrfLandedPolarCraters: You step onto the ice and immediately almost slip over. Some things never change about ice.
Thankfully, the suits have climate control. You try and stop yourself thinking about you freezing your ass off without it.
DunaFlyingLowPoles: You see a far-off gas plume eject a large chunk of ice into the thin air. You consider jumping into one to see how high you can get.
Everywhere you look, you see white. The undulating icy plains stretch toward the steeply curving horizon.
DunaFlyingLowMidlands: For a moment, you think you catch a glimpse of a small, shiny, black-topped object in the distance. Then it's gone.
Up here, the dust seems a lot thinner. Until you see a dust storm rolling in off the lowlands from your vantage point.
DunaFlyingLowLowlands: The featureless plains stretch off into the distance. It's too cold here for any mirages.
Even off the ground, the air is strangely calm. It's still really cold though, and that snaps you out of your feeling of unease.
DunaFlyingLowCraters: OK, from up here you can see the edges of this vast bowl in the landscape. You feel small.
Even down here the thin Dunaian atmosphere is too tenuous to slow you down much.
DunaFlyingLowHighlands: There's not enough atmosphere to slow you down much up here, you'll have to use your jets unless you like faceplanting.
The dust seems to be dyeing your suit and you're sure you'll be the laughing stock of the whole KSC when you return in pink.
DunaFlyingLowMidlandSea: Airborne sand grains skitter off your visor glass, creating an almost hypnotic hissing noise.
Despite moving at some speed through the air, you don't feel any kind of substantial wind resistance. The atmosphere must be extremely thin.
DunaFlyingLowNorthernBasin: The wind tries its best to buffet you around, but you manage to stay steady and get a look around from a slightly higher vantage point.
The wind blowing around you is cold. Like, really cold. You long for the warmth of your seat on the ship.
DunaFlyingLowSouthernBasin: You distract yourself from the effects of the rushing cold air by imagining sitting back in the Astronaut Complex breakroom with a cup of warm koffee and a biscuit.
It's even colder than usual up here at these high latitudes, and the wind chill from flying makes it even colder. You hope the airheads at the AC fixed your suit heater before you left.
DunaFlyingLowNorthernShelf: You struggle to maintain your aerial position in the winds and consider tying yourself to the craft.
There is an eerie lull in the weather, and for a few seconds the dust clears enough for you to marvel at the canyon's vast scale.
DunaFlyingLowMidlandCanyon: The sun is shining, but it's still cold here. Cold enough to make you wish you had brought some hot koffee with you.
You're in the air but thanks to the looming walls, you don't feel like you're getting any higher or lower.
DunaFlyingLowEasternCanyon: The dust is still thick, but does thin out a little when off the surface. Still can't see much, though.
You look down, and try to make out patterns in the swirling dust. That's a... swirly thing. That's another swirly thing.
DunaFlyingLowWesternCanyon: From up here you can see both walls. Quite the narrow canyon indeed; perfect for some high-speed hovering-craft racing!
The straightness of the walls you can see makes your eyes start to water.
DunaFlyingLowPolarHighlands: It looks much the same from up here as it does down there. Hilly and white.
The peaks are covered in ice, but as far as you can tell there are no glaciers to be found.
DunaFlyingLowPolarCraters: From the air, you can clearly see the shape of the icy depression. Quite a large hole indeed.
The hope you had of being able to have a snowball fight fade as you see it's all ice down there.
DunaFlyingHigh: Luckily the Dunaian atmosphere is very thin. Otherwise you and your craft would get separated very quickly pulling a stunt like this.
Even though the drag force is very low, you feel like you should really get back in.
The atmosphere is thin at best at the surface, and all the way up here you barely feel any aero forces or drag.
DunaInSpaceLowPoles: The curving edge of the planet shows just a hint of red surrounding a sea of white ice below you. It's beautiful!
The mountain chains are just visible as a series of wiggly lines stretching over the sea of white.
DunaInSpaceLowMidlands: You mentally trace the lines of the ancient valleys in the landscape. Former rivers, maybe?
The squiggly patterns in the land below are so mesmerising it takes a snappy comment from Mission Control to bring you back.
DunaInSpaceLowLowlands: It looks boring down there. Even the Midlands might be a more interesting place to land.
A dust storm front moves lazily across the flatlands, obscuring the surface behind it.
DunaInSpaceLowCraters: Even from here you can see the outline of the craters. You feel glad Kerbin only has one of these great holes.
You try to get a good look, but a glint of sunlight off the ice caps has you attempting to rub your eyes through your visor.
DunaInSpaceLowHighlands: Them's some big mountains. You wonder if there's gold in them there hills too.
It's difficult to tell where the Highlands end and the Midlands begin. All of Duna looks the same after a while.
DunaInSpaceLowMidlandSea: You look down, and try to imagine the Midland Sea when it was still full of water. It's hard, given the state of the place now.
The surface in the depression looks darker and the edges are clearly visible, especially in the great semicircular scar in the north.
DunaInSpaceLowNorthernBasin: From here, you can see the basin, with the huge rebound peak in the centre giving it away as a crater. It's enormous!
The huge basin below looks like a doughnut, to compliment the koffee stain canyon to the east.
DunaInSpaceLowSouthernBasin: It's easy to track the edges of the basin on the land, but where it disappears under the ice it's hard to make them out.
The depression is just about visible running under the ice as a darkened patch of lower elevation
DunaInSpaceLowNorthernShelf: That's a big scar. Duna must have been in a serious fight at some point.
The waves of airborne sand blowing through the canyon below form beautiful patterns over the surface.
DunaInSpaceLowMidlandCanyon: You trace the outline of the canyon in front of you. It reminds you of a koffee stain.
It looks like someone had their koffee cup on the surface or tried to saw through the crust. Either way, it's impressive.
DunaInSpaceLowEasternCanyon: This canyon is by far the biggest of Duna's great scars, slicing through plains and hills alike.
If you tilt your head sideways, you could swear the canyon looks like a crooked smiley face. What do you mean, 'you're seeing things'?
DunaInSpaceLowWesternCanyon: The great scar in the landscape below links two of the biggest basins on Duna like some kind of great cutting.
Zooming in, you see half a crater on the edge of the canyon. Was it made by half a meteorite, or has the rest fallen into the canyon as it widened?
DunaInSpaceLowPolarHighlands: The icy hills glint in the sunlight far below you. You wonder if there are any UFOs buried down there.
DunaInSpaceLowPolarCraters: It's white, and it looks cold down there. If you're going down there, you should probably go and search for some warm gloves.
The crater appears as a dark dot set against the slightly pinkish whiteness of the polar ice cap.
DunaInSpaceHigh: No matter which way you look, that big, red eye is always reflected in the corner of your visor.
You stare at the planet far beneath you, trying to work out which place would be the most suitable to narrowly avoid exploding on.
Duna's colours encompass white, red, orange and brown. Definitely needs more blue and green.

DresSrfLanded*: You feel like a few smaller rocks should be visible in the distance. However, when you look up, you spot nothing of the sort.
DresSrfLandedLowlands*: This is a very low land.
DresSrfLandedImpactEjecta*: "It appears that an asteroid has hit very hard here. Where could all of them be coming from?"
DresSrfLandedCanyons*: For a planet of this size and gravity, there are an awful lot of craters.
DresInSpaceLowImpactEjecta*: For a minute you think that the burn marks below are the ones of your ship...
DresInSpaceHigh*: It's an airless rock floating through space, all alone and lonely. Sniff.
"If I don't get back in soon, there will be green splotches down there..."

JoolFlyingLow*: I think I just heard something crack in my helmet. Can I go home?
You are advised to get back into the capsule, fast.
I should have taken a green space suit for camouflage.
You feel a sudden urge to see what would happen if you landed on a gas giant.
HOW DID I LET YOU GUYS TALK ME INTO THIS!?
You realize that diving into a gas giant never sounded like a good idea.
Kerbin, I think we have a problem. I do, anyways...
As you wing-walk amongst the green clouds, you suddenly wonder how you will get home now.
Your life flashes before your eyes. You realize it's best you leave Jool as soon as possible.
JoolFlyingHigh*: You feel your skin burn as you leave the vessel. It would be a good idea to get back on board!
You find that the outside pressures are equal with your suit's interior pressure. Removing your helmet would still be a rather poor idea.
Feeling insignificant near the towering mass of the gas giant, you are inclined to think that size matters after all.
You want to get back in as soon as possible.
JoolInSpaceLow*: All you can see is a rather large green gas giant.
You see Jool sparkling below you like an endless ocean of green.
Your thoughts are interrupted by the incessant beeping of the radiation alarm, so you turn it off and enjoy the peace and quiet.
You look around and think you just saw Kerbin.
JoolInSpaceHigh*: You look at the green planet's many moons and feel that somewhere, something incredible is waiting to be tasted.
You feel happy that the biggest planet is green.
Gazing down at Jool, you wonder where the ground is.

LaytheSrfLanded: You feel a slight tremor. Perhaps you should have landed on that OTHER island.

You try to open your helmet's protective glass ever so slightly. The air is rough, humid and thin. You gasp for oxygen but there is not much to be found. Maybe Kerbals with more high-altitude experience would do just fine.
You do a series of breathing exercises and open your helmet to try Laythian air. It feels fine but gives you a strong sense of intoxication. You decide it's because of lower oxygen content.
After determining that the air is, if only just, breathable, you ponder whether turbojet engines would be able to run here.
You wonder for a moment whether it would be safe or not to open the seal on your helmet. After a moment of consideration, you decide to play it safe and leave the helmet on.
LaytheSrfLandedPoles: Can I take off my spacesuit?
LaytheSrfLandedShores: Looking beyond the horizon, you conclude that this is a perfect place to set up a base for.
You are glad to see the solid ground, but it seems that after such a long journey you almost forgot how to walk.
This would make a great beach resort!
LaytheSrfLandedDunes: You wonder if you could swim to the next island from here.
LaytheSrfLandedCrescentBay: You feel drunk because of the thin air. Mission Control asks you to put your helmet back on.
LaytheSrfLandedTheSagenSea: There might be good diving on this planet. Unfortunately, Mission Control did not pack any flippers.
LaytheSrfSplashed: You wonder if you could swim to the nearest island.
You realize that this is the first bath you have had since leaving Kerbin.
You start thinking of how cool it would be to have motorboats here. You then proceed to make motorboat sounds.
You attempt to drink some water. Then you remember the co-worker who died after ordering some "H2O too".
You tread water. Perhaps you should have chosen a drier landing spot.
Swimming is really hard in these suits you know.
LaytheSrfSplashedCrescentBay: The low land to water ratio and sedate water cycle of Laythe combine to give its oceans low salinity. You're forced to constantly swim to keep afloat.
LaytheSrfSplashedTheSagenSea: You begin to wish you'd brought a boat.
You wonder what lies beneath this liquid, is this water?
LaytheFlyingLow: Now I really wish I had brought that parachute.
LaytheFlyingLowPoles: You realize that you forgot to bring sunscreen.
LaytheFlyingLowShores: You are sorely tempted to remove your helmet.
LaytheFlyingLowDunes: You feel comfortable, back at Kerbin after such a long sleep. Wait a minute...
LaytheFlyingLowCrescentBay: You find that changes in your environment oddly turn your thoughts rapidly from desserts towards life insurances.
LaytheFlyingLowTheSagenSea: It is much colder here than the scan from orbit suggested.
LaytheFlyingHigh: As you repair one of the heat shielding tiles that fell off, you are reminded of your first return through Kerbin's' atmosphere.
LaytheInSpaceLow: As you gaze down upon the flowing waters of Laythe, you discover that you need a toilet.
LaytheInSpaceLowPoles: You search the sky, hoping to see Jool.
LaytheInSpaceLowShores: The air looks tolerable and the water looks very inviting. A beautiful place to be!
LaytheInSpaceLowDunes: Your Geiger Counter is emitting a continuous hum. Time to flee.
LaytheInSpaceLowCrescentBay: Gazing around the landscape, you feel very homesick.
LaytheInSpaceLowTheSagenSea: As much as you'd like to find life, the only green wiggling creature here is in a spacesuit.
LaytheInSpaceHigh: You almost feel like you are floating above Kerbin.

VallSrfLanded*: The ground rumbles ominously beneath you before you realize that you're just hungry.
You wish you'd brought your ice skates.
Ice melts down and evaporates from the friction of your space boots. You concentrate very hard on keeping your balance.
VallSrfLandedPoles*: The rocks are of a bluish-green color... Pretty!
VallSrfLandedMidlands*: The surface shivers as the deeper crust buckles and fractures. You want badly to get back inside the ship now.
VallSrfLandedHighlands*: The ground beneath you suddenly lurches, and you hear the sound, carried up through your feet, of immense ice plates scraping against each other.
VallSrfLandedLowlands*: Well, the ice is... thick. You wonder if there's a frozen waterfall somewhere.
VallInSpaceLow*: You look at all the valleys and wonder what made them that way.
VallInSpaceLowHighlands*: Closer investigation of the planet makes you think of Minmus. You are once again hungry for mint dessert.
VallInSpaceHigh*: You see a sparkling stream of crystals blast into the sky in the distance.

TyloSrfLanded: They say the gravity of Tylo adds five pounds.
TyloSrfLandedMidlands: If surface composition is of any indication, there is a distinct possibility of finding underground lakes and caverns somewhere around.
TyloSrfLandedHighlands: Is it me, or did my spacesuit just get really heavy?
TyloSrfLandedLowlands: You try jumping... It isn't very effective
TyloSrfLandedMara: Terrain looks like a mix of rocks, ice and dirt. You wonder at the lack of atmosphere.
TyloInSpaceLow: You swear you just found Tylo's missing atmosphere! It's just too bad it floated away before you could grab it.

BopSrfLanded: You feel uneasy. This place is dark and inhospitable.
You feel that something is wrong. Even more so than when you were in your spacecraft. You feel like there's something behind you, but when you turn around, nothing is there.
BopSrfLandedPeaks: The low gravity is counterbalanced by a strong attraction to Jool. It is very green.
Mission Control tells you not to take off your helmet.
BopSrfLandedRidges: You note that it seems boring here, and wish you'd been allowed to bring the beach ball you wanted to.
BopSrfLandedPoles: There's something ominous lurking here... you just know it.
BopSrfLandedSlopes: The air feels murky and ominous. A second later, you remember there is no air on Bop, so it's probably just your suit being out of order.
BopSrfLandedValley: You look around at the tops of the round lumps around you and think you see a small flash in the distance. You put down "weird" in the mission report.
BopInSpaceLowPeaks: I wish that we were not here.
I can see the ship from up here!
BopInSpaceHigh: The moon strikingly resembles a potato.
Mission Control tells you that Bop is that little brown thing. You had gone on EVA to move it away from the ship.
Bop looks disturbingly like Gilly. You hope you will be able to make it home this time.

PolSrfLanded: You worry that you'll never come back down if you jump.
This place might be very weird, but it's not as bad as it could be.
PolSrfLandedPoles: You make a little sandcastle, then try to blow over. Wait, there's no atmosphere.
You chuckle when you realize that if you plant a flag, there'll be a pole on Pol's pole.
PolSrfLandedLowlands: Every edge looks dangerously sharp. You step carefully, trying not to shatter anything.
PolSrfLandedHighlands: Looking at all the jagged rocks, you feel very thankful about not landing on them.
PolSrfLandedMidlands: The rocks, the sand, and space itself seem to be filled with glitter. It gives you a headache.
PolInSpaceLow: While outside the capsule, it is easier to see that Pol's surface is incredibly uneven. It also has an uncanny resemblance to a grain of pollen.
PolInSpaceLowHighlands: Mission Control tells you to immediately begin saving water. You ask why, but your suit anttena breaks off in the wind. Wait, hold on...

EelooSrfLanded*: As you think about the distance and time it took to get you here, a sudden shiver runs down your spine.
You feel like Eeloo has been alone for a long time. You contemplate staying a little while longer.
It is very cold. You realize that you probably should have brought a sweater.
You want to build a snow-Kerbal. Sadly, Eeloo is made of ice, not snow.
You wonder what would come of an ice-carving competition held here.
You suddenly realize that there's no way you'll be able to walk home from here.
EelooSrfLandedPoles*: Even though the suit is well insulated, you can feel the cold seeping into your feet.
EelooSrfLandedNorthernGlaciers*: You get the feeling you left your oven on back home.
EelooSrfLandedLowlands*: You lie down and make a snow Kerbal.
EelooSrfLandedHighlands*: You're standing on a giant dirty snowball.
EelooSrfLandedIceCanyons*: You step off the capsule to the crackling of ice under your boots. Your eyes adjust to the faint light. From way out here, Kerbol is just another star. You reflect upon the several year journey it took to get here.
EelooSrfLandedCraters*: You look at the Sun, and feel very, very lonely...
EelooSrfLandedMidlands*: You pat the ground. Hopefully you cheered up the planet a little.
EelooInSpaceLow*: It's cold this far from the sun. How long until you're warm again?
EelooInSpaceLowPoles*: Even through the padding of your spacesuit, there's a distinct chill out here.
EelooInSpaceLowNorthernGlaciers*: You wonder what made you believe all the cheery people that assured you time and again that you were coming back.
EelooInSpaceLowLowlands*: The sun looks so distant now, compared to home...
EelooInSpaceLowHighlands*: As you look at the ground, you think a snowball fight might be fun.
EelooInSpaceLowIceCanyons*: You discover it is very cold. You ask Mission Control if you can get back into your ship now.
EelooInSpaceLowCraters*: You start a pun about saying "Eeloo," but then Mission Control comes back from the bathroom.
EelooInSpaceLowMidlands*: Even with spacesuit protecting you from the cold of space you feel yourself freezing... and lonely.
EelooInSpaceHigh*: It looks cold and lonely. You feel a sudden outburst of sympathy.

evaScience.description: A quick EVA science experiment prepared and performed by a Kerbal outside the vessel. The generated result is only the introductory line: the game will automatically append a second, animation-specific follow-up text after this
evaScience.hints: Write exactly ONE short sentence in past tense describing the (successful) preparation/execution of the EVA experiment, framed by how favorable or harsh the current body + situation conditions are (landed//space low/space high), with a touch of irony and/or mild surprise. Do not mention the specific animation item or add a second sentence.

gravityScan.description: Measurement of local gravitational variations using an accelerometer, also referred as 'Gravioli detector'.
gravityScan.hints: Comments on subtle changes in gravity that hint at mass concentrations, voids, or uneven internal structure; may contrast expected gravity with what the instrument actually records.

SunInSpaceLow: The gravitational pull is very high. Maybe that's why the planets don't fly away?
The manufacturer's manual for this instrument has classified readings in this range as "very unwise". There is a somewhat unhelpful footnote that the warranty does not cover melting.
The readings are so far off the scale that the dial loops back around to 1.
SunInSpaceHigh: You look at the small measurement, and feel slightly relieved. Perhaps you won't be incinerated today.
You stare at the reading. And stare. And stare.
The sensor monitors the orbital dynamics of the planets.

MohoSrfLanded*: Moho's gravity and density implies that a metal-rich core occupies at least 60% of the planet's mass, a figure twice as great as for Kerbin!
The gravitational pull of this planet is high for one of its size. The core must be extremely dense.
MohoSrfLandedNorthPole*: Detailed scans hint at hollow space in the interior of the planet.
MohoSrfLandedNorthernSinkholeRidge*: You notice the gravity scans have been saying 666 over and over again.
MohoSrfLandedNorthernSinkhole*: You notice the gravity scans have been saying 666 over and over again.
MohoSrfLandedMidlands*: The gravity beneath the instrument shifts almost imperceptibly.
MohoSrfLandedWesternLowlands*: The gravimeter confirms that the lowlands are large.
MohoSrfLandedCentralLowlands*: The gravimeter confirms that the lowlands are large.
MohoSrfLandedMinorCraters*: The sensor is giving odd readings here.
MohoSrfLandedSouthWesternLowlands*: The gravimeter confirms that the lowlands are large.
MohoSrfLandedSouthEasternLowlands*: The gravimeter confirms that the lowlands are large.
MohoSrfLandedCanyon*: The gravity is quite consistent here.
MohoSrfLandedSouthPole*: Detailed scans hint at hollow spaces in the interior of the planet.

EveSrfLandedPoles: The sensor's overload warning is on. It appears Eve's gravity is almost too much.
"Warranty void if exposed to more than 0g". Better not break it then.
EveSrfLandedMidlands: Kerbals are short enough already in 1g, so you wonder how short a 1.7g heavyworlder would be.
The gravimeter mounting bracket bends alarmingly under the force.
EveSrfLandedExplodiumSea: You feel glad there are no tides. Just the presence of the liquid in that sea is bad enough, and that's without it chasing after you.
EveSrfLandedLowlands: The gravity here makes you feel really fat, while at the same time, you look quite skinny.
Together with the thick atmosphere, this gravity will seriously hamper efforts to take off.
EveSrfLandedHighlands: The gravity of Eve strongly supports your personal "Planet Core is a Black Hole" theory.
You knew it! You didn't put on weight, Eve put the weight on you!
EveSrfLandedPeaks: Given the wealth of data to go on, the gravimeter finishes its scan in record time.
Comparison to the Eve average and expected values allows the determining of the mountains' missing mass anomaly.
EveSrfLandedImpactEjecta: The very high gravity of Eve means the meteorite that struck here must have done so with great speed.
You wait for the gravelly ejecta to stop shifting under the ship before measuring the gravity.
EveSrfLandedCraterLake: Holy gravioli, that's a lot of them!
EveSrfLandedWesternSea: Something about the gravity of the sea is seriously off, but you can't put your finger on it.
EveSrfLandedOlympus: The free-air gravity anomaly is enormous. The roots on these mountains must be like a huge geological tree trunk.
The position from the top of the world gives detailed insight into Eve's crustal structure.
EveSrfLandedEasternSea: Just looking at the readout makes you feel heavier.
EveSrfLandedFoothills: Nothing out of the Eve ordinary, aside from some pull from the nearby "adult" mountains.
The weight presses down on the gravimeter. You could swear it likes it.
EveSrfLandedShallows: So heavy... its weight level must be over 9000!
EveInSpaceLowPoles: Gravity ticks up and down as you pass over the varied polar surface.
EveInSpaceLowMidlands: Graviolis swirl around as they go up and down, in and out of Eve.
Small nicks in gravity betray the presence of many buried, eroded and long forgotten craters from Eve's ancient past.
EveInSpaceLowExplodiumSea: Sudden spikes and dips in gravity reveal prominent continental shelves deep under the surface of the seas.
Eve has no major moon, so the oceans do not experience tides.
EveInSpaceLowLowlands: Super gravity versus super atmosphere. What would win?
With the reading complete, you're one step closer to working out why the gravity is so darn high.
EveInSpaceLowHighlands: The high gravity will make even short falls hazardous. Kerbals should be careful here.
Now close to Eve, the equipment gets its first tastes of the punishing Eve gravity.
EveInSpaceLowPeaks: Gravimetric data from the peaks is analysed from multiple angles to generate a subsurface map of the mountains.
The numerous mountain peaks each have a distinct gravitational signature.
EveInSpaceLowImpactEjecta: The lip of the craters provides a distinctive shape to the gravity line.
You hope the high gravity doesn't cause you to suffer the same fate as the impactor.
EveInSpaceLowCraterLake: The normal gravimetric traces of an impact basin are faint and truncated. Something deep down must be removing them.
That's a big hole. The gravimeter concurs.
EveInSpaceLowWesternSea: Unexpectedly, the Western Sea shows strong gravimetric signs associated with major continental rift basins.
EveInSpaceLowOlympus: The signals of the huge mountains dwarf all those around them.
The regional gravitational anomaly suggests the mountain roots extend down dozens of miles into Eve's mantle.
EveInSpaceLowEasternSea: The floor of the basin seems to have very little underwater topography aside from a handful of islands.
EveInSpaceLowFoothills: The gravity looks uncomfortably high. You'd rather stay up here, thanks.
The proximity of large nearby peaks and craters makes it difficult to isolate the signatures of the individual landforms.
EveInSpaceLowShallows: The shallower portions of the oceans don't have a lot going for them gravimetrically.
You hope the liquid down there is dense so you don't sink into it. Someone tells you buoyancy doesn't work like that.
EveInSpaceHighPoles: The varied polar landscape outputs a highly interesting and colourful gravimetric map.
EveInSpaceHighMidlands: Zzzzz. Wake me up when something interesting comes up.
EveInSpaceHighExplodiumSea: The sensor spikes every time you orbit over the ocean.
EveInSpaceHighLowlands: The transition between land and sea is softened at this high altitude.
EveInSpaceHighHighlands: You don't understand how Eve can keep its rotational axis straight with all this lopsided mass.
EveInSpaceHighPeaks: The sensor says it found something, and beeps insistently at you.
EveInSpaceHighImpactEjecta: You dial up the sensitivity on the equipment to get a good scan of the crater.
EveInSpaceHighCraterLake: The signature of the massive crater is muted more than it should be from high altitude.
EveInSpaceHighWesternSea: Tantalising hints of a major positive gravitational anomaly start to appear.
EveInSpaceHighOlympus: Every needle swings to point at the huge rocky edifice on the distant surface.
EveInSpaceHighEasternSea: The gravimeter stops working. KSC tells you to switch it off and on again.
EveInSpaceHighFoothills: A piece of ship hull drifts past the sensor and registers on the super-sensitive equipment. Fascinating!
EveInSpaceHighShallows: You wonder if you could use the gravimeter to try and scan for creatures under the purple waves.

GillySrfLandedMidlands: Peering very closely at the dial, you notice there's indeed some gravity here.
You have to tap the sensor a few times before it realizes that gravity is, in fact, present.
GillySrfLandedLowlands: The needle on the gravity sensor seems to have moved about half a millimeter.
The instrument hardly recognizes the asteroid below.
GillySrfLandedHighlands: With Gilly's low gravity, you now question why Jeb wanted to put Mainsails on the lander.
The gravioli detector reports that there is NO gravity on this dwarf asteroid... wait, there's like 0.01 gravity units here.
GillyInSpaceLowMidlands: Are you sure the sensor's even working? The numbers are so small they don't register on the cheap LCD.
The gravimeter threatens to float away in the near-zero-g.
GillyInSpaceLowLowlands: The display reads "Error HL3". Upon consultation with the manual, you discover that this means the sensor can't find any gravity to scan.
The sensor outputs a beautifully detailed gravity map - of Eve.
GillyInSpaceLowHighlands: The gravity scanner can't decide which way is up.
The gravity scanner decides that the ship has more gravity than Gilly.
GillyInSpaceHighMidlands: The gravity is ridiculous. Playing ker-ball on Gilly might end up putting the ball in orbit.
GillyInSpaceHighLowlands: You manage to get an accurate reading despite the overpowering pull from Eve.
The sensor thinks the most gravity is coming from behind you.
GillyInSpaceHighHighlands: The readings are so low that the margin of error still touches zero.
If you squint at the data enough, you think you can see small variations in gravity from the lumpy peaks.

KerbinSrfLandedWater: The graviolis aren't too sure about this wet stuff, but they report that everything else seems in order.
The graviolis are soaking wet, but they report nominal gravity.
KerbinSrfLandedGrasslands: The graviolis seem strangely attracted to the grass. There aren't more of them, but they seem more content here.
The grass doesn't seem to be too bothered by gravity. Look at them, swaying in the wind and such.
Huh, the gravity here is exactly the same as at Jeb's house. Who'd have guessed.
KerbinSrfLandedHighlands: The graviolis seem very weak here. Maybe it has something to to with the high altitude.
The graviolis react differently as you pass over certain hills and valleys.
KerbinSrfLandedShores: The graviolis throw themselves a beach party. You're not invited.
You are distracted by all the swimsuit-clad Kerbals around you, but manage to take a reading of the local gravity and sign a few autographs as well.
KerbinSrfLandedMountains: On solid ground you are able to detect the faint anomalies in the gravitational field caused by the mountain you are standing on. The guys back at KSC will be excited!
You can tell that the gravity is still on by the fact that you're standing on a mountain instead of floating in outer space.
KerbinSrfLandedDeserts: You look around at some dried bones scattered on the ground, and feel the tug of Kerbin's gravitational field pulling you to the earth. Or maybe that is just your imagination. Time for some snacks!
You splash juice over the gravity scanner. It was probably pretty thirsty.
KerbinSrfLandedBadlands: The gravity here is just...gravity. The scientists said it would somehow be 'bad' gravity. Wish we were paid like them for being wrong, rather than exploding in a giant crashing fireball.
Yep, looks like the same gravity you find anywhere else on Kerbin. Seems that gravity isn't too impressed by the so-called 'Badlands'.
Gravity doesn't care that you're in the Badlands. Gravity knows how to treat everything right.
KerbinSrfLandedTundra: You wonder how much bigger the bears and wolves would be without this gravity. Thanks gravity!
The gravity here seems to be stunted, just like everything else in this bleak landscape.
KerbinSrfLandedIceCaps: The graviolis are all huddled together, and you can't get an accurate reading. Even THEY are freezing their butts off out here!
The scanner gives some interesting readings of the sub-surface. What secrets lie beneath the polar ice caps?
KerbinSrfLandedNorthernIceShelf: Gravity is lower compared to the land of the ice caps. There must be a serious lack of rock beneath your feet.
You hit upon the idea of using gravimetric analysis to map the bathymetry underneath the impenetrable pack ice.
KerbinSrfLandedSouthernIceShelf: The gravimeter abruptly stops working. On closer inspection, the heat of the circuitry melted some salty ice, shorting the very expensive equipment.
Gravitationally speaking, the southern ice sheet is as featureless as its surface.
KerbinSrfSplashed: The instrument starts sparking and shorts out before you can take a reading.
KerbinSrfSplashedWater: The gravity scan detects disturbances seemingly originating from far underneath the ocean's surface...
The sensor has detected changes in the local gravity that seem to be related to the passing of the Mun.
KerbinInSpaceLow: For some reason, the planet seems to be pulling you in. What a surprise!
KerbinInSpaceLowWater: As you activate the gravity scanner, you wonder what sort of effects the oceans might have on the reading.
The sensor shows a surprising variance as it passes over the oceans, hinting at what lies deep below the waters.
KerbinInSpaceLowGrasslands: You happily munch on a snack while performing a gravity scan over the Grasslands.
A scientist at KSC once told you that without gravity, the grass would grow into outer space! On second thought, he probably wasn't a real scientist.
The instrument surveys the gravity over the Grasslands. It appears the field is very stable here.
KerbinInSpaceLowHighlands: The instrument surveys the Highlands of Kerbin. The rolling hills seem to affect local gravity slightly.
The gravity signature of the Highlands is only slightly distinguishable from the plains around it. There must be a lot of missing mass below the surface.
KerbinInSpaceLowShores: As the gravity scan continues, your mind wanders to good memories you have of Kerbin's shores.
The sensor passes over the shores of Kerbin. The readings appear to be nominal.
KerbinInSpaceLowMountains: Despite the vast edifices below, the Kouguer reading changes little. The geophysicists can figure that one out.
The mountains below give off a suitably impressive gravitational signature.
KerbinInSpaceLowDeserts: The dunes appear to have a stable gravity, though some spikes indicate the underground terrain varies in composition.
Gravitational data from orbit reveals an incredibly complex network of subterranean formations beneath the deserts of Kerbin.
KerbinInSpaceLowBadlands: *singing* 'Get low, get low, get low, get low'. Oh yeah, gravity. Yeah, we got that.
The gravitational data is completely nominal, something you didn't expect passing over a place called the 'Badlands'.
Turns out Badlands gravity is just like regular gravity. Who knew?
KerbinInSpaceLowTundra: You and the graviolis huddle together for warmth.
The graviolis seem sluggish and unresponsive. You wonder if they need a snack as much as you do.
KerbinInSpaceLowIceCaps: The sensor detects variances under the surface of the ice. Is there a landmass underneath?
You complete your gravity scan over the Ice Caps. You eat a snack to celebrate. Then you eat another one because you like snacks.
KerbinInSpaceLowNorthernIceShelf: The sensor detects variances under the surface of the ice. Is there a landmass underneath?
You feel much more comfortable doing gravimetry from up here rather than down there, freezing your butt off.
KerbinInSpaceLowSouthernIceShelf: The gravitational signature moves only slightly as you pass over the southern ice cap. Looks like it's water all the way down.
Graviolis cascade down towards the surface, closely following magnetic field lines.
KerbinInSpaceHigh: The gravity seems to be incredibly high for a celestial body of this size. It must be composed of an extremely dense material.
KerbinInSpaceHighWater: Your gravity scan high over Kerbin's Water ends much like your other gravity scans. With a delicious snack.
The smooth stream of graviolis creates a strong reading. You send the report back to KSC for their analysis.
KerbinInSpaceHighGrasslands: Gravity scans over the Grasslands are normal...too normal. You begin to suspect that the grass is hiding something.
As you fly high over the Grasslands you can feel the graviolis swarming around you. They seem excited.
KerbinInSpaceHighHighlands: Even at this distance, the gravity scan picks up the distinct signature of the massive mountains below.
Gravity scans from high orbit are yielding some interesting tidbits about the Highlands. Speaking of tidbits, where did you put those snacks?
KerbinInSpaceHighShores: The gravitational signature changes rather significantly as you pass over the shores and over the ocean.
The gravity seems pretty normal here, though you can tell the graviolis wish they were at the beach.
KerbinInSpaceHighMountains: The massive mountain range below registers on your gravitational scanner as you fly by. Just another day in the life of an kerbonaut.
The graviolis are excited about the prospect of passing over the mountains of Kerbin.
KerbinInSpaceHighDeserts: Orbital gravity scans make the deserts far more interesting than they appear to be to the naked eye.
The graviolis here seem dry and dusty like the surface below. You consider offering them a glass of water, but have no idea how to speak gravioli.
KerbinInSpaceHighBadlands: The sensor gives a very negative readout as you pass over the badlands.
You somehow get the feeling that the gravitational sensor disapproves of what it's seeing.
The gravity scanner seems surlier than usual.
KerbinInSpaceHighTundra: You wonder why the gravity field seems weaker here, and then remember that you are flying over the Tundra. Everything is smaller in the Tundra.
You let the gravity scanner do its thing while you admire the view you have over Kerbin's Tundra.
KerbinInSpaceHighIceCaps: The spike in graviolis here indicates a large mass below. Maybe it's the Ice Caps?
The Ice Caps' gravitational signature is surprisingly distinctive considering your distance.
KerbinInSpaceHighNorthernIceShelf: The frozen-over Northern Ocean bears a distinctive gravitational signature together with the polar hills ringing it.
Frozen sea appears to have the same gravimetric characteristics as normal sea.
KerbinInSpaceHighSouthernIceShelf: You could swear the gravitational signature changed since you last looked. Did a bug lump of ice just melt?
Most of the reading is clouded out by noise from the adjoining continent.
KerbinSrfLandedKSC*: The scanner indicates that gravity does, in fact, exist.
KerbinSrfLandedAdministration*: The graviolis try to look professional. It's not really working.
KerbinSrfLandedAstronautComplex*: The new recruits crowd around to check out your awesome sensor.
KerbinSrfLandedBaikerbanur*: Ultra-local gravimetric analysis suggests the presence of a hangar-sized void under the surface.
KerbinSrfLandedBaikerbanurLaunchpad*: You can't be bothered to go through the analysis process and pull up some records from when the pad was built long ago.
KerbinSrfLandedCrawlerway*: You measure the gravity slightly increasing as the crawler goes by.
KerbinSrfLandedFlagPole*: Being on top of the pole you realize the gravity of the situation.
KerbinSrfLandedLaunchPad*: The sensor gets burned to a crisp from the rocket exhaust, and is useless now.
KerbinSrfLandedMissionControl*: The operators here think that the universe revolves around them, but your gravity readings indicate otherwise.
KerbinSrfLandedRnD*: The graviolis feel very much at home here, surrounded by kerbals that care about them.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDCentralBuilding*: You feel like a rockstar with your gravioli detector here.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDCornerLab*: You don't really care about the gravioli experiment here. You just like how cool it makes you look to take readings.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDMainBuilding*: You have to fight off hordes of R&D techs trying to disassemble your gravioli detector to see how it works.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDObservatory*: This is the perfect spot to observe graviolis, and your measurement indicates: nominal. Now that was disappointing.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDSideLab*: Confused as to which side this lab is on, the graviolis don't know whether to read positive or negative.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDSmallLab*: The graviolis feel cramped in here, and register a higher reading than normal.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDTanks*: The graviolis seem confused with all the tanks around. We should try this experiment somewhere else.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDWindTunnel*: The instrument gets blown away and rolls down the tarmac before you can take an accurate reading.
KerbinSrfLandedRunway*: You are too busy dodging taxiing planes to get an accurate reading.
KerbinSrfLandedSPH*: The gravity here seems normal. Snack time!!
KerbinSrfLandedSPHMainBuilding*: Despite the ongoing debate, the gravity here seems the same as at the VAB.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHRoundTank*: The graviolis seem too busy doing circles in the tank to pay attention to the task at hand.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHTanks*: The graviolis seem confused with all the tanks around. We should try this experiment somewhere else.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHWaterTower*: The gravity sensor defers to the barometer on this one.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStation*: The graviolis seem to be strangely attracted to the tracking station. You are disappointed in them.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishEast*: Graviolioliolioliolioliolioli. Sorry, the scanner seems to have gotten stuck with the interference from the dish.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishNorth*: The graviolis bounce back and forth between the dishes, completely disinterested in your measurement.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishSouth*: The graviolis bounce back and forth between the dishes, completely disinterested in your measurement.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationHub*: The graviolis are drawn into the tracking station like a giant vacuum. You always thought this place sucked.
KerbinSrfLandedVAB*: The graviolis stare in awe at the construction going on inside.
KerbinSrfLandedVABMainBuilding*: Despite the ongoing debate, the gravity here seems the same as at the SPH.
KerbinSrfLandedVABPodMemorial*: You take in the gravity of the situation. It is definitely more intense here.
KerbinSrfLandedVABRoundTank*: The graviolis are having too much fun doing circles in the tank to pay attention to the task at hand.
KerbinSrfLandedVABSouthComplex*: Some of the graviolis sneak out to stow away on board the parts inside the building.
KerbinSrfLandedVABTanks*: The graviolis seem confused with all the tanks around. We should try this experiment somewhere else.
KerbinSrfLandedIslandAirfield*: The graviolis are laid back here, after they realise there's no flights around.
KerbinSrfLandedWoomerang*: The gravimeter is so well calibrated you can follow the foundation piles far deeper into the ground than you expected them to run.
KerbinInSpaceLowKSC*: The graviolis appear happy to finally be escaping Kerbin's gravitational well.
KerbinInSpaceHighKSC*: The graviolis look concerned that they may not be able to interact much at this altitude.

MunSrfLandedMidlands: The gravioli curve here is smooth with no anomalies. For once.
MunSrfLandedNorthernBasin: The graviolis are strangely non-polar here.
MunSrfLandedEastCrater: The ship slides a few inches down into the crater as the graviolis swarm around you.
MunSrfLandedNorthwestCrater: The graviolis here seem to be 'tilted', whatever that means.
MunSrfLandedSouthwestCrater: The gravity scans here are normal. Nothing to see here folks, move along.
MunSrfLandedFarsideCrater: The graviolis all squish over to the near side of Farside Crater.
MunSrfLandedCanyons: The data returned by the instrument reveals an ever-so-slight anomaly caused by the Canyons. Or maybe by your box of snacks.
MunSrfLandedPolarCrater: Despite our location the graviolis don't seem to be polarized.
MunSrfLandedPoles: The graviolis aren't sure whether this is the north or south pole, so they just flow 'down.'
MunSrfLandedPolarLowlands: The gravity scan is far less interesting than snacks. You emphatically relay this back to Mission Control.
MunSrfLandedHighlands: Gravity schmavity. Let's eat some snacks!
MunSrfLandedHighlandCraters: The graviolis seem to have a higher velocity towards Scotland here since you're not in the Lowland Craters.
MunSrfLandedMidlandCraters: The graviolis seem to be resting at the bottom of the craters. You make a note of this.
MunSrfLandedEastFarsideCrater: The graviolis are having fun bouncing off the crater walls.
MunSrfLandedTwinCraters: The graviolis seem to be distributed directly between the two craters.
MunInSpaceLowMidlands: You're not too sure which of the scary-looking numbers (and letters!) on the screen are relevant to your situation. So you just write them all down and send them off to the guys back at KSC.
MunInSpaceLowNorthernBasin: The scanner is reading a large positive gravitational anomaly while over the Northern Basin.
MunInSpaceLowEastCrater: Huh, even this close the Eastern Crater registers the exact same gravity as the Western Crater. Good thing we brought a good map!
Perhaps one day this data will be used to model tidal patterns back on Kerbin. Or something like that.
You let the graviolis go to work while you daydream about one day landing in the East Crater below. Or any other spot on the Mun really.
MunInSpaceLowNorthwestCrater: Like the other craters, the Northwest Crater appears to be associated with a large positive gravity anomaly.
MunInSpaceLowSouthwestCrater: You report a large positive gravitational anomaly over the Southwest Crater. The scientists explain that this is likely due to the basaltic lava flows that filled it in after the impact.
MunInSpaceLowFarsideCrater: The graviolis report nominal readings for a crater this size. Maybe the next one will change things up.
MunInSpaceLowCanyons: The gravity readings fluctuate as you move from canyon to flat ground and back to canyon again.
MunInSpaceLowPolarCrater: The scientists explained that accurate gravitational modelling is critical for ensuring navigation accuracy for future missions. Fine by you, especially if it means getting to go back again.
MunInSpaceLowPoles: The gravity scanner spits out an impressively long sheet of paper. To your dismay, it's almost entirely filled with numbers.
MunInSpaceLowPolarLowlands: Your gravity scan yields delicious science while you munch on a delicious snack.
MunInSpaceLowHighlands: You diligently record the gravitational data low over the Mun's Highlands. Anything that makes landing there easier is fine by you.
MunInSpaceLowHighlandCraters: Wow these gravioli particles sure get excited when you activate the scanner.
MunInSpaceLowMidLandCraters: You are slowly getting an accurate map of gravitational variances across the munar surface. It's not nearly as uniform as you thought it would be.
MunInSpaceLowEastFarsideCrater: The gravity fluctuates slightly - but noticeably - as you pass over the crater below.
You try to convince the graviolis that this is indeed an entirely new crater and not at all the same as the last one.
MunInSpaceLowTwinCraters: The gravitational anomalies below don't seem to fit the usual explanation of basaltic lava flows. The scientists at KSC are determined to find an alternative theory.
MunInSpaceHighMidlands: Watching the graviolis work is incredibly satisfying even if you aren't too keen on the math involved.
MunInSpaceHighNorthernBasin: A scientist back at KSC is guiding you through this gravity scan. He seems awfully excited about something called 'mascons'.
MunInSpaceHighEastCrater: Reading: nominal. It's a crater on the mun, what did you expect?
MunInSpaceHighNorthwestCrater: Bit by bit, the scientists back at KSC are using your data to build an accurate gravitational model of the Mun.
MunInSpaceHighSouthwestCrater: The gravity scanner has finished printing a black-and-white model of the Mun's gravity field. It looks rather dull so you take out your crayons and start colouring.
MunInSpaceHighFarsideCrater: The Mun's low gravity seems ideal for orbital infrastructure. You hope you live long enough to help make that happen.
MunInSpaceHighCanyons: The gravity signature is quite deep here. You suppose that makes sense.
MunInSpaceHighPolarCrater: The gravitational anomaly emanated by the huge crater below has actually managed to ever so slightly adjust your orbit.
MunInSpaceHighPoles: Polar measurement of the Mun's gravitational field? Check! Time for a snack.
MunInSpaceHighPolarLowlands: The KSC scientists seem particularly interested in gravity readings while over the Mun's poles.
MunInSpaceHighHighlands: The results are back and... Kerbin's Highlands are still first place! Suck it Mun!
MunInSpaceHighHighlandCraters: You transmit the data back to KSC. Perhaps they can send you back something useful after they've processed the information.
MunInSpaceHighMidlandCraters: Your gravity map is showing the Midland Crates as small anomalies from the general background. Could there be a link between the formation of craters and the Mun's gravitational field?
MunInSpaceHighEastFarsideCrater: You wonder what sorts of questions this data will answer back at KSC. Other than "What is the gravity like above the Mun's East Farside Crater?"
There might be a slight gravitational anomaly here. Or you might just be seeing things and be in dire need of a snack.
MunInSpaceHighTwinCraters: The Twin Crater's gravitational signature is quite unique. Not surprising considering its unique features.

MinmusSrfLanded*: The readings confirm what you've suspected for some time - Minmus is a small moon with weak gravity.
MinmusSrfLandedHighlands*: The sensor readings indicate that this would be an excellent location for a bouncy castle.
MinmusSrfLandedFlats*: You notice the gravity scans have been saying 666 over and over again.
MinmusSrfLandedGreatFlats*: The gravity readings dash your hopes - based on its size, Minmus is far too dense to be made of ice cream.
MinmusSrfLandedGreaterFlats*: Despite assurances from mission planners that the readings here would be off the charts, the sensor refuses to register anything higher than "rather weak" gravity.
MinmusSrfLandedLesserFlats*: The readings are conclusive: What goes up will take a fairly long time to come down.
MinmusSrfLandedPoles*: The gravity scan answers a question you came up with while eating breakfast this morning - yes, Minmus really is lighter than the Mun.
MinmusSrfLandedSlopes*: The sensor is picking up a lateral gravity gradient... Oh wait, it's just the tilt of the slope. Why did we land here again?
MinmusInSpaceHigh*: Sadly, the readings indicate that Minmus is far too dense to be composed of mint ice cream.
MinmusInSpaceHighFlats*: The needle doesn't seem to be moving. You're unsure whether this means that the instrument isn't working, or that the gravity is very, very weak.

DunaSrfLanded: The gravity is high enough to walk normally but low enough for EVA packs to work like jet-packs. Colonizing here just got a lot more appealing.
The tidal effects of Ike seem to get less and less as you move away from its geostationary spot.
DunaSrfLandedPoles: A burst of gas from underneath jolts the ship, and you have to reset the detector.
DunaSrfLandedMidlands: The sensor refuses to work, citing 'FOREIGN CONTAMINANT'. You sigh, knowing you have to clean it out again.
Seems pretty normal aside from Ike's libration causing slight tides.
DunaSrfLandedLowlands: The lands here produce no significant deviations from the Duna mean aside from a very slight lowering of gravity strength.
Beep, boop. Beep, boop. Beep, boop. The noises of the sensor are more interesting than the results.
DunaSrfLandedCraters: Surface measurements of the gravitational anomaly that exists here allow precise measuring of its effect on Duna's internal structure.
While you wait for the gravimeter to finish its scanning, a rock rolls downhill and bounces off the window with a crack to remind you you're on a slope.
DunaSrfLandedHighlands: Gravity here is slightly higher than elsewhere but less than expected.
You leave the scanner to do its thing and try not to worry about the dust ruining an 8,800-Fund piece of science kit.
DunaSrfLandedMidlandSea: The net gravity strength here varies ever so slightly with Ike's libration pattern.
All the while the sensor is running, you find yourself hoping aloud that the sand stays out of it. The warranty was voided long ago, and gravimeters are expensive!
DunaSrfLandedNorthernBasin: The detector doesn't pick up much out of the ordinary.
DunaSrfLandedSouthernBasin: The ice cap in the south distorts the gravity here slightly, and the basin itself shows the signatures of volcanic activity.
DunaSrfLandedNorthernShelf: The sensor attempts to measure the gravity through the craft's shaking in the winds. Eventally, it gives up and asks for a shock absorber.
The wind drops for long enough to allow the meter to complete its readings. Gravity looks to be ever so slightly lower here, but internal structure is the same.
DunaSrfLandedMidlandCanyon: Looks like standard crustal patterns here. Whatever made this canyon wasn't anything tectonic.
DunaSrfLandedEasternCanyon: That's a big bump on the Kouguer graph, which suggests significant crustal thinning is present in the area.
DunaSrfLandedWesternCanyon: The lack of any gravimetric anomalies here and the proximity to an ex-sea suggests this valley was carved by water or other erosional processes.
DunaSrfLandedPolarHighlands: Picking up a Kerbin-sized hunk of ice is easy. If you entered a strongkerbal contest remotely, you would win gold!
DunaSrfLandedPolarCraters: While the detector does its thing, you distract yourself with a view of the pretty white landscape.
DunaInSpaceLowPoles: There doesn't seem to be any gravity here. You grab a towel that is floating by and stow it away.
DunaInSpaceLowMidlands: The gravimeter reading dips ever so slightly whenever you pass over one of the snaking valleys.
No anomalies here, just the perfect region for Dunaian interior mapping.
DunaInSpaceLowLowlands: Down here, orbital coverage is relatively HD, yet still no anomalies show their heads. Keep looking.
Even KSC gets bored with the lack of anything happening and orders you to scan somewhere else.
DunaInSpaceLowCraters: Gravitational field strength dips noticeably as you pass over the crater. Surface measurements would let us nail down evem more detailed readings.
You don't see the normal crustal shock signatures found at Kerbin's own crater, suggesting Duna has a different internal structure.
DunaInSpaceLowHighlands: The line on the readout jitters around as you pass over huge massifs and valleys.
Graphical output shows a classic crustal thickening pattern, but less pronounced that otherwise thought. Maybe Duna's crust is thick enough not to form mountain roots?
DunaInSpaceLowMidlandSea: A dip as the elevation drops, and a bump as it rises. Nothing special, really.
Readings spew from the device onto the monitor as a wavy line, but not really that wavy.
DunaInSpaceLowNorthernBasin: Overall gravity strength forms a W-shaped graph thanks to the presence of the rebound peak.
DunaInSpaceLowSouthernBasin: Not too exciting. There's a tiny dip in overall strength, the Kouguer wave shows a bump as if the crust was thinner here.
DunaInSpaceLowNorthernShelf: The local gravity strength dips extremely noticeably (well, relatively speaking) as you pass over the huge chasm.
The various gravimetric lines go crazy, probably related to the giant canyon below. You didn't need this fancy kit to spot it.
DunaInSpaceLowMidlandCanyon: No major variations are detected, aside from a tiny drop in overall gravity as you pass over the valley.
DunaInSpaceLowEasternCanyon: Readings scroll across the screen, and when you pass over the canyon gravity drops slightly while the Kouguer reading spikes.
DunaInSpaceLowWesternCanyon: The needle dips slightly as you pass over the chasm, but there are no significant tectonic-associated changes.
DunaInSpaceLowPolarHighlands: Slight variations in gravity hint at the presence of entire moutains buried deep within the ice.
DunaInSpaceLowPolarCraters: The size of the blip created by the depression is too large for the ice alone, the crater must extend into the rock deep below.
DunaInSpaceHighPoles: The ice far below doesn't seem to affect the gravity much. Try getting closer.
DunaInSpaceHighMidlands: The Midlands' signal is drowned out by gravimetric noise from the surrounding highlands and canyons.
Even safely out of its reach, Ike still manages to be annoying and mess with your readings.
DunaInSpaceHighLowlands: All you're getting is the noise from nearby mountain ranges. Hopefully we'll find something once we get closer.
The sensor must be in the wrong way, because it's giving you some beautiful data on the Duna-Ike gravitational relationship but nothing from Duna.
DunaInSpaceHighCraters: Readings point teasingly at the presence of a gravimetric anomaly, but you can't isolate it at this altitude.
You're eating a snack when the display suddenly gives its loud 'anomaly bleep'. You give the console a smack for its trouble.
DunaInSpaceHighHighlands: The gravimeter ticks up slightly when passing over the mountain belts.
Did one of those floating magic boulders fly past? Because something just spiked the gravioli count!
DunaInSpaceHighMidlandSea: You bash the monitor, as the line isn't moving. It still doesn't move.
The readings slowly collate into a gravimetric map of... one single colour, with a dip thanks to Ike in the middle.
DunaInSpaceHighNorthernBasin: Not much. There are two small dips from passing over the basin, maybe a lower-altitude observation will reveal more?
DunaInSpaceHighSouthernBasin: Getting a lot of interference from the ice caps and Ike, but there looks to be some small anomalies.
DunaInSpaceHighNorthernShelf: Even from high orbit, the effects of the canyon appear clearly on the readout.
"Pilot! Follow that anomaly!". Huehuehue. Always wanted to say that.
DunaInSpaceHighMidlandCanyon: Ike's influence is especially strong here (it's right overhead), making it very hard to conduct precise measurements of the ground.
DunaInSpaceHighEasternCanyon: While the machine beeps and whirrs doing its thing, you invent the new sport of zero-G wall tennis with a crumpled page from a safety manual as the ball and a book as the bat.
DunaInSpaceHighWesternCanyon: Whatever impact that canyon has, it must be pretty small as you aren't getting much from way up here.
DunaInSpaceHighPolarHighlands: Doing a cartwheel takes no effort. You accidentally hit a switch while spinning, hopefully it didn't do anything.
DunaInSpaceHighPolarCraters: From up here you can get a gravimetric image of the whole area, with the crater clearly visible as an area of lower gravity.

DresSrfLanded*: The sensor is on the fritz again.
DresSrfLandedHighlands*: This data suggests Dres is not as large as the other bodies orbiting the Sun.
DresSrfLandedCanyons*: The gravity is so low, you wonder if Dres is a planet at all!

JoolInSpaceLow*: The readings fluctuate tremendously as the moons pass by.
The sensor is painfully giving readouts while trying to keep itself in one piece.
Jool's massive gravity well causes time to slow from your perspective.
You secure the sensor with a few more bolts just in case. It trembles in fear, but you give it a reassuring pat.
You begin to understand the gravity of the situation.
JoolInSpaceHigh*: The massive gas giant's gravity well takes hold of your craft. You begin to wonder how much fuel you have left.
Jool has much higher gravity than Kerbin.
The science team is going to get to write that "Large things in space produce large numbers" paper after all.

LaytheSrfLanded: You detect massive fluctuations as Jool tugs on the moon's surface.
LaytheSrfLandedPoles: You notice the gravity scans have been saying 666 over and over again.
Shaking the scanner does not seem to budge it. Instead you get out a golf ball and club. Gravity must be weaker than on Kerbin because you just hit it far enough for a hole in one!
LaytheSrfLandedShores: You notice the gravity scans have been saying 666 over and over again.
LaytheSrfLandedDunes: You notice the gravity scans have been saying 666 over and over again.
LaytheSrfLandedTheSagenSea: There does appear to be some gravity here.
LaytheSrfLandedCraterBay: The gravity scan reads 69. You, for no reason at all, say "nice".
LaytheSrfSplashedCrescentBay: You notice the gravity scans have been saying 666 over and over again.
LaytheSrfSplashedTheSagenSea: Theinstrument shorts out before you can get an accurate reading.
LaytheInSpaceLowPoles: Your scanner shows that the gravitational draw of this moon does not change wherever you go. The water makes the planet smoother than a baby Kerbal's bottom.
LaytheInSpaceLowShores: You drop your pencil and it floats gently way.

VallSrfLanded*: You notice the gravity scans have been saying 666 over and over again.
VallInSpaceHigh*: You can feel some gravity already.

TyloSrfLanded: Confirmed: Getting home will not be easy.
You notice the gravity scans have been saying 666 over and over again.
TyloInSpaceLow: The gravity here is negative for some reason.

BopSrfLanded: There seems to be a spike over one location... wait, it's gone now.
BopSrfLandedPeaks: The scanner seems split between Jool and Bop. You put it down to a defect.
The scanner refuses to point at Bop. After frantic calls to the manufacturer, Mission Control tells you that it is rated to high.
BopSrfLandedPoles: You notice the gravity scans have been saying 666 over and over again.
BopSrfLandedValley: You get weird results. Something seems Krakeny...
BopInSpaceLowPeaks: The peaks seem rather close. The gravity scanner agrees.
BopInSpaceHigh: The gravity levels are fluctuating slightly. Not enough to cause any obvious problems, but enough to indicate that there is more to Bop than we currently understand.
BopInSpaceHighPeaks: The scanner is still pointing at Jool.
What does this "%NaN%" mean? Mission Control tells you to watch out for Krakens.

PolSrfLanded*: There are roughly twice as many left-handed negative graviolis as right-handed ones here. That means something. Nobody knows what, though.
You notice the gravity scans have been saying 666 over and over again.

EelooSrfLanded*: This planetoid has a small but significant gravitational force, perfect for extreme snowball fights.
EelooInSpaceLow*: The gravity of Eeloo is relatively small, which in turn gives it a small SoI. You are happy that the guys back on Kerbin are actually skilled enough to hit an object of this size from so far away.
EelooInSpaceHigh*: Before you left Kerbin, the program director told you of the great gravity of your mission. It is clear to you now that he exaggerated.

infraredTelescope.description: An infrared space telescope used to observe distant astronomical targets from orbit, using the local body solely as an observation platform.
infraredTelescope.hints: Focus on observation conditions such as thermal background, stellar glare, planetary shadowing, and orbital stability, and on distant targets within or beyond the local system. The local body is not the observation target.

DresInSpaceHigh*: It's... a void. There is nothing there. Either Dres has no internal heat, or it simply doesn't exit.

magnetometer.description: Measurement of the local magnetic field.
magnetometer.hints: Descriptions of magnetic field strength and structure; if the body lacks a field, the instrument may detect activity from the parent planet or ambient space.

LaytheInSpaceLow: You try putting a magnet by the magnetometer, just to make sure it's working right.
You wish you had a compass. The magnetometer won't tell you which way is up.
LaytheInSpaceHigh: Jool's magnetic field seems to be protecting Laythe from the worst of Kerbol's wrath.

mobileMaterialsLab.description: A set of test materials exposed to the local environment.
mobileMaterialsLab.hints: General visual descriptions of how different materials respond to environmental factors.

SunInSpaceLow: Everything is on fire.
The materials are turning crispy and golden. You start to feel hungry again.
The samples seem to have completely fried.
Things are starting to melt in here.
Some of the containers are so hot that their glass is melting.
Some of the samples appear to be dissolving. The remaining samples appear to be dissipating. It is hard to distinguish the two effects from one another.
The samples are cooking up nicely. Time to turn them over and add some salt.
Some of the pieces facing the sun are now covered with little glowing cracks.
Some of the material samples have started to glow from the intense radiation.
The samples appear to be sweating.
The red hot glow of the metallic samples rivals the blazing intensity of the sun.
It says here "directly expose to sun for five minutes for a brilliant golden color."
The materials in the lab very clearly want to be farther away from the sun.
SunInSpaceHigh: The rocks show little signs of change. Some phosphorous samples glow a bright blue color, indicating the high radiation of interplanetary space.
The materials, now exposed directly to the sun, are decidedly uncomfortable.

MohoSrfLanded*: You record trace amounts of evaporated samples.
MohoSrfLandedCentralLowlands*: The racks holding the samples melt in a highly interesting fashion.
MohoSrfLandedSouthWesternLowlands*: The materails are currently embroiled in a civil war. You are unable to determine the cause.
MohoSrfLandedCanyon*: The samples have taken well to Moho and are now brown.
MohoInSpaceLow*: Some of the samples suggest Moho has a weak magnetic field.
MohoInSpaceHigh*: The materials have been affected by the intense heat and many of the softer ones have melted entirely.

EveSrfLandedPoles: It's still warm enough to make a lot of things rather soft or outright liquid.
EveSrfLandedMidlands: The gel samples begin to evaporate, making odd colored smoke. You suddenly wonder if this could be used to make colored exhaust.
EveSrfLandedExplodiumSea: The materials that have not melted are one by one starting to catch fire. This probably has implications for space suit design.
EveSrfLandedLowlands: Some of the canisters are crushed like tin cans from the atmospheric pressure. You try not to imagine the ship meeting the same fate.
EveSrfLandedHighlands: The samples appear torn between their allegiance to green and their new environment of purple. They turn an indecisive shade of blue.
EveSrfLandedPeaks: Despite the roasting temperatures, it's not enough to set anything on fire given the lack of oxygen. Some materials char, though.
EveSrfLandedImpactEjecta: Some of the monitoring equipment has suffered the same melty fate as the samples.
EveSrfLandedCraterLake: Most of the paints on the samples have dripped off or turned black.
EveSrfLandedWesternSea: A small amount of dust has covered the samples in even more purple shades.
EveSrfLandedOlympus: Some of the samples of organic material remind you of the results from the time your parents forgot to set the oven timer.
EveSrfLandedEasternSea: The sluggish air movement is slow, but the thick air grants it enough force to blow a couple of samples alarmingly close to the edge.
EveSrfLandedFoothills: A bottle of water explodes from the steam pressure in the heat, blasting the sample bay with shards and superheated water.
EveSrfLandedShallows: You are extremely disappointed to find the ice cream you packed to relax by the beach with has melted and caught fire.
EveSrfSplashedPoles: The oceans seem to be stripping the paint off of the lab's materials safety decals.
EveSrfSplashedExplodiumSea: The rubber duck you always keep in the bay as a good luck charm floats around, melting in the boiling liquid.
The materials melt and/or explode. We may want to get out of here.
EveSrfSplashedLowlands: The materials start to dissolve. The liquid here appears to be acidic.
EveSrfSplashedImpactEjecta: Several objects that should not be floating float out of the bay, bobbing in the toxic sea.
EveSrfSplashedCraterLake: The materials look happy as they quickly dissolve in the mix of chemicals that make up this sea.
