EveSrfSplashedWesternSea: Some sparks from a shorting cable give you a miniature heart attack as they dance over the not-water, but nothing explodes.
EveSrfSplashedEasternSea: When the materials bay was opened, most of the samples fell out. Some of them floated, and others sank. Fascinating!
EveSrfSplashedShallows: Several materials that are quite close to the materials of your ship are readily attacked and destroyed by the fluid.
EveFlyingLow: The samples are torn out of the container by the pressure of the atmosphere. Perhaps Kerbtape wasn't enough.
Dribbles of melting polymers trail out of the bay, borne aloft on the thick purple winds.
Small amounts of airborne purple dust sticks to the rapidly softening materials, dyeing them purple.
EveFlyingHigh: Ten out of ten samples agree: "The stuff below you is a liquid."
Even though you are still high in the planet's atmosphere, the samples are beginning to decompose due to the heat and pressure.
The samples are rattling around in the bay from heavy aerodynamic forces.
EveInSpaceLow: All samples turn purple out of fear.
You could swear the samples are turning purple already, and you're still in space.
When you open the bay, there are no samples. You close the doors and reopen them, and they're all there again. Is Gilly playing magic tricks on you?
EveInSpaceHigh: The materials bay, after an apparent deliberation, produces a liquid. It's almost, but not quite, entirely unlike tea.
The radioactivity has caused the blutonium samples to glow even more. Apart from that, everything is normal.
Some of the material do not seem to have survived the journey from Kerbin in the hard vacuum environment.

GillySrfLandedMidlands: The loose samples take a very long time to roll to the lower end of the bay.
GillySrfLandedLowlands: Some of the samples float out of the materials bay.
GillySrfLandedHighlands: When disturbed, the objects take a long time to return to the floor in a new and interesting pattern. You wonder if you could sell the Materials Lab as a piece of modern art.
GillyInSpaceLow: It looks like someone's been trying to hide snacks in the Materials Lab.
GillyInSpaceHigh: The samples behave in much the same way that they did while in deep space.

KerbinSrfLanded*: Why are we performing this experiment here? We could have just stayed at home and done it.
KerbinSrfLandedWater*: You take care to limit your samples' exposure to the humid sea air. Some of them are rather susceptible to corrosion.
The materials lab seems particularly damp. You pull out a mop and bucket to be safe as you finish your report.
KerbinSrfLandedGrasslands*: The materials lab has grass-stains on it now from that landing. So much for returning it to the manufacturer for a refund when we are done.
You crack open window as you write up the lab report and bask in the warm sunlight and soothing breeze.
A blade of grass flies into the lab and lands right in your experiment, ruining the results. Oh well, we'll just make something up for the folks back at KSC.
KerbinSrflandedGrasslands*: A butterfly flies into your experiment. You spend a distracting amount of time thinking up various non-lethal methods to get rid of it.
KerbinSrfLandedHighlands*: Most of the materials in the lab have either boiled off or frozen at this altitude. You decide that it's not worth the effort to do research, and decide to have some snacks instead.
One of your samples gets loose and you have to go and chase it down as it rolls down the hill.
KerbinSrfLandedShores*: You give the materials lab a wee beach umbrella.
Why are you in the lab when you could be at the beach?
The report seems to be the same as last time. You wish you could be hanging out on the shores right now.
Your lab report contains thinly-veiled suggestions that the next set of experiments be done at the beach. For science, of course.
KerbinSrfLandedMountains*: The lab slowly rocks back and forth as you perform your experiments perched on top of a particularly craggy mountain.
You're quite certain that lugging around an entire materials lab while on top of a mountain is as dangerous as it is unnecessary. But hey, you're just an astronaut.
KerbinSrfLandedDeserts*: The desert dust appears to have contaminated the samples. You learn that deserts aren't a good place to bring expensive science equipment.
Watching the dust swirl among the samples, you realize that you're going to have to spend a lot of time cleaning them up for the next mission.
You take your juice pack and pour it all over the samples to prepare them for the dry, dusty ordeal they are about to face. Mission Control sighs and tells you to use a different set of samples.
KerbinSrfLandedBadlands*: You expose the samples to the badlands of Kerbin, but worry what it will do to them.
Turns out the Badlands is a bad place for a materials lab. Who knew?
Some of your samples are starting to give you attitude. You're pretty sure this a bad sign.
KerbinSrfLandedTundra*: The samples inside your lab have devolved into a hunter-gatherer state as a response to the harsh conditions of the Tundra.
The lab seems small and cramped here. You quickly finish your report and get some fresh air.
As you open the materials bay, snow rapidly accumulates inside, making it difficult to perform your experiments.
The materials seem to have shrunk in their containers, which makes completing your research difficult. Hey, we're getting hazard pay for this, right?
KerbinSrfLandedIceCaps*: The water sample has frozen solid and the goopy samples appear to be extra goopy.
Some samples are very brittle, while others have made a fire to warm themselves.
KerbinSrfLandedNorthernIceShelf*: The materials have congregated around the bay lamp for warmth. It must be pretty darn cold.
The icy samples keep slipping away from being grabbed. Don't they want to come into the warmth?
KerbinSrfLandedSouthernIceShelf*: Your samples are huddling together for warmth in the frigid environment.
The thin glaze of ice that has formed on the bay doors cracks and cascades off as they grind open.
KerbinSrfSplashedWater*: You submerge the materials bay into the water and it comes back up with a decent haul of codfish.
Everything has gotten really wet. One of the samples caught fire! Whoa!
Exposing the power source of the lab to water has been enlightening.
The materials inside the lab start to mix in with the water. You suspect that might not be very safe.
KerbinSrfSplashedGrasslands*: As expected, most of the samples sink while others float out. You swear you saw a yellow rubber ducky among them.
KerbinSrfSplashedShores*: A dastardly seagull has made off with half the samples in the materials lab and left you a foul-smelling present. Damn you seagull, damn you.
The materials seem to have been contaminated by bits of sand and seaweed, but you perform your experiments anyway.
As you open the materials bay a seagull flies out and poops on your hand. It seems you can never escape the reach of the beach...
You're pretty happy about the experiment results...but not as happy as you would be if you were on the beach. So how about it, Mission Control?
KerbinSrfSplashedDeserts*: Several of the samples expand from soaking up water. The cactus looks particuarly pleased.
KerbinSrfSplashedBadlands*: A few samples have melted into the water, and will probably create new forms of Badlands life.
KerbinSrfSplashedTundra*: Materials float just as well in cold water as warm water. You know this because the bay is full of it.
KerbinSrfSplashedIceCaps*: You have determined that water temperature does not affect buoyancy.
KerbinSrfSplashedNorthernIceShelf*: You see a rubber duck float out of the Materials Bay, frozen in a block of ice.
KerbinSrfSplashedSouthernIceShelf*: The cold water reacts slowly with even the volatile materials.
KerbinFlyingLow*: As you fly over the Badlands, you hear evil laughter and what sounds like thunder coming from inside the lab.
You resist the urge to cackle madly and speak in a German accent as you pass over the Badlands.
Some of the materials get sucked out the bay door when you accidentally open the door. You plan on blaming a seagull when they land on someone below.
The lab is in complete disarray after hitting turbulence.
A particularly persistent bird is following you around, perhaps attracted by the smell emanating from your exposed samples.
The lab seems to be picking up some of the dry, dusty particles from the surface as you fly so close to the ground.
Your low-altitude flight is kicking up a lot of dust. That can't be good for the samples.
You find it difficult to concentrate on your lab research flying this close to the terrain. You write down some random gibberish to send back to KSC.
You hope the autopilot keeps you from crashing into any stray hills as you play with the materials lab.
Materials are strewn across the lab as the ship performs a mostly-expected gravity turn.
You hope the autopilot is up-to-snuff as you write your lab report while your craft zips past several mountain peaks.
You are so distracted by the thought of one of the craggy peaks below scraping up against the lab that you're unable to finish the assay.
KerbinFlyingHigh*: You are flying high over the Badlands, so naturally your thoughts turn to recreational substances.
As you expose the samples to the Badlands' atmosphere, you force yourself to accept the fact that they will never be the same again.
Even at this altitude, none of your samples are spared from the horror that is the Badlands.
Woohoooo! We're sciencing!
Several of the samples have developed small fur-like projections.
The materials in the lab are viscous and difficult to work with. After struggling for a while you write up the data that you can, ready for the folks back at KSC.
You make note to bring a climate-controlled materials lab next time. The one you currently have appears to be frozen solid.
The samples are shivering too hard for you to get any useful science out of them.
Almost above the mountains and into space. When we get there we should have some snacks. You know, as a reward for awesomeness.
You're quite certain the hills of the Highlands don't grow this high and so you are able to relax and focus on your experiment.
The atmosphere buffets the side of the lab, distracting you as you write your lab report.
You wonder if that half sandwich you left in the fridge at home is still there, as you write up yet another lab report. When will the reports end and the sandwiches begin??
You prepare the lab for both reentry and deep space travel, since you don't remember which direction you're headed.
Your lab report contains nothing out of the ordinary, but you write it up for the scientists back at KSC anyway.
You sneak long glances out the window as you wait for the experiments to complete.
In your lab report, you include a note asking Mission Control to send you someplace warmer next time.
With all the experiments well underway you grab a box of snacks, wishing it was bigger than it is.
All of the materials are still wet from the mishap-that-shall-not-be-named earlier. You jot down some random numbers and send it back to KSC.
You perform your experiments as you fly high over Kerbin's mountains. Oh look! Is that a yeti?
KerbinInSpaceLow*: The samples don't appear to be bothered too much by the fact that they're over the Badlands. Perhaps its influence is limited to within the atmosphere?
The view from the lab is particularly distracting today, and you find it takes an extra hour to complete your report.
Hey, didn't we used to have a microwave in this lab? How are we supposed to get good results when we cant properly heat up our snacks?
The samples barely react other than giving a small shudder.
You play music as you write your lab report. It reminds you of your home amidst Kerbin's grassy plains.
LLLLAAAABBBB RRRREEEEPPPPOOOORRRRTTTT IIIINNNN SSSSPPPPAAAACCCCEEEE!!!! Ahem, yes, everything is nominal.
You pull out your trusty lab notebook to make a note of what you observe. After 2 minutes of searching for a pencil you remember you are in space now, and find it floating in a corner.
You perform your experiments with a rock from the Highlands as a guest of honor.
You finish your lab work, write up the report, and pull out the snacks. Job well done!
The light reflects off the surface of the planet below as you stare out the window. Oh yeah....science. You finish writing up your report for the kerbals back at KSC.
You quickly slam the door shut on the lab after peeking in. It doesn't look good in there, to say the least.
As you open the bay doors, a sample floats out, heavily convulsing and making high pitched noises via radio. Mission Control denies your request to kill it with fire.
The samples begins to float out of the main chamber. You manage to close the doors, but not before losing a few to the endless void. You wonder how the lab techs will feel about this.
The materials seem to float through empty...space. That's it! We must be in space!
Low-gravity experiments in space over Kerbin. Just another day for a kerbonaut.
The lab has a certain 'vacuuminess' to it here. You note that in the report to KSC.
As you open the doors of the material bay, you see tiny dust particles from the deserts of Kerbin float free. You wonder where they will eventually end up and hope the answer isn't 'in your sock'.
The lab seems peaceful as you go about your study over the grassy plains below.
The mountains below have little to no bearing on the outcome of your experiments. Even so, you reference them in your report as much as possible, to impress the folks back home.
KerbinInSpaceHigh*: You consider how nice it is to be in the lab rather than in the Badlands below you.
The materials bay seems cautiously optimistic about being this far away from the Badlands.
This far out, the Badlands have little to no noticeable affect on your array of samples.
Some of your samples create interesting refractions as they are exposed to the sunlight reflecting from the blue waters of Kerbin.
As you write down your lab report you visually confirm that Kerbin's oceans do indeed make up the majority of its surface.
The lab shimmers blue in the reflected light from Kerbin's ocean below. Or maybe you're just delirious from lack of snacks.
You are unable to properly finish your lab report. The sight of all the water below you keeps making you get up and go to the bathroom.
You finally finish writing a disgustingly long lab report. Oh well, at least your office has a good view.
The air in the lab seems stale and cramped even though you are far above the planet. You wonder why.
Ah a quiet journey, a mouthful of snacks, and a beautiful view of Kerbin's mountain ranges! If it wasn't for the lab report you have to write, this would be the perfect moment.
You think you glimpse KSC out the window as you finish up your lab report.
A frozen glowing squid has just floated out of the Materials Bay.
You write something suitably scientific and go back to watching the auroras swirl about the Ice Caps.
The lab has a certain serenity flying so high above the planet. There is no place you'd rather be right now.
As you finish your lab experiments one of the massive peaks below catches your eye. It's amazing how small things look from way up here.
As you write up your lab report, you marvel at how smooth Kerbin looks despite the Highlands.
You brought along some dirt from Kerbin's Grasslands for this experiment. You're not really sure what to do with it though.
Far below there is a biome of some sort. You are too far away from the planet to tell which one, though. We need better facilities in this lab.
You look down at the surface through the lab window as you process results from your latest experiment. Kerbin looks so small from up here.
The gel samples shrink, boil and freeze simultaneously when exposed to the hard vacuum of space.

You take your little ball of sand from Kerbin's desert and let it loose into outer space. You fondly imagine it growing up and one day becoming a mighty asteroid! Then you remember what asteroids tend to do to inhabited planets and desperately try to gather it back up.
The sight of Kerbin from high over the Ice Caps is enough to make you pause your experiments and take some time to enjoy the view.
You intermittently stare out the window at the mountains below as you write up your report. Space is awesome!
KerbinSrfLandedKSC*: The materials all seem to be posing, preparing for the scientists' examination.
The samples look like pretty much everything else around here.
KerbinSrfLandedAdministration*: The materials shine. You assume they want to look good when facing their bosses.
KerbinSrfLandedAstronautComplex*: The new recruits are fascinated to see what a materials lab looks like.
KerbinSrfLandedCrawlerway*: The Materials Bay rolls off and gets stuck between the tracks.
KerbinSrfLandedFlagPole*: The materials seem unimpressed by your climbing skills.
KerbinSrfLandedLaunchPad*: You think that this experiment is rather superfluous.
The materials are neatly arranged, waiting for launch. You suppress the urge to shake the container.
KerbinSrfLandedMissionControl*: Mission control wonders why you have to do your experiment HERE.
KerbinSrfLandedRnD*: You are humbled to perform your experiments here, at their birthplace.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDCentralBuilding*: You feel right at home here in the lab.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDCornerLab*: The materials fail to indicate which corner you are in.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDMainBuilding*: The staff here spend so much time in the lab themselves that they are completely uninterested in your results.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDObservatory*: You observe the materials while the R&D staff observe you.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDSideLab*: The materials fail to indicate which side you are at.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDSmallLab*: You are amused at the puny size of this lab compared to yours.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDTanks*: Your lab blends in with the tanks here, so you can do your experiments incognito.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDWindTunnel*: Materials were "Ejected due to extreme baro-differential". Luckily you managed to hang on.
KerbinSrfLandedRunway*: The materials are all facing upwards. You hastily turn them sideways.
KerbinSrfLandedSPH*: The great view of the new planes distracts you from getting any meaningful lab work done.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHMainBuilding*: The engineers here eye your lab module with disdain.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHRoundTank*: You consider how much the lab looks like a tank.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHTanks*: Your lab blends in with the tanks here, so you can do your experiments incognito.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHWaterTower*: A leak from the tower washes away your materials before you can experiment on them. Oh well, snack time!
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStation*: You take the leftover materials and throw them at the tracking station, hoping they stick to the side of the building.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishEast*: You play loud rock music in the lab, saying you are testing materials, but really you're just trying to annoy the folks at the tracking station.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishNorth*: You position the lab directly in front of the dish to see what effect it will have on the materials.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishSouth*: The materials leak out and start corroding the tracking dish. You hope no-one else saw that.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationHub*: You make a show of performing your experiment here, so the tracking station staff can see what a small contribution they make to the program.
KerbinSrfLandedVAB*: A welding rod fell into your experiment mid-way. You don't know if this will effect the results, so you finish your report anyway.
KerbinSrfLandedVABMainBuilding*: The general commotion around here vibrates the materials off the lab bench. You figure this qualifies as a result, and leave to have some snacks.
KerbinSrfLandedVABPodMemorial*: You consider how similar the materials you are using are to the pod outside.
KerbinSrfLandedVABRoundTank*: You consider how much the lab looks like a tank.
KerbinSrfLandedVABTanks*: Your lab blends in with the tanks here, so you can do your experiments incognito.

MunSrfLanded: As you look away, one sample makes a run for it.
MunSrfLandedMidlands: The munar soil has mixed with one of the materials, producing a gray paste that is getting everywhere.
MunSrfLandedNorthernBasin: All the materials seem to be squished to the bottom of their containers. It takes you a while to complete your experiments.
MunSrfLandedEastCrater: The materials promptly turn into a gooey mess from all the dust flying around at the surface.
MunSrfLandedNorthwestCrater: You keep hoping to see Carey Grant go running through the lab. No such luck.
MunSrfLandedSouthwestCrater: The lab is cozy with the light reflecting off the crater outside. You pull out a box of snacks and get to work on it. Working on the labwork will come later.
MunSrfLandedFarsideCrater: Working in a lab in a crater on the moon makes you feel like a mad scientist. You note this in your report.
MunSrfLandedCanyons: For a moment, one of the materials reflects a black shape somewhere on the distant surface.
MunSrfLandedPolarCrater: All the materials are polarized and sticking together. Is there ANYTHING that's not difficult in space?!
MunSrfLandedPoles: Mobile Materials Lab - Polar Mun Report: Why did we bring this thing instead of more snacks? Thanks. - Signed, The Crew.
MunSrfLandedPolarLowlands: You aren't quite sure of what to make of the lab results. Guess the scientists back on Kerbin can figure it out.
MunSrfLandedHighlands: The vista from here is very distracting, so it takes you some extra time to complete your report.
MunSrfLandedHighlandCraters: As you complete your lab reports you look forward to EVA-ing and running down the slope of the crater to see how fast you can get going.
MunSrfLandedMidlandCraters: You find it difficult to finish your report with everything at an angle. There were so many craters around it was impossible to find a level landing site.
MunSrfLandedEastFarsideCrater: The materials promptly roll down the hill to the lowest part of the crater as soon as you open the canister. You guess that's why its a 'Mobile Materials' Lab.
MunSrfLandedTwinCraters: Perched between the two craters the materials seem to be divided equally in their containers. You note this in your report.
MunSrfLandedLowlands: The samples have climbed out and planted a flag. Looks likes you're not getting that World First.
MunInSpaceLow: Your experiments are still unable to conclusively prove whether or not the Mun is made of cheese. You formally request permission to go lower.
You gaze down at the crater below, absentmindedly stirring a flask of some unnamed substance.
You hear repeated cries of "Are we there yet?" coming from the materials bay.
You are too busy doodling the crater below to finish your lab experiments, so you send the doodle back to KSC instead of your report.
After much deliberation, the samples have all agreed that the Mun does not in fact appear to be made of cheese. You proudly send this report back up to KSC.
Your material tests indicate that similar metals brought into contact with one another form a strong bond when in vacuum. That should lead to some interesting developments in spaceflight.
The materials lab is ready for action! You, however, need a snack first to get you going.
Oh dear, it seems that you have misplaced your snack inside the materials bay. Now you can't tell what's edible and what's not.
You pick a sample at random and subject it to a plethora of scientific examinations including the time-tested method of sticking it in your mouth to see what it tastes like.
Watching the surface below, you can't help but think how great it would be if you had a munar surface sample to study using the equipment in your lavishly-equipped lab.
The instructions on the materials bay claim that the samples inside can be used as an emergency food supply. You hope you brought enough snacks.
Experimentation over the Mun has some interesting variances from experimentation above Kerbin.
An experiment in munar orbit, how exciting! You can't wait to see how they compare with the results from Kerbin orbit.
The materials bay door jams. You give it a few swift kicks until it pops open.
Ah it's so nice to have your own laboratory far from the restrictions of Kerbin. No gravity and especially no safety supervisor!
There seems to be an argument at Mission Control over which experiment to do first. You sit back and eat some snacks while waiting for them to decide.
In today's experiment you observe your pet hamster's activities while in munar orbit gravity.
MunInSpaceHigh: Your lab report is strategically written to convince the KSC to let you land on the Mun.
The samples ask to be woken up when you're on the Mun.
Why are we in a lab up here when we could be playing....erm, EVA-ing, in the crater below?
You fill your lab report with scribbled images of you planting a flag on the Mun (not to scale). You hope the folks back at KSC get the hint.
Wow, this place needs some acne medication.
You finish your lab report in time to catch a glimpse of the huge Farside Crater down below.
It's getting very difficult to keep track of your experiments as they float around in the low gravity. Maybe you shouldn't have tried to them all at once.
This particular experiment is from an elementary science class that won a national contest. Neat!
You decide to put off writing your report until you reach the dark side of the Mun. There won't be much to see and you'll be able to concentrate better.
Your lab is going through materials at an alarming rate. Perhaps you shouldn't have replaced the entire bottom rack with snacks.
Just another day at the lab... Oh wait, no it isn't! You're flying over the Mun!
Solar radiation reflected off the Mun's surface adds an interesting new angle to your science experiments.
Laboratory work is lots of fun! You can just leave things floating around instead of putting them away!
Zero-gravity experiments are messy! You wish there was sort of space vacuum you can use. Your gaze falls on the airlock and something clicks in your head. Hmm...
Your current experiment spontaneously combusts. Good thing you have more! Now where did we go wrong last time?
Your samples seem to be doing well so far on this journey. You wonder if they are as excited as you are to get to the Mun.
Your experiment involves several high-powered lasers, which never ceases to amuse you.

MinmusSrfLanded*: Bacterial samples appear to gorge themselves on what looks like microscopic mint scones.
MinmusSrfLandedHighlands*: Some of the surface dust reacts with the metal samples to produce an aquamarine rust.
MinmusSrfLandedMidlands*: The samples appear to have set up a communist government. You are not sure how you can tell.
MinmusSrfLandedFlats*: As you open the doors a sample dashes past to taste the surface.
MinmusSrfLandedGreatFlats*: The low temperature has frozen one of the liquid samples into a sort of iced cream. Was it green before?
MinmusSrfLandedGreaterFlats*: Some of the biological samples have not fared well. The metals, however, appear to be doing just fine.
MinmusSrfLandedSlopes*: Mission Control informs you that you have to put on safety goggles before doing any experiments. You fail to fit them on your helmet, and Mission Control reponds by telling you to immediately abort the mission.
MinmusInSpaceHigh*: The samples react to the lack of an atmosphere.

DunaSrfLanded: The samples appear to be permanently red. You consider sending missions in pink EVA suits to reduce cleaning costs.
The samples are slowly turning red. Or is that just your eyesight?
DunaSrfLandedPoles: Nothing much happens to the samples when exposed to geyser gas. Whataver it is, it muct be pretty inert.

You suddenly remember you left the bottle of whisky you smuggled on board disguised as a sample in the chamber. As you watch, ice encrusts the side of the bottle, but the alcohol stays liquid. Who knew liquor could be a science experiment?
DunaSrfLandedMidlands: The thin atmosphere of Duna evidently isn't too thin, since it blows several of the samples out of the chamber.

An arc lamp in the bay blows out, and the resulting white-hot sparks cause the aluminium sample to react with dust in the chamber, throwing off an impressive amount of fire to produce a kind of white ash.
DunaSrfLandedLowlands: The fine lowlands dust worms its way into everything. Even behind the lens of the observation camera.
The samples haven't changed since you last looked at them. Not even the slightly thicker air and fine dust can change them.
DunaSrfLandedCraters: Observing becomes somewhat difficult as red dust cakes over the camera lens.
You half-hoped to see the bolide fragment do something interesting after coming here. Sadly, it doesn't.
DunaSrfLandedHighlands: There's not enough wind here to disturb the samples, and the thin air doesn't affect then either.
Dust has gotten into the door mechanism, and you thump it to make it open. Fat lot of change you get, anyway.
DunaSrfLandedMidlandSea: You put a small sample of subsurface sand into the chamber, and hear a series of tiny pops from the sodium as you heat it. Interesting.
You lost a few of the test samples in transit, but that's not really an issue as they all just turn red anyway.
DunaSrfLandedNorthernBasin: The coarse dust doesn't penetrate as far into everything as the standard insidious stuff you find on Duna.
You leave the samples to do their thing, and go fetch the snacks you warmed earlier.
DunaSrfLandedSouthernBasin: The mixture of ice and dust cakes the samples. Nothing happens, apart from them going pink.
It's cold here. Suffice to say, all the samples freeze.
DunaSrfLandedNorthernShelf: You open the doors, and the wind immediately sweeps every single sample out of the bay. Damn.
Run the experiment if you want, but the bay camera has been sandblasted to pieces and I am NOT going out there to look.
DunaSrfLandedMidlandCanyon: A sudden, unexpected gust of wind knocks several samples out of the chamber, but you're more concerned about the spacesuits turning pink.
Sand piles up in drifts against the samples. It's pretty fine, so you bash the chamber and it all cascades out onto the ground.
DunaSrfLandedEasternCanyon: The samples quickly disappear under a thick caking of dust. True to form, the observation computer attracts the most.
The doors grind open with the sort of crunching sound made by large quantities of dust in the servo mechanism.
DunaSrfLandedWesternCanyon: You get the standard Duna reaction from most is the samples (i.e. going slightly red and cold). However, you put one of the lithium hydroxide CO2 scrubbers from the capsule into the bay as an afterthought, and watch it form a white, powdery coating.
The dust that blows into the bay appears to be from two sources, as it forms two different piles of fine and grainier sand.
DunaSrfLandedPolarHighlands: Most of the samples crystallise and the white samples look even whiter. Maybe with a pink hue.
You notice tiny beads of mercury condensing on the inside surface of the bay's striplight and hurriedly turn it back on to keep it warm.
DunaSrfLandedPolarCraters: Ice cascades off the sample bay doors as they open, and it's more interesting than what happens to the samples (they get cold, and that's it).
Following exposure, most of the liquid samples freeze while a few boil instead. The solid ones do very little.
DunaFlyingLow: The atmosphere whips most of the samples of the bay immediately. Seems you underestimated it.
There's a fizzling sound as the dust shorts something out in the bay mechanism. It comes back online after a few shakes and presses.
DunaFlyingHigh: Some of the lighter samples still shift around in the rushing high-altitude air.
The upper atmosphere is too rarefied and thin to react with anything other than pushing stuff about.
DunaInSpaceLow: A thruster jolt must have disturbed one of the samples, since it's currently all over the wall.
The samples haven't done much in space, but what they do on the surface must be very interesting.
DunaInSpaceHigh: The radiation on the journey here has bleached some of the samples, and polymers have turned brittle.
Normal spacey stuff has happened to the samples. Yes, that's what we call it.

DresSrfLanded*: The samples are quiet. Too quiet.
DresSrfLandedRidges*: The samples appear to have set up a anarchist governmeant. You are not sure how you can tell.
DresSrfLandedCanyons*: The samples seem to have no reaction to the surroundings, but while examining them you think you hear a faint screaming. Is it the samples, or you?
DresInSpaceLow*: Something must have hit the lab and mixed up all the samples. Probably a micro-meteor or some swamp gas. You had nothing to do with it.
DresInSpaceHigh*: The objects are still floating around and making a mess, though some of them have grouped together and occasionally you see a spark between them.

JoolFlyingLow*: The samples are running around in circles.
One of the samples is panicking.
The pressure of the Jool's atmosphere is so high that a few of the samples have begun fusing together.
JoolFlyingHigh*: Jool's immense gravity is pulling the materials towards one side of the container.
JoolInSpaceLow*: Pressured by Jool's gravity, the samples have begun a revouloution. Who they are revoulting against is undeterminable.
JoolInSpaceHigh*: We came, we saw, we did science. And we're still here.

LaytheSrfLanded: Some of the samples spill and soak into the soil. You silently pray that the samples are sterile.
LaytheSrfLandedPoles: Some of the samples seem to be frozen.
Snow suddenly blows into some of the samples. You hope it doesn't effect the results.
LaytheSrflandedShores: The Mobile Materials Lab hums and whirs as it processes the sand you shoveled into it.
LaytheSrfLandedDunes: You put a sand-like substance into the Mobile Materials lab. It hums and whirs for some time and then it beeps. The screen says "Sand".
LaytheSrfSplashed: Certain samples react explosively with Laythe's oceans. Or perhaps the sensor is reacting explosively? It's hard to tell.
LaytheSrfSplashedCrescentBay: The Goo seems to be afraid of water.
LaytheSrfSplashedTheSagenSea: Despite the temperature one of the samples is starting to glow hot as it touches the water.
LaytheFlyingLow: The materials suddenly gather by the window, looking at the surface below.
They look the same as they were on Kerbin.

VallSrfLanded*: You hear a knocking coming from the inside of the materials bay, but good judgment keeps you from investigating further.
VallSrfLandedHighlands*: You accidentally drop some ice from the lab onto the surface. You wonder if there is a way to tell the difference between Kerbin's ice and Vall's ice.

TyloSrfLanded: It seems as if the samples have put on some weight.
TyloSrfLandedHighlands: The samples look very similar to peanut butter. Then you realize this isn't a sample canister and someone got peanut butter all over your materials!
TyloSrfLandedMara: The samples appear to have started a corporate oligarchy. You're not sure how, but the container is now full of mines and factories.
TyloInSpaceHigh: The metal is metallic, the wood is wooden, the glass is glassy. If Tylo is going change any of this you will have to get closer.

BopSrfLanded: The materials are getting a little freaked out, which in turn freaks you out. Bop is truly an evil place.
BopSrfLandedPeaks: You think you see a small tentacle, but the video flickers, and it's gone.
BopSrfLandedRidges: The materials appear to have set up a new facist government, and are attemping to invade a nearby fuel tank.
BopInSpaceLow: You open the doors and notice something small and green. The scientists said they lost a radioactive squid the other day. You hope this won't affect your journey home.

PolSrfLanded: You hear a sneeze in the bay.
PolSrfLandedLowlands: Small pieces of Pol cover the samples.
PolSrfLandedHighlands: Some of the samples have shriveled up.
PolSrfLandedMidlands: The materials appear to have set up a monarchist govarnment, and are building a very small palace.

EelooSrfLanded*: A liquid canister spills onto the ground and flash freezes in place. Not very informative, but very pretty.
EelooSrfLandedNorthernGlaciers*: You look away, and some of the materials shape into a dog.
EelooSrfLandedLowlands*: All of the materials have frozen solid, except for one which has mysteriously caught fire.
EelooSrfLandedHighlands*: You illuminate one of the samples, and it follows the light. You play with it for a little while.
EelooSrfLandedIceCanyons*: A strange green crystal has grown. Maybe Mission Control will know what to do with it.
EelooSrfLandedCraters*: One of the samples goes mad and jumps out of the materials bay. He was always the weird one anyway.
EelooSrfLandedMidlands*: Most of the materials are frozen solid.
EelooInSpaceLow*: You used the science lab to assemble a miniature model of the science lab. There was a lot of downtime while waiting to get to Eeloo.
EelooInSpaceHigh*: The samples have stopped moving. You aren't sure if this is because of the cold or the time it took to get here.

mysteryGoo.description: A container holding a semi-sentient, highly reactive substance that exhibits curiosity, mood, and adaptive behavior depending on its surroundings.
mysteryGoo.hints: Focus on the Goo’s apparent personality and actions: it prefers wet or fluid environments, dislikes dry or barren ones, and is especially content in space — most of all far from gravity. Describe active, sometimes playful or stubborn responses, with rare physical stress effects only in extreme conditions.

SunInSpaceLow: The Goo is developing a nice tan.
The Goo skitters around in its canister!
The Goo seems to be in a panic.
The Goo is beginning to glow. A lot.
The Goo bubbles, releasing a noxious gas and whimpering slightly.
You notice that the Goo has started to glow and has become very radioactive. It would be of best interest to stay away!
The Goo seems to have gone all wobbly.
The Goo is starting to sweat.
The Goo boils on one side and freezes on the other.
The Goo explodes.
The Goo almost burns up as the sun heats the spacecraft.
The Goo starts to turn red.
The Goo seems to be very concerned about burning up.
The Goo morphs into a mesmerizing array of shapes.
The Goo shrivels up and gives you the evil eye.
Say, why's the Goo sizzling?
The Goo seems to be dancing from the radiation. The Kerbals back at Mission Control decide to join in.
After shaking aggressively for some time, the Goo stops moving and turns orange. It also begins giving off a great deal of heat and light.
The Goo violently shakes in the container. It doesn't seem to be liking the extreme heat one bit.
In an attempt to cool itself down, the Goo has expanded to fill the container.
The Goo is evaporating quite quickly.
Oh great! the Goo has evaporated.
SunInSpaceHigh: The Goo looks like a rainbow.
The Goo has made an official request for overtime pay.
You aren't sure if the Goo is tanning or not.
The Goo moves in bubbles around the container. It's very mesmerizing. You could watch it for hours...

Out of the corner of your eye you could've sworn the Goo turned into two paddles and a ball, but when you look straight at the monitor, sadly, there is no pong game to pass the endless hours in space.
Without warning, the Goo begins changing shapes. As it shifts and warps you feel drawn to it, but the loud beeping of another module gets your attention. Where did the time go?
As hours turn into days, you begin to think the Goo is conspiring against you.
You really want to throw the Goo out into space, but the canister is almost unbreakable.

MohoSrfLanded*: The Goo seems to be trying to escape.
MohoSrfLandedCentralLowlands*: The Goo bakes into a substance suspiciously like hardened bubble gum.
MohoSrfLandedMinorCraters*: The Goo is getting very dusty. You wonder if it will sneeze.
The Goo has taken on a nice golden brown.
MohoSrfLandedCanyon*: The Goo is having way too much fun in the sun.
MohoInSpaceLow*: The Goo's response to the situation gives insight into Moho's composition and origin... probably.
It seems that the Goo canister is an excellent fusion reactor here. Oh... it stopped.
Much of the Goo evaporates, leaving behind small clumps of minerals.
MohoInSpaceHigh*: You swear you just heard the Goo asking for a cold drink.
The Goo wants to get closer to Moho, its newest friend.

EveSrfLandedPoles: You look at the Goo and think that you see an image of a Kerbal. When you look back again, all you see is the standard routine of being crushed under the atmospheric pressure.
EveSrfLandedMidlands: The Goo looks stressed. Perhaps it's under a lot of pressure?
EveSrfLandedExplodiumSea: The Goo is also looking purple here. What else is new?
EveSrfLandedLowlands: The Goo tries to escape, but can't seem to get up the sides of the container.
EveSrfLandedHighlands: The Goo sits calmly bubbling away, showing no signs of being affected by a magnetic field. Maybe Eve releases gas from its surface to replace the atmosphere stripped away by the solar wind.
EveSrfLandedImpactEjecta: Looking relieved, the Goo slumps to the bottom of the container.
EveSrfLandedWesternSea: The Goo is dense and viscous and sticks itself to the bottom of the canister.
EveSrfLandedOlympus: The Goo lumps up in the middle of the container. Is it afraid of falling out?
EveSrfLandedFoothills: The Goo has completely flattened against the bottom of the canister. A tendril extends slowly towards you, as if to say: "Can we go home now?"
EveSrfLandedShallows: The Goo has produced a cooler and offers you a tiny cold beer.
EveSrfSplashedPoles: The Goo is trying to beat back the sea from its container.
EveSrfSplashedExplodiumSea: Some local fluids have seeped into the container. The Goo vehemently avoids the stuff.
EveSrfSplashedLowlands: The Goo has donned a hazmat suit. Maybe you should too.
EveSrfSplashedImpactEjecta: The Goo tentatively reaches out to the liquid, but recoils back.
EveSrfSplashedCraterLake: The Goo appears to have merged with the liquid.
EveSrfSplashedWesternSea: The Goo has escaped into the purple liquid and seems to be waving goodbye as it floats away.
EveSrfSplashedEasternSea: The Goo breaks free of the canister, excited for a swim. Upon contact with the purple liquid, however, it quickly returns and curls up in the corner.
EveSrfSplashedShallows: A tiny wave washes over the canister. The Goo panics.
EveFlyingLow: The Goo now appears like a straw blown painting. It looks distinctly purple but so does everything else.
The Goo's structure is being blown and reformed by the wind as you fly.
EveFlyingHigh: The Goo is building its own rocket, not trusting you to bring it back home.
The Goo has either completely disappeared or been bleached purple.
EveInSpaceLow: The Goo indicates to you that it would prefer a different travel destination.
The Goo stares nervously at Eve.
EveInSpaceHigh: The Goo is staring towards the planet. Is it homesick?
The Goo is practicing turning purple to blend in.

GillySrfLanded: The Goo has clumped up into a spheroid and become slightly brittle.
You don't think the Goo has any trust in Gilly.
The Goo slowly bounces up and down in the canister; it isn't sure if its in space or on a moon.
GillySrfLandedMidlands: The Goo is acting like it's about to go home.
GillySrfLandedLowlands: The Goo spins gracefully in the micro-gravity.
GillySrfLandedHighlands: The Goo rolls placidly around the container.
GillyInSpaceLow: The Goo has turned into a hard cone. You wonder if it could hold some snacks?
For a brief moment, the Goo defies all known laws of physics and takes the shape of a 4-D hyper-cube. Attempting to comprehend this only succeeds in giving you a headache.
GillyInSpaceHigh: The Goo begins to spin around inside its canister, and it looks like its rapidly changing colors.

KerbinSrfLandedWater*: The Goo gravitates towards the seaward side of the container.
The Goo wants to go swimming in that water over there.
The Goo looks to be doing the "potty dance". It should have gone back on the launchpad.
KerbinSrfLandedGrasslands*: The Goo takes on the shape of a leaf.
The Goo turns green with envy.
The Goo greedily gobbles up dirt and plant matter. You make a mental note not to touch it.
KerbinSrfLandedHighlands*: The Goo is gurgling as if it is gasping for air in the thin atmosphere of the Highlands.
The Goo seems to enjoy rolling up and down the hills.
KerbinSrfLandedShores*: The Goo has attracted a small horde of worshipping crabs. Fascinating.
The Goo looks a little crispy there above the sands.
The Goo has a little umbrella sticking out, reminiscent of your favorite beverage.
KerbinSrfLandedMountains*: The Goo looks happy to be back on solid ground. Actually, so do you.
The Goo doesn't seem nearly as bothered as it should be by the fact that it's hanging off the side of a mountain.
The Goo seems slightly intimidated by the sheer size of the surrounding mountains.
KerbinSrfLandedDeserts*: The Goo seems to hate it here.
The Goo appears to be drying up here. Maybe all this hot, dry weather has something to do with it.
The Goo appears not to be wet. Likely due to lack of water.
KerbinSrfLandedBadlands*: As the goo churns in its container you are reminded of a horror movie where an amorphous goo devours everything in its path. Hope this experiment ends better than the movie.
You try to decide which terrifies you more: Mystery Goo or the Badlands. You break down and cry instead.
The Mystery Goo explodes. Badlands landing confirmed.
KerbinSrfLandedTundra*: The Goo challenges you to a snowball fight. You cheat and use your opposable thumbs to great advantage.
The Goo seems to have shrunk into a pea size ball. You consider eating it, but decide to just throw it outside the door instead.
The Goo shrivels in the harsh wind and asks you to close the canister.
KerbinSrfLandedIceCaps*: The Goo starts to freeze. It doesn't seem to like it.
The Goo tentatively slides itself across the ice. It doesn't know how to stop so you have to go and pick it up.
The Goo hides in a corner of its container with some hot koffee.
KerbinSrfLandedNorthernIceShelf*: Bits of ice and snow become lodged in the Goo as you open the container. The Goo doesn't seem too happy about that.
The Goo's temperature suddenly drops several dozen degrees below ambient, as if proving it could do better.
KerbinSrfLandedSouthernIceShelf*: The Goo shivers as you open the container. You could swear you hear it say "Hey! Shut the door!"
The Goo decides that the best way to stay warm is by being inside your spacesuit. You aren't too happy about this arrangement.
KerbinSrfSplashedWater*: The Goo dives underwater and comes back with a little jellyfish buddy.
The Goo seems to be soaking up the water like a sponge.
You douse the Goo in shark repellent and tell it to spread itself out around the craft.
The Goo escapes into the water!
The Goo is freshly hydrated and starts to dissolve.
In an effort to remain invisible, the Goo turns itself into a fish. A fish shape. A fishy shape. It looks like a fish.
KerbinSrfSplashedGrasslands*: The Goo does not like being covered in muddy water.
KerbinSrfSplashedShores*: The Goo seems to turn into jelly and dissolve. Not very appetizing.
The Goo wobbles around in its container and starts trying to seep out of the holes.
The Goo is scaring away all the seagulls. You give it a little high-five.
KerbinSrfSplashedDeserts*: The goo reached out tentatively, as though not believing the water is real.
KerbinSrfSplashedBadlands*: The Goo is trying its best to not float out of its container. It must really want to stay with the ship.
KerbinSrfSplashedTundra*: The Goo attempts to dissolve, but recoils from the cold water.
KerbinSrfSplashedIceCaps*: The Goo hurriedly plugs the holes of its containers to keep the cold water out.
KerbinSrfSplashedNorthernIceShelf*: The Goo seems very unhappy at its dunking in the icy water.
KerbinSrfSplashedSouthernIceShelf*: The Goo seems curious about the icy slush in the water.
KerbinFlyingLow*: The Goo doesn't like the prospect that you might fly over the Badlands. Surprise, surprise.
The Goo is crammed into one side of the canister as the speeds increase.
You hear a faint, distorted guitar riff coming from The Goo.
The Mystery Goo almost seems...eager. You decide you don't really want to know why.
The Goo swishes around its container. You stop yourself from taking a sip despite the lack of onboard drinks.
The Goo is a jiggly pyramid.
The Goo seems to be scared of heights.
A particularly stupid bird slams into your craft and is quickly absorbed by the Goo.
The Goo seems disappointed in your flying skills.
The surface looks like it could use a drink. You wonder what would happen if you pour the Goo out the window.
The Goo seems to be holding on to the edge of the container as if it is afraid to fall out.
The Goo ripples its surface in a fair imitation of the terrain below.
A bird flies into the Goo, which disturbs it rather violently.
The Goo seems to be enjoying the refreshing breeze now that you opened the container.
The Goo holds on to the container lid for dear life. It appears as though you are caught in a life-or-death struggle.
The Goo seems to be eyeing the mountains with both fear and longing. You both hope that the SAS keeps working.
The Goo wants to belly flop into the ocean below. You manage to close the canister just in time.
KerbinFlyingHigh*: The Mystery Goo becomes depressed as you fly towards the Badlands. You want to give it a comforting hug then remember why it's a bad idea.
The Goo seems to be shrinking away from the sight of the landscape below. You don't know if this is normal, or if you should be feeding it more snacks.
The Goo is...definitely doing something. You scribble something to that effect in your report, serene in the knowledge that you're not the one who has to read it.
You wonder what would happen if you made an entire mountain out of Goo.
The Goo slowly jiggles in its container as the thin atmosphere buffets its surface.
You wonder what would happen if you fill one of those valleys with Goo. For science.
The Goo is thrilled to feel the wind whipping through its hair. Its hair? But it's Goo, it doesn't have hair. In any case, the Goo seems ecstatic.
Mystery Goo-based precipitation, now there's a thought. Did you just solve Kerbin's desertification problem?
The Goo appears to be heating up. A lot.
The Goo tries to fly away but since it lacks wings or thrusters, it fails.
You regard the Goo uneasily as it seems to be stretching out in the container as if it is reaching for space. Or maybe it's just imitating a blade of grass.
*singing* 'Bad, Bad, Leroy Brown. Badder than the...'. Oh yeah, science. The mystery goo remains dark and mysterious.
The Goo has formed into a small pocket mold at the base of the container.
The Goo is wearing sunglasses. You wonder where it got them from.
The Goo sends a little piece of itself flopping over the side. Someone on the ground is about to get a little surprise.
You pull the Goo out of its container and wring it out like a sponge. It doesn't look happy with you.
The Goo makes a fair imitation of the waves far below.
KerbinInSpaceLow*: The Mystery Goo radiates menace as you pass near the Badlands.
The Goo looks particularly dark and brooding here.
The globules of the Goo wobble as they bounce around inside the canister.
You hope taxpayers won't find out how much observing the Goo in space costs.
The Goo seems to have happily curled into a ball, utterly uninterested in the planet below.
The Goo seems to have formed into the shape of a sphere.
The Goo suddenly forms itself into a scale replica of Kerbin, accurately reflecting the visible biomes below. You didn't know it could do that.
The Goo flickers once every few minutes as stray cosmic rays strike the canister.
The Goo is forming into the shape of an odd looking egg.
You contemplate the Goo as the Goo contemplates the kerbals on the surface contemplating you. Strange.
The Goo takes this opportunity to shake the sand out of its fur. Wait...since when did the Goo have fur?!
The Goo seems to be eyeing the snack you are holding. "Bad Goo, that's my tasty snack!"
The Goo is playing with the rock you brought from the Highlands.
The Goo seems to be leaking out of the container. You knew we should have used titanium for those things.
The Goo is holding on to the inside of the container, and you're unable to open it. You record the Goo as being "uncooperative".
The Goo seems to be trying to imitate the shape of a mountain. You wonder why.
You and the Goo sit in companionable silence as you gaze out over Kerbin's mountaintops. Then you shove it back inside its canister before it makes your capsule slimy.
The Goo makes a splashing sound as you open the container, and you are reminded of the Shores of Kerbin.
You tell the Goo stories about your fun beach adventures. It decides to share its own but you can't understand what it's saying.
The Goo immediately stops what it's doing when you peek inside the canister. Very suspicious.
The Goo seems to be cavitating at 33.47652 MHz. Strange. You wonder what that means.
As the Goo enjoys its new-found freedom as you pass over Kerbin's Water.
KerbinInSpaceHigh*: Mystery Goo and Badlands seem like a bad combination to you. Rather than opening a Pandora's box, you forgo the experiment and make up some data to send back to KSC.
Dispersing Mystery Goo in space high above the Badlands is not your idea of a good work safety practice.
You wonder what would happen if you mixed the Goo with Koolaid powder. An experiment for another day.
As you spot Kerbin's oceans from the viewport, you decide to tell the Goo an old joke about a one-armed pirate and his pet jellyfish.
You let the Goo out. It seems to like space as much as you do. Maybe it wants to grow up to be an astronaut.
After having yet another box of snacks you decide to check on the Goo and find it spilled all over the floor. See, this is why we can't have nice things.
You look at the Goo pensively, wondering what secrets its inky depths hold. And whether there are any snacks in there.
You and the Goo keep each other company as you pass high over Kerbin's land and sea.
As you stare blankly at the Goo you wonder if there is anti-Goo somewhere, spinning in the opposite direction.
As you open the Goo canister high over Kerbin's mountain ranges, you worry that it might not want to go back in this time.
The Goo seems to pulse with dull flashes of colour. Is it mimicking an aurora?
The Goo seems happy, but mildly reticent of your current location.
You find your hand covered in the Goo after you realize you were daydreaming about snacks again. You sure hope this stuff washes off.
You join the Goo in fraternal contemplation of the majesty of the Highlands from outer space.
The Goo waves goodbye to the planet of its birth. Not to be outdone, you do the same, only with both hands.
The Goo seems to be leaning out of the container to get a better view of the surface below.
The Goo seems to be taking a perverse satisfaction at the lifelessness of outer space. You nervously try to stay out of its sight.
The Goo appears to vibrate merrily.
The Goo is feeling real chill.
You poke the Goo, and some of it gets stuck to your finger. You quickly rub it off on your spacesuit, glad that you don't have to clean up at the end of this mission.
The Goo seems fine with being high over Kerbin.
KerbinSrfLandedKSC*: The goo lunges towards the nearest dumpster, perhaps the one that the goo can came from in the first place.
The Goo has given up and is heading home.
The goo seems to be giving you a funny look.
The Goo seems bored.
The Goo looks as if it is a puddle of water.
The Goo needs to go back to the lab.
KerbinSrfLandedAdministration*: The Goo wants to go the other way.
KerbinSrfLandedAstronautComplex*: You convince the new recruits that tasting the Goo is a rite of passage.
KerbinSrfLandedBaikerbanur*: The Goo turns its nose up at the place.
KerbinSrfLandedBaikerbanurLaunchpad*: The Goo asks to be launched from a proper launchpad.
KerbinSrfLandedFlagPole*: The Goo looks up the pole in Awe.
KerbinSrfLandedLaunchPad*: The Goo doesn't seem to be doing much right now.
The Goo doesn't seem to be doing much right now. Maybe you should poke it with a stick.
The Goo seems very excited for what's about to happen.
The Goo is now a little nervous.
The Goo wants to go somewhere
The Goo appears to be wondering what the next few minutes are going to entail.
The Goo appears to have braced itself for high g-forces.
Don't worry, The Goo won't do anything right now. It'll just sit there.
KerbinSrfLandedMissionControl*: The Goo seems enamored with all the flashing lights on the control panels.
KerbinSrfLandedRnD*: The Goo looks forward to researching the researchers.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDCentralBuilding*: The Goo looks around inquisitively at all the eyes peering down at it.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDCornerLab*: The Goo gets down to business doing its own research.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDMainBuilding*: The Goo contemplates the meaning of Kerbalhood.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDObservatory*: The Goo observes you observing it. Eerie.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDSideLab*: The good sloshes to the side of its container in honor of the research that has been performed here in the past.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDSmallLab*: The Goo looks cramped here, like it needs to stretch its...uhhhh...self. Yeah, that's it: stretch itself.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDTanks*: The Goo promptly begins determining its compatibility with the substance in the tanks.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDWindTunnel*: The Goo is promptly splattered against the wall as you open the container. What did you expect, it's a wind tunnel!
KerbinSrfLandedRunway*: The Goo seems to behave very much the same as it always does around here.
The Goo has a black tone as light reflects off the runway into the canister.
KerbinSrfLandedSPH*: The engineers at the SPH spread some of the Goo on the control surfaces, in hopes of actually making their plane fly.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHRoundTank*: The Goo contemplates how much of the tank it could fill.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHTanks*: The Goo splits itself into pieces and hides in the tanks.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHWaterTower*: The Goo does indeed seem a bit watered down here.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStation*: The Goo seems indifferent to this place despite you muttering under your breath.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishEast*: The Goo sticks to the dish as you spread it over the surface.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishNorth*: You toss the entire container of Goo at the dish, and it passes completely through, leaving a gaping hole.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishSouth*: The Goo seems to have worked its way into the wiring of the dish and shorted it out. No big loss.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationHub*: You threaten to "unleash" the Goo on the first staff member here who looks at you sideways.
KerbinSrfLandedVABMainBuilding*: As you open the container the Goo gets covered in old bits of rocket parts and pieces of blueprints.
KerbinSrfLandedVABPodMemorial*: The Goo seems somber. Or maybe inspired. It's hard to tell.
KerbinSrfLandedVABRoundTank*: The Goo comments that its container is far more round than the tank.
KerbinSrfLandedVABSouthComplex*: The Goo sees one of its own inside, and tries to hatch a breakout scheme.
KerbinSrfLandedVABTanks*: The Goo seems oddly defensive around the tanks here.
KerbinSrfLandedIslandAirfield*: The Goo want to take off.
KerbinSrfLandedWoomerang*: The Goo thinks the place is cute.
KerbinFlyingLowKSC*: The Goo hangs on for dear life.
KerbinFlyingHighKSC*: The Goo airs itself out in the cool breeze of the upper atmosphere.

MunSrfLanded: The Goo appears to be in the same state of awe as you are.
MunSrfLandedMidlands: The Goo is as giddy as a schoolgirl.
MunSrfLandedNorthernBasin: The Goo wonders where it can wash its hands. This is a basin, right?
MunSrfLandedEastCrater: The Goo sits there contentedly. You're not sure how an inanimate Goo can be content, but that really is the only way to describe it.
MunSrfLandedNorthwestCrater: You call the Goo a goober. It completely ignores you. So does Mission Control.
MunSrfLandedSouthwestCrater: The Goo seems to be imitating the paparazzi, following you around and constantly flashing like it's taking pictures of you.
MunSrfLandedFarsideCrater: The Goo is happy to be on the far side of anything. Especially if it isn't actively crashing into it.
MunSrfLandedPolarCrater: The Goo seems to have split in two and moved to opposite ends of its container.
MunSrfLandedPoles: The Goo shows signs of bipolar behavior.
The Goo has become a slightly tubular structure.
MunSrfLandedPolarLowlands: The Goo seems to be trying to do the limbo under one of the landing struts. Strange stuff, that Goo.
MunSrfLandedHighlands: The Goo just hangs out in its container. Even when you poke it with your finger.
MunSrfLandedHighlandCraters: The Goo rolls around and around the rim of the crater, slowly moving toward the lowest part in the center.
MunSrfLandedMidlandCraters: The Goo is no less gooey here than elsewhere. Strange stuff.
MunSrfLandedEastFarsideCrater: The Goo seems to have gotten dirty. Did anyone bring a duster?
MunSrfLandedTwinCraters: The Goo is bouncing from one crater to the other like flubber.
MunInSpaceLow: The Goo seems as excited as you are at being this close to the Mun.
The Goo melts into a welcoming ball.
The surface of the Goo has become covered with little craters.
The Goo demands a medal for being the first non-Kerbal in EVA around the Mun.
The Goo looks like a bowl as it imitates the crater below.
The Goo ripples and jiggles and suddenly forms itself into a remarkably accurate miniature Mun.
The Goo cowers in its container, afraid that your ship may be the next addition to the crater below.
In today's experiment, you will observe the Goo to see if it can spontaneously reproduce. Hmm this could take a while...
The Goo challenges you to a race to the munar surface! You laugh and lock it back inside its canister. Little do you know, a small piece of it has snuck into your suit...
The Goo demands to be taken seriously and you dutifully refrain from snorting juice out of your nose every time it tries to dance.
The Goo is obscuring the viewport and refusing to move. You have no choice but activate the specially-installed windshield wipers.
The Goo splats itself across the viewport, shocking you out of your reverie.
The Goo immediately makes a break for the munar surface. However, its blob form doesn't seem too suited for the job and it just ends up floating helplessly some distance from the craft.
The Goo has decided that your cause is worthy, and has committed to your quest to one day land on the Mun. +1 companion.
You explain to the Goo that it should be honoured it was chosen for this historic journey. It seems to stand a little straighter.
The Goo is trying to unlatch the airlock. You manage to stop it just in time.
The Goo scoffs at your pathetic attempts to understand its nature.
The Goo insists on a gluten-free diet. You promise it that all snacks on board are indeed free of those horrible, nasty gluten things.
The Goo is quick to adjust to munar orbit gravity. Quicker than you, in fact.
MunInSpaceHigh: You very briefly consider filling one of those canyons with Goo.
The Goo seems to enjoy itself.
It's amusing to watch The Goo bounce around.
The Goo is really enjoying itself. You decide to let it romp around for a few more minutes while you have a snack.
The Goo seems to be quivering with excitement.
The Goo is lonely and asks for a hug.
The Goo is bouncing off the walls. Who knows why.
The Goo stares into your very soul. This is gonna be an awkward flight.
Hmm the Goo seems to react well to various sound patterns. You decide to play some music and are utterly shocked when it gets up and starts doing the Carlton.
The Goo has found your hidden snack stash! Unfortunately it seems that its appetite matches your own.
The Mun appears to captivate the Goo's attention. Once can only guess what it thinks of this historic journey.
The Goo confides that it doesn't really believe that the Mun is made of cheese.
Your next experiment is an attempt to determine whether the Goo needs rest. This might take a while...
The Goo is snoring, so you quietly shut the canister and decide to take a nap yourself.
The Goo has eaten your snack! What part of "Do Not Touch", did it not understand?
The Goo has recently been showing signs of homicidal tendencies. You lock it back in its canister and hope for the best.
The Goo doesn't want to eat its vegetables. Mission Control advises you to use the "Here comes the airplane" approach.
The Goo is completely inactive, even with applied stimulation. Did you bring the right canister?

MinmusSrfLanded*: The Goo starts to bounce slowly from top to bottom.
The Goo begins to resemble a particularly delicious bowl of rocky minty ice cream you had once. You slap yourself out of it.
MinmusSrfLandedHighlands*: The Goo turns into a scale model of Minmus, and indicates the landing site with a little X.
MinmusSrfLandedLowlands*: Even The Goo seems to want a taste of the minty surface.
MinmusSrfLandedFlats*: The Goo is erupting into dance!
MinmusSrfLandedGreatFlats*: The Goo appears to be... licking its lips? You recheck the oxygen levels in your suit.
MinmusSrfLandedGreaterFlats*: The Goo flops out of the container and becomes indistinguishable from the surface.
MinmusSrfLandedLesserFlats*: The Goo seems less flat than before.
MinmusSrfLandedPoles*: The Goo seems to be doing an impression of a snow globe.
MinmusSrfLandedSlopes*: The Goo looks hungry. Very hungry.
MinmusInSpaceLow*: The Goo sticks to the Minmus side of the container. It appears to be attracted to the tiny moon. You wonder if The Goo is hungry too.
The Goo looks greener than ever.
The Goo gives you a letter to give to its wife back on Kerbin in case it doesn't make it.
MinmusInSpaceHigh*: You feel like eating the Goo.
"Mission Control, the Goo is sitting on my face-plate and won't get off. Please advise."

DunaSrfLanded: The Goo has gotten red dust all over itself.
DunaSrfLandedPoles: The Goo shrinks from the openings in its container and sticks to the shadows at the back. Maybe it's dazzled by the ice too?
DunaSrfLandedMidlands: The Goo seems to have spattered about the container during the landing.
The Goo looks bored, as it requesting to be taken to a more interesting part of Duna.
DunaSrfLandedLowlands: The strong winds from a passing dust devil ripple the exposed Goo. You think it's enjoying it.
DunaSrfLandedCraters: The Goo rocks from side to side, as though on a boat (or, in this case, a ship).
The Goo begins to faintly glow and almost crackles with energy. It seems to be energised by this place.
DunaSrfLandedHighlands: Here the Goo is turning red and angry.
The Goo adjusts itself on the slope so it is always level.
DunaSrfLandedMidlandSea: The Goo looks bored here. So are you.
DunaSrfLandedNorthernBasin: The Goo arranges itself into a ring-shaped blob.
DunaSrfLandedSouthernBasin: The Goo looks to be shivering, and has gone pink.
DunaSrfLandedNorthernShelf: The Goo looks to be cowering in a corner of its container. Maybe it's unsettled by the wind as well.
The Goo must have battened down the hatches and hid, since there's no sign of it.
DunaSrfLandedMidlandCanyon: The Goo goes as red as the ground.
The Goo puts on a helmet. You wonder why.
DunaSrfLandedEasternCanyon: The Goo turns as red as the surrounding dust clouds.
The Goo looks to be wearing a facemask. How it got in there is a mystery (it's not called 'mystery' for nothing), but it sure knows its priorities.
DunaSrfLandedWesternCanyon: The Goo turns a reddish colour, like it has everywhere else.
The Goo looks impressed with something.
DunaSrfLandedPolarHighlands: The Goo slowly rotates, catching what little dust is in the air at these latitudes.
The Goo staunchly avoids one side of the container. Did we pick the wrong pole?
DunaSrfLandedPolarCraters: The Goo turns the same colour as the ice.
DunaFlyingLow: The red color excites The Goo.
DunaFlyingHigh: The Goo looks longingly homeward. At least, you think that's what it's doing.
DunaInSpaceLow: The Goo has split into lots of little Goos. They appear to be voting on a leader.
The Goo likes it here. It's like a second home for The Goo!
DunaInSpaceHigh: The Goo is forming into geometric shapes! Sphere, pyramid, cube, diamond... Is that a face? It's your face!

DresSrfLandedRidges*: The Goo pulls a rabbit out of a hat. You start clapping, before realizing the Goo has neither a hat or a rabbit.
DresSrfLandedCanyons*: The Goo trembles. Is it fear?
DresInSpaceHigh*: The Goo seems to look for a way to escape.

JoolFlyingLow*: The Goo looks VERY concerned.
The Goo has taken on a greenish hue as it flubs about the container.
The Goo does not appear content with the current situation.
The Goo container has imploded and the goo is hanging on to the ship.
JoolFlyingHigh*: The Goo begins to bubble.
The way the Goo jiggles under Joolian winds reminds you of how your cheeks jiggle during launch.
The Goo appears to be taking down observations about you.
JoolInSpaceLow*: The Goo seems to be at peace with itself.
You feel malicious intent emanating from the Goo.
JoolInSpaceHigh*: The Goo seems to be moving more slowly here, almost as if it needed the warmth of the Sun.
The Goo tries to inflate and appears larger than the planet. It clearly has no sense of scale.

LaytheSrfLanded: The Goo seems excited by the climate.
LaytheSrfLandedPoles: The Goo seems excited. Perhaps it wants to swim in the ocean?
LaytheSrfLandedShores: The Goo bubbles and burps a little.
LaytheSrfLandedDunes: The Goo twists into strange and frightening forms, possibly due to a strong magnetic field on this moon.
LaytheSrfLandedPeaks: The Goo is forming shapes that mimic the terrain nearby. You conclude it likes this location.
LaytheSrfSplashed: The Goo is wet and cheerful.
LaytheSrfSplashedPoles: The Goo shivers and tries to avoid the water.
LaytheSrfSplashedShores: It appears that the Goo wants to do some sunbathing. Who gave it the sunscreen?
LaytheSrfSplashedTheSagenSea: The Goo has decided to go for a short swim.
It bounces around from excitement... or it might be just the waves.
LaytheFlyingLow: You note that the mystery goo Containment Unit is wobbling a little. Is that the Goo? Or is it just the screws coming loose?
The Goo seems to be shivering. Perhaps the draft is making it cold?
LaytheFlyingHigh: There does not seem to be much activity coming from the Containment Unit. Only close examination reveals a slight pulsing.
The Goo is pulsing gently. It is 15% more radioactive than when in Laythe's atmosphere.
LaytheInSpaceLow: The Goo seems right at home in space.
LaytheInSpaceHigh: As you peek inside the Goo Containment Unit, it pops up from the bottom, surprising you.

VallSrfLanded*: The Goo oscillates once, then three times, then finally nine times.
VallInSpaceLow*: The Goo sparkles in the dim light.
VallInSpaceHigh*: The Goo turns blue and hard, but fluctuates internally.

TyloSrfLanded: The Goo has a warm, slightly bluish glow. According to the manual, this means it's either been exposed to some weird particles or it's just happy to be in one piece.
TyloSrfLandedHighlands: The Goo resembles a green pancake.
TyloSrfLandedLowlands: The Goo has no confidence in your ability to return home.

BopSrfLanded: You ponder what the Goo is. Only here have you ever thought this.
BopSrfLandedPeaks: The Goo turns into an odd, green-colored ball.
BopInSpaceLow: The Goo has assumed a greenish shape with multiple extrusions.
The Goo seems agitated and attempts to escape its container.
BopInSpaceHigh: The Goo seems to try to get your attention.
The Goo bops you playfully on the head.

PolSrfLandedPoles: The Goo seems to be allergic to its surroundings.
PolSrfLandedLowlands: You wonder if the Goo has more poles than it did before.
PolSrfLandedHighlands: The Goo starts buzzing like a bumblebee.
PolSrfLandedMidlands: The Goo has assumed a flower shape.
PolInSpaceLow: The Mystery Goo resembles a grain of pollen almost identical to the Joolian Moon.
PolInSpaceHigh: The Goo twists itself into some sort of six-dimensional hyper-cube. You are glad that you became an astronaut instead of a scientist.

EelooSrfLanded*: The Goo has become many small grains, ideal for an Eeloo sundae.
The Goo appears to have shattered into many tiny frozen fragments upon touchdown.
The Goo walks out of the container, picks up a sample, and walks back in.
The Goo has wrapped itself up in one of our snack bags. Who has been feeding the Goo?
The Goo appears to be shivering.
EelooSrfLandedPoles*: The Goo quivers, rattling the container shut several times.
EelooSrfLandedNorthernGlaciers*: As the fine ice crystals kicked up by your landing on the surface slowly gather on the the top of the container, you gingerly tap at the Goo with your finger, expecting it to shatter at any moment.
EelooSrfLandedLowlands*: The Goo has taken the form of its container. It's not very good at it, however.
EelooSrfLandedHighlands*: The Goo is mysterious as ever.
EelooSrfLandedIceCanyons*: The ground seems to be somewhat reminiscent of Kerbin's mountains.
The sides of the container, for the first time, are clean! The Goo has gathered at the bottom, and looks very dusty.
EelooSrfLandedCraters*: While the Goo samples are processed, you realize that, after many days in space, you are hungry. The Goo looks all too much like the soup your grandma used to make.
EelooSrfLandedMidlands*: The Goo turns white. It seems to feel at home here.
EelooSrfLandedBabbagePatch*: The Goo has morphed into the word, "SCRAM." You feel a sudden urge to run into an elevator, before renembering that you don't have one.
EelooInSpaceLow*: The Goo seems to force its way down towards the planet, as if trying to give it a hug.
The Goo seems to be shaking due to the low temperatures. It almost makes you want to hug it...
The Goo looks like it's freezing solid.
The Goo looks like it is making a high-pitched note of anguish, but there is no sound in space so you can't be sure.
EelooInSpaceHigh*: The Goo starts to float away. No Goo, come back!
The Goo feels like it's time to go home. Whichever direction home is.

seismicScan.description: A seismic reading of local ground activity.
seismicScan.hints: Detection of geological activity—major or minor—or small events such as landslides, or meteor impacts.

MohoSrfLanded*: A small rumble shakes the cockpit before you can access the sensor.
MohoSrfLandedNorthernSinkholeRidge*: The sensor picks up anomalous vibrations from deep within the planet. You wonder what could be down there.
MohoSrfLandedNorthernSinkhole*: Despite the tremendous heat of the sun, thermal expansion isn't causing any noteworthy movement.
MohoSrfLandedMidlands*: Thermal expansion from the sun is causing geological activity.
MohoSrfLandedCentralLowlands*: The meter shows the maximum amount of force needed to jiggle Jebediah's love-handles for a fortnight.
MohoSrfLandedHighlands*: This place seems much calmer than you would think. Tests indicate that there hasn't been any geologic activity in billions of years.
MohoSrfLandedMinorCraters*: The shaking of the needle reminds you how cold the dark side of this planet gets.
MohoSrfLandedCanyon*: There is a strong anomaly in the sensor that you can't determine the cause of. After much deliberation, you decide to ignore it.

EveSrfLandedPoles: It's very quiet up here. No local seismicity to be found.
Bored with the lack of results, you try to turn the seismometer into a makeshift heart monitor, only to realise how stressed you actually are.
EveSrfLandedMidlands: A strange echoing effect is apparent. It could be a sign of underground cavities buried deep beneath the surface.
It sure is a mess down there, judging from the very confusing reponse to pings.
EveSrfLandedExplodiumSea: The shifting currents below the surface induce very faint vibrations in the rock.
EveSrfLandedLowlands: The seismometer registers the creaks of the vessel as seismic events.
The device spits out an impressive array of sparks, but no data.
EveSrfLandedHighlands: The scan yields a great deal of important information, but none of it says "purple".
Mission Control tells you to put the boom box away so the readout doesn't consist of your jammin' beats.
EveSrfLandedPeaks: Very little activity is detected. The tectonic processes that formed the mountains must have long since ceased.
Some of those mountainsides look unstable. You hope an earthquake doesn't make them collapse while you're here.
EveSrfLandedImpactEjecta: Distorted seismic waves passing up through the rock outline the crater's breccia lens and shatter cones.
The scientists back at KSP would give their right hand to have been able to record the impact event on a seismograph.
EveSrfLandedCraterLake: The large circular lake causes seismic waves to reverberate and echo around the sea.
EveSrfLandedWesternSea: Waves slapping on the shore produce regular tiny pulses of movement.
EveSrfLandedOlympus: Whatever seismic waves may be found lower down, up here they're very muffled by the great bulk of the mountain.
A number of skittering rocks on the steep slopes produce a swarm of noisy signatures.
EveSrfLandedEasternSea: A small number of shallow, genuine earthquakes are found to be coming from the seafloor.
EveSrfLandedFoothills: The scan hints at the internal structure of Eve's crust.
As with any highly sensitive piece of scienctific equipment, the seismometer doesn't look like it will last much longer.
EveSrfLandedShallows: Can you use the seismometer to detect sea monsters bumping around in the ocean?

GillySrfLanded: The moon is so small and dead that the only noteworthy reading you get is an echo of your own landing bouncing back from the far side.
There is no activity on this barren rock.
GillySrfLandedMidlands: So far, the only seismic readings are those caused by you bouncing off the walls of the cabin.
GillySrfLandedLowlands: You fear your vessel has altered Gilly's course.
GillySrfLandedHighlands: Gilly seems to be seismically as dead as a doorknob.

KerbinSrfLandedWater: Wave impacts generate rythmic thumps on the seismograph.
KerbinSrfLandedGrasslands: The seismometer registers the gentle jostling of the blades of grass against it. Other than that it doesn't seem to be reading anything.
You wonder what it is about grass that keeps the earthquakes away.
You jump up and down a few times, just so the seismometer will have something to show to Mission Control.
KerbinSrfLandedHighlands: With such a massive mountain below your feet you have little hope of the seismometer registering a reading. So you absentmindedly munch on some snacks to pass the time.
Seismographic readings give hints of the turbulent geologic history of the Highlands.
You roll the seismometer down the hill and laugh at how confused it gets.
KerbinSrfLandedShores: The seismometer registers the force of waves hitting the shore and not much else.
The shallow water echoes vibrations from the muddy soil below.
Seismic waves reflecting within the subsurface reveal many layers of material comprising previous beaches from long ago.
KerbinSrfLandedMountains: The mountain seems far too large and sturdy to be shaken by anything, but you set up the sensor anyway to appease the folks back at KSC.
You send your seismic data back to KSC and glance nervously at the mountain beneath you. They'll tell you if there was an active volcano down there, right?
There's not much activity here beyond the odd rockfall. The titanic processes that built these great citadels of rock must have ceased aeons ago.
