KerbinSrfLandedDeserts: *singing* "I've been through the desert on a horse with no name, it feels good to be out of the rain". Oops! As you were singing you drop the accelerometer into one of the cracks in the dry ground, and can't reach it. Oh well, time to make something up for the scientists back at KSC again.
Seismic readings seem to indicate a large artificial structure directly underfoot. The lost tomb of an ancient Kerbal king perhaps?
KerbinSrfLandedBadlands: The ground here rumbles with constant activity. Maybe this is why they call it the Badlands.
So here we are, the center of all earthquakes on the entire planet. Or so the scientists say, but who believes them anyway with their fancy 'hypotheses' and 'equations'.
You wonder if there used to be a 'Goodlands' here before the earthquakes.
KerbinSrfLandedTundra: The seismometer is sensitive enough to detect the subtle tremors cause by the nearby massive ice sheets grinding against rock.
The seismometer trembles at the thought of being left here.
It appears there is normal seismic activity under the layers of dirt.
KerbinSrfLandedIceCaps: The seismometer registers the distant ground impact of an avalanche. You look around nervously.
One of the nearby glaciers appears to be moving rather fast, judging from all the seismic noise it's making.
KerbinSrfLandedNorthernIceShelf: The seismometer registers the rumbling, cracking sounds that you can hear coming from underfoot. You are amazed by the power that the ice holds.
Seismic readings indicate that the ice beneath is undergoing movement and stress on an enormous scale.
KerbinSrfLandedSouthernIceShelf: Given the fragile state of the ice, an earthquake would be particularly bad right about now.
A long line of tensional signatures near the edge of the ice shelf suggests a major calving event is about to take place.
KerbinSrfLandedKSC*: After a series of rather nominal readings there is a massive quake that nearly breaks the instruments. Either a spacecraft just attempted take-off or the scientists made another "discovery."
The incredibly sensitive equipment detects footsteps of people in the VAB, and even Dilorf dropping a pitcher full of ice water. You decide that's not your problem right now.
The needle jumps and shakes violently; moments later, you hear a distant explosion. Looks like somebody let Bill into the cafeteria kitchen again.
The lack of geologic activity - explosions not counting - reminds you why they built the space center here.
The instrument indicates heavy surface-borne activity nearby.
The instrument indicates high levels of seismic activity coming from...the research lab.
KerbinSrfLandedAdministration*: The needle moves. Mortimer probably exploded again over the spending of funds.
KerbinSrfLandedAstronautComplex*: You try to impress the new recruits with the word seismometer, but you're not sure if it worked.
KerbinSrfLandedBaikerbanur*: The seismometer registers a boulder rolling towards the centre from the mountain. Perhaps this is why they moved to Cape Kerbal.
KerbinSrfLandedBaikerbanurLaunchpad*: The vessel jolts very slightly as the decaying concrete slowly crumbles under it.
KerbinSrfLandedCrawlerway*: The Seismometer goes off the charts as the crawler slowly trundles by.
KerbinSrfLandedFlagPole*: The needle moves as you drop down the pole.
KerbinSrfLandedLaunchPad*: The ground trembles with the ghosts of failed launches past.
You wonder if repeated rocket launches have caused the ground to become unstable.
KerbinSrfLandedMissionControl*: All the stomping around here registers clearly on the seismometer.
KerbinSrfLandedRnD*: The R&D scientists disassemble the seismometer for parts.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDCentralBuilding*: You miss the reading while explaining to one of the scientists how this thing works.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDCornerLab*: You stuff the seismometer into the corner, and hope that no one remembers it.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDMainBuilding*: The R&D scientists interpret the data from the seismometer in real time, and you realize maybe this science stuff really IS useful.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDObservatory*: Your observations are observed in the observatory by an observer.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDSideLab*: The seismometer falls on its side and breaks. Oh well, time for some snacks anyway.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDSmallLab*: Despite the cramped conditions the seismometer performs perfectly and registers: absolutely nothing.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDTanks*: You recruit some friends to bang on all the tanks, and the reading goes off scale.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDWindTunnel*: As usual, your instrument gets blown away here before you can take a reading. Why do you even try?
KerbinSrfLandedRunway*: The ground here conducts vibrations about as effectively as concrete. You report your findings to the tower, but they don't seem very impressed.
You calibrate the detector one more time before lifting off.
KerbinSrfLandedSPH*: The planes are too light to register anything on the seismometer here.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHMainBuilding*: Nope, still nothing. Let's have some snacks while we're bored.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHRoundTank*: You bang on the side of the tank, which registers on the seismometer.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHTanks*: You recruit some friends to bang on all the tanks, and the reading goes off scale.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHWaterTower*: You wonder if dumping all the water will make the needle move.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStation*: Nothing happening here. No big surprise.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishEast*: You consider what a waste of your time taking this measurement is.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishNorth*: Vibrations from the moving dish overwhelm everything. Why are we even taking this measurement?
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishSouth*: Ugh, more seismometer measurements. Maybe you'll be nicer to Gene next time.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationHub*: You throw the seismometer at the side of the building hoping that THAT might finally register a reading.
KerbinSrfLandedVAB*: All the activity at the VAB registers clearly on the seismometer.
KerbinSrfLandedVABMainBuilding*: You get distracted looking at all the rockets and completely forget about taking a reading here.
KerbinSrfLandedVABPodMemorial*: Reading: nominal. Yep, that's really it.
KerbinSrfLandedVABRoundTank*: You bang on the side of the tank, which registers on the seismometer.
KerbinSrfLandedVABSouthComplex*: Sawing and hammering from the workshop ripples out towards the sensor.
KerbinSrfLandedVABTanks*: You recruit some friends to bang on all the tanks, and the reading goes off scale.
KerbinSrfLandedIslandAirfield*: Waves hitting the island provide the majority of noise.
KerbinSrfLandedWoomerang*: The framework of the pad vibrates very slightly as the wind whistles through it.

MunSrfLanded: It just detected an impact somewhere on the Mun.
Nothing to worry about. Really!
MunSrfLandedMidlands: The Mun appears to be geologically dead. All you can detect are the slight tremors generated by meteorite impacts.
MunSrfLandedNorthernBasin: The sensor seems clogged from all the Munar dust. You give up and have a snack.
MunSrfLandedEastCrater: There's an odd signature coming from the crater to the west. You report it to Mission Control, hoping they'll be proud of you.
MunSrfLandedNorthwestCrater: The massive impact appears to have created a fault line.
MunSrfLandedSouthwestCrater: The quakes stop suddenly. You start to get alarmed, then realize you knocked the plug loose.
MunSrfLandedFarsideCrater: The readings here are solid, but they might be better if we could do them in a canyon.
MunSrfLandedCanyons: The readings indicate a massive rockfall nearby.
The periodic moon-quakes seem clear and easy to read here. The science team will want to see these numbers.
MunSrfLandedPolarCrater: The permanently shaded areas in the corners of the craters have slightly less seismic activity than the rest of the Mun.
MunSrfLandedPoles: You wonder how long it would take an impact at the south pole to register at the north pole.
MunSrfLandedPolarLowlands: The scans indicate a large series of quakes incoming! Oh wait, your hand brushed the sensor. False alarm.
MunSrfLandedHighlands: The instrument registers a small anomaly. You aren't sure if this was a genuine seismic event or just a false positive from you shuffling your feet.
MunSrfLandedHighlandCraters: The ground appears to have many small fractures from the multiple impacts, causing slight distortions in readings.
MunSrfLandedMidlandCraters: You carefully place the sensor down on the dusty surface and pat it on the top. 'Now there's a good little sensor.'
MunSrfLandedEastFarsideCrater: You set the seismometer down and it promptly tumbles down the embankment to the bottom of the crater. At least it registered something!
MunSrfLandedTwinCraters: You hope that the geology here will concentrate any minute vibrations from deep within the Mun.

MinmusSrfLanded*: You detect some seismic activity, most likely caused by tidal forces.
MinmusSrfLandedHighlands*: The lake beds rumble periodically. Perhaps the tidal forces are causing heating under all that ice.
MinmusSrfLandedFlats*: You detect somewhat violent shuddering somewhere on the opposite side of Minmus. It was likely caused by a small meteorite impact.
MinmusSrfLandedGreatFlats*: You watch the data as it clearly shows that nothing interesting is happening at all.
MinmusSrfLandedGreaterFlats*: The seismometer detects some minor quakes, and what sounds like bubbling. Perhaps these areas are resurfaced by occasional geological activity.
A large impact nearby sends shockwaves throughout the planet. This is a perfect opportunity to map the interior!
MinmusSrfLandedLesserFlats*: The vibrations indicate there's some rock under all that ice.
MinmusSrfLandedSlopes*: A boulder rolling down the neighboring slope interferes with your readings.
There's a lot of static on the sensor feed. Are we sliding down the hill?

DunaSrfLanded: The planet doesn't seem to have much seismic activity.
DunaSrfLandedPoles: The seismic scan picks up some vibrations as gas vents from the ice cap underneath.
The scan detects some activity, probably associated with melting of the polar cap.
DunaSrfLandedMidlands: The readings indicate a solid iron core. This planet might have had a functioning geodynamo in the distant past before its core fully cooled.
A refracted ping is recieved from an antipodal mountain. This reveals a still partially-molten interior. Could the mountain be a dormant volcano?
DunaSrfLandedLowlands: The instrument detects some very faint tremors. Maybe Duna isn't so dead after all?
Nothing. Zilch. Nada. The only vibrations have come from you dropping a snack.
DunaSrfLandedCraters: Not much interesting happens when you run the seismic scan. Maybe the planet is geologically dead, or perhaps it is just having a nap.
The ground is still and silent, like everywhere else on this freezing rustball. You wonder what the impact would have felt like.
DunaSrfLandedHighlands: The seismograph barely responds. If this planet ever had seismic activity, it doesn't any more.
Tiny vibrations from skittering rocks on the surface are all that show up on the equipment.
DunaSrfLandedMidlandSea: The libration of Ike overhead generates almost undetectable tremors from ground movement akin to tides.
The entire planet looks to have flatlined, or at least the seismograph has.
DunaSrfLandedNorthernBasin: Pings sent down into the bedrock are scattered and refracted in all directions, indicating the presence of extensive rock faulting and fracturing.
There's no detectable activity here, which means no active tectonics in the area.
DunaSrfLandedSouthernBasin: Cracking and shifting ice in the basin creates some weak, shallow waves.
The sparse seismic activity seems to peak during the day, as though the nearby ice melting is resposible for shockwaves.
DunaSrfLandedNorthernShelf: The wind rattles the seismometer so much it's impossible to get a reading. Apparently, this is a magnitude VI dunaquake, if such a thing exists.
In amongst all the vibration noise, the meter picks up a tiny rumble from deep beneath the ground. Could this place be a relic of an ancient fault line?
DunaSrfLandedMidlandCanyon: The ground is perfectly still here, save for some vanishingly faint pings from the other side of the planet.
Ike's libration overhead generates regular swarms of tiny movements in the crust.
DunaSrfLandedEasternCanyon: What's that? Did everyone feel that? The seismometer certainly did, and we appear to have landed in a rift valley zone.
After a while, the sensor has detected no less than eleven small quakes. Duna is still active after all!
DunaSrfLandedWesternCanyon: Not a peep. Either Duna is dozing, or it really is seismially dead.
Discouraged, you leave the sensor running for some time. When you return, the only vibrations are from your feet and the wind.
DunaSrfLandedPolarHighlands: The scattering of seismic pings sent down suggests the presence of multiple ice layers and rock far below.
Your scan records a few small tremors. Hopefully there are no Duna dinosaurs.
DunaSrfLandedPolarCraters: The detector picks up the vibrations of ice cracking and shifting beneath the ship. It's quite disconcerting.
Not much, apart from some ice noises. Either Duna is seismically dead or ice is a good shock absorber.

DresSrfLanded*: Seismic sensors show that this dwarf planet has little geologic activity. You wonder if detonating a fuel tank would somehow remedy the situation.
DresSrfLandedPoles*: There is a faint pulsating vibration, like a heartbeat. But it's not a heartbeat, because that would be crazy.
DresSrfLandedHighlands*: You tap the ground a few times. Yep, it's on. This planet is just dead.
DresSrfLandedCanyons*: There's no internal movement. Aside from the impacts of meteors, the needle never moves.

LaytheSrfLanded: The seismic graph is going crazy. The Joolian system seems to be subjecting Laythe to an unholy chiropractic session.
You notice the ground starting to shake. You might want to leave soon.
Laythe's surface stretches and flexes under the strain of tidal forces from Jool.
The sensor is detecting heavy seismic activity deep below the surface. Perhaps Laythe has previously undetected volcanic activity. Only time will tell.
You've set the craft right on top of a fault line! You find this both fascinating and absolutely terrifying.
Laythe shows some very important seismic activity. You're not sure the craft can survive a tsunami, but at least it keeps the moon temperate.
The readings fluctuate dramatically in conjunction with the waves. Perhaps it would be better to run this experiment on land.
LaytheSrfLandedPoles: Fluctuations within the planet's core are detected as signs of the tidal forces exerted by Jool.
LaytheSrfLandedShores: The detector picks up a seismic anomaly! You feel shaken.
Waves breaking on the shore is interfering with the sensitivity of your instrument.
LaytheSrfLandedDunes: Seismic scan reports distant booms, both under the ocean and on the surface. They seem to be volcanoes.

VallSrfLanded*: You detect some underground tremors. You decide they are caused by cryovolcanic events.
VallSrfLandedMidlands*: This data would be very valuable in advancing science if the screen hadn't iced over.
VallSrfLandedHighlands*: The seismic scan indicates that there is a sudden density change a significant distance below the surface.
VallSrfLandedLowlands*: Wow, what a quake! Something big must have just shifted underground.
VallSrfLandedNortheastBasin*: Using the power of electricity, the screen shows nothing. Not even if you believe.

TyloSrfLanded: This moon appears to be mostly inactive geologically. You detect little to no activity.
TyloSrfLandedHighlands: Periodic quakes roll through the moon, indicating the presence of massive cave networks.

BopSrfLanded: The seismometer briefly goes off the charts, then seems to decide that Bop is geologically dead.
Some vibrations have been detected under your vessel. You start to think about the creepy stories your friends have told you.
Bop seems to be impacted often... what is hitting it?
BopSrfLandedPeaks: The small moon is resonating with an odd musical frequency. Hooking the sensor feed up to some speakers, you recognize it as a toccata.
BopSrfLandedRidges: The planet gives off steady quakes which grow in intensity as Vall passes; as it leaves, they settle down again. This would be expected if the other moons did the same.
BopSrfLandedPoles: There are subtle vibrations that cannot be explained by Bop's physical characteristics.
BopSrfLandedSlopes: How can it be that Bop is this rock-steady for such a small moon?
BopSrfLandedValley: Among all the shakes and jitters, you notice a continual pulsing pattern. It makes you feel uneasy.

PolSrfLanded: Apart from the occasional Kerbal falling down the cliff, there is nothing for the seismometer to detect.
PolSrfLandedPoles: There seems to be a lot of seismic activity around Pol's poles.
PolSrfLandedLowlands: These results are gibberish! The interior of this little moon must be messier than Bob's office.
PolSrfLandedHighlands: After squinting at the device, you can still see vibrations from your landing on the screen, circling the (very tiny) globe.
PolSrfLandedMidlands: The sensor registers very weak vibrations, which could provide a clue as to why Pol is that crumbled up.

EelooSrfLanded*: There's a slight quake every now and then, but not much noteworthy activity.
There's not much seismic activity out here.
Some seismic activity suggests that the interior of Eeloo is not completely frozen.
EelooSrfLandedPoles*: Darn it, Jim! I'm a doctor, not a geologist! How do I know if there's going to be an Eeloo quake?
EelooSrfLandedLowlands*: Vibrations suggest that the surface of Eeloo is in fact moving over a liquid core.
EelooSrfLandedHighlands*: Was that a small tremor, or did the planet just shiver?
EelooSrfLandedIceCanyons*: The scans indicate that the ice below you is moving slightly.
EelooSrfLandedCraters*: The planet seems as still and dead as a rock right now. A large, frozen, ice-covered rock.
EelooSrfLandedMidlands*: The ice and rock appear to be shifting slightly, indicating some internal heating.

surfaceSample.description: A small sample collected directly from the surface of a celestial body.
surfaceSample.hints: Preliminary remarks on the physical nature and chemical composition of the soil or regolith.

MohoSrfLanded*: Yup, its a chunk of Moho.
Despite your belief you realize that Moho is in fact not a giant cookie. You hope you brought some medication.
MohoSrfLandedNorthPole*: The ground is made up of old lava flows, suggesting periods of intense volcanic activity.
MohoSrfLandedNorthernSinkholeRidge*: The sample shows signs of sodium, potassium and hydroxide. These are great resources for life support.
MohoSrfLandedNorthernSinkhole*: Ahah! You spot a familiar-looking rock a couple of meters away from you. It reminds you of the snack pack you've just eaten.
MohoSrfLandedMidlands*: Rocks lying around seem to be ejecta from volcanic eruptions.
MohoSrfLandedWesternLowlands*: You perform an analysis of the local soil composition.
MohoSrfLandedCentralLowlands*: The sample shows signs of scorch marks and intense heat, but you already knew that.
MohoSrfLandedHighlands*: The sample is very brittle and is covered with micro-cracks. It suggests that a super-heated atmosphere may have once existed here.
MohoSrfLandedMinorCraters*: Rocks all look the same to you. Wishing you had taken that geology course back on Kerbin, you try to break some stones by hitting them hard against each other. They seem to be very sturdy and don't break at all.
MohoSrfLandedSouthWesternLowlands*: The sample reveals complex artificial polymers, often used in reinforced fabrics and plastics.
MohoSrfLandedSouthEasternLowlands*: Get this to the lab, stat!
MohoSrfLandedCanyon*: The sample shows signs of past volcanic activity.
MohoSrfLandedSouthPole*: This will make a great gift for little Werner Von Kerman Jr.!

EveSrfLandedPoles: What could have possibly turned this soil purple?
The sample seems compliant, at least for now. You decide to keep it away from the power cabinet just in case.
EveSrfLandedMidlands: You accidentally drop the sample. Its fall seems cushioned by the atmosphere.
What could have possibly turned this soil purple?
EveSrfLandedLowlands: You reflect that returning this 1 kilogram rock to Kerbin will require well over a ton of rocket fuel. Is it worth it? Sure! It's purple!
The soil seems to be significantly heavier here.
EveSrfLandedHighlands: It appears that the upper layers of the surface are by far the most purple. Perhaps whatever causes this color falls as precipitation.
You analyze the sample that turns out to be an amethyst.
EveSrfLandedPeaks: The highlands regolith is loose and gravelly, and some stones are stained more purple than others.
You pick up a basaltic rock. It seems to be eroded by wind and not by liquid.
EveSrfLandedImpactEjecta: The surface sample reveals a lot of interesting chemical compounds. They suggest that much of the body that hit Eve burned up before reaching the planet's surface.
You pick up a rock. It feels slightly molten and has an eroded look to it.
EveSrfLandedCraterLake: You try to analyze the sample as thoroughly as possible, since it probably isn't going back.
EveSrfLandedWesternSea: Bringing this home will be an extraordinary challenge.
EveSrfLandedOlympus: The rock you pick off the ground seems to possess unnatural power.
The stone feels very warm in your hands as you turn it over and examine it.
EveSrfLandedEasternSea: You've come all this way for THIS?
EveSrfLandedFoothills: This sample glows in the dark. You think that you should use the lead-lined sample box.
You try to pick up a rock the size of a bouncy-ball, but it seems to weigh more than 5 Kerbals.
EveSrfLandedShallows: The sample seems to be contaminated with explodium, since it acts like an eraser.
EveSrfSplashedPoles: Whatever substance the liquid is looks very, very dangerous.
EveSrfSplashedExplodiumSea: You hope the sample canister can survive the pressure of Eve's atmosphere before you take it back home.
The surface sample just says "Something evil".
The pH of this liquid is "Out of measurement range".
EveSrfSplashedLowlands: This purplish liquid sample looks like your favorite soft drink. You are tempted to drink it.
EveSrfSplashedImpactEjecta: You try tasting your sample, and find that it is indeed not grape soda or anything that should come within a mile of any living thing.
EveSrfSplashedCraterLake: This liquid looks an awful lot like mercury now that you've collected it in a bottle. You hope your suit is as waterproof as the manual promised.
You find traces of liquid blutonium.
EveSrfSplashedWesternSea: Johnny was a chemist's son, but Johnny is no more; What Johnny thought was H2O, was H2SO4.
Further analysis will determine if this liquid is indeed grape-flavored, as you hope it is.
EveSrfSplashedEasternSea: You take a sample, and conclude that it is denser than Kerbin's core.
The purple water dyes the suit pink.
This isn't water, it is sulfur dioxide.
EveSrfSplashedShallows: The dense liquid is difficult to push the sample container into thanks to buoyancy.
The toxic mixture known as explodium should be toxic to all life, but you can't shake the feeling something is watching you under the surface.

GillySrfLanded: You grab a large boulder, at least twice your size, only to realize that it won't fit in the ship. You casually toss it off to the side and pick a more reasonable sample.
You accidentally toss the sample into the air and it sails off into space, so you grab another one.
This sample may contain the secrets of the formation of the Solar System! Or it might just be a bit of dirt and rocks...
Taking a surface sample in low gravity is kinda tricky. As you dig the shovel in, the reaction force sends you flying upwards.
Unfortunately, the surface is composed of loose rock of various sizes. It looks like you'll have to wait a while longer for that baked potato.
Carborundum - clear evidence that Gilly was once subjected to a whole lot of "boom boom".
Your Geiger counter clicks too many times to warrant keeping this.
You do a little show and tell with a huge boulder to Mission Control before they tell you to grab an actual sample.

You pick up a rock. It looks so dull that you throw it away in irritation. It soars into the skies, disappearing at the horizon. You wonder if it made an orbit until it hits you in the head from behind.
You pick up a rock, but you're so strong that the rock flies into space.
As you lift the rock, the dust underneath it reaches escape velocity.
GillySrfLandedMidlands: You could almost fit this 'moon' into the container.
The analyzer reports that there's carborundum found in the sample. With this we can upgrade our telescopes.
GillySrfLandedLowlands: You have a hard time picking up some dust samples as it flies out of your scoop and into the sky.
GillySrfLandedHighlands: You've found great amounts of carbon, but where are the diamonds?
The chondrules in the regolith remind you of white chocolate chip brownies.

KerbinSrfLandedWater: You seem to have captured a small crustacean. Mission Control is less than impressed.
The land surface near the water contains high amounts of water, salt and seaweed.
KerbinSrfLandedGrasslands: You wonder why you put on a two-million-dollar spacesuit and got into a multi-billion dollar rocket just to pick up some dirt.
Yep. Looks like dirt.
Beautiful green grass.
Yep. Still dirt.
This is some quality grass.
I didn't sign up for this.
An incredibly long, furry, purple creature slithers bizarrely out of the grass. You rub your eyes and when you look again there is nothing in sight.
I got a jar o' dirt! I got a jar o' dirt!

You pick at the dirt and discover a worm under the grass. You promptly name him Kelbin and attempt to hug him but end up squishing him in your arms. You feel bad so you open up a container and gently place him inside.
You notice that someone may have planted onions here once.
Years of training has brought kerbalkind here, to find this handful of soil! You hope you won't be fired for that.
The substance contains a remarkable amount of organic material. Excited, you inform Mission Control of this fact, but they seem less than thrilled.
You pick a likely-looking plant to put in a pot and be your companion as you journey across the stars.
You try to taste the ground, but realize that that would be silly. You kiss it instead, happy to be here alive, for once.
KerbinSrfLandedHighlands: Dirt, grass, and some small rocks. You suspect this isn't going to be much of a ground breaking discovery.
You discover dried out straw, lichens, and moss.
You find a tiny fleck of a shiny yellow material among the rocks. It seems that there is gold in these hills.
The rocks here are far more interesting than the ones you find elsewhere. You pick up a few to add to your rock collection.
KerbinSrfLandedShores: You pick up a small creature with far too many legs. Mission control denies your request to keep it as a pet.
You take a sample of the soil. It's very muddy and sandy. There might be a body of water nearby.
You scoop up some watery sand and decide the best use for it is to build a sand castle.
Hey look, a wristwatch! Finders, keepers!
You build a sand castle, then take a sample of it. It's sand.
You pick up a particularly nice-looking seashell and add it to your personal collection. The not-so-nice ones you send to Mission Control.
KerbinSrfLandedMountains: The rock sample you pry from the mountainside seems prouder than all your other rock samples. As if it knows that it was part of something unimaginably greater.
Here the surface is mostly barren... snow, dirt and rocks. You're very confident that if you looked under the snow, you'd find more dirt and rocks.
The air is thin, the climate is frigid, and the ground is impenetrable.
KerbinSrfLandedDeserts: Lots of sand and rocks here. You're thankful that you're in a climate-controlled suit, because it sure looks hot out there.
Sand, sand, and more sand. A rock adds some badly needed variety to the landscape.
You saw a blue box on the horizon, but it was just an illusion. Also, you picked up a stone.
You remember your wilderness survival training... wait, do you need that training in space? What are you doing here in the desert? Someone is going to answer for this.
The sand is dry and loose and it looks like it's going to take some effort to clean your gear afterwards.
You build a sand castle to establish your dominance over the desert and demand that all nearby sand creatures swear fealty.
KerbinSrfLandedBadlands: The grass here is definitely not as green as it is in other places, but the knowledge that you've been at a place called the Badlands makes you feel cool.
Rock, coal, and dead plant matter make up the sample.
Your surface sample tries to bite you.
KerbinSrfLandedTundra: You scoop up some snow. Half goes in the bag while the other half flies towards Bob's head at a respectable velocity.
The ground here seems to be completely frozen. It was very hard to scoop up this sample.
You remind yourself to take a surface sample before relieving yourself.
The ground here is partially frozen and tufts of moose hair cover the matted grass.
KerbinSrfLandedIceCaps: It's ice and snow, what a surprise!
This ice is not very solid, the ice caps are melting.
As you analyse the sample, you see a massive chunk of ice break off. You want to leave.
You are amazed at how much microscopic life is contained within the piece of frozen water currently in your hands.
This ice is not very solid, indicating that the ice caps are melting.
As you analyse the sample, you see a massive chunk of ice break off in the distance. You want to leave.
KerbinSrfLandedNorthernIceShelf: You pick up a piece of ice and lick it. It tastes cold and vaguely salty.
You take a sample of the ice. Its texture is brittle with watery attributes. Is this what happens when water gets too cold?
You got a sample but feared to dig any deeper because of the cracking sounds. You try to not think back on how you landed here.
KerbinSrfLandedSouthernIceShelf: Even the top layer of ice is hard and glassy, and digging out a chunk takes a lot of effort.
The material appears to lower the surface temperature of whatever it comes in contact with.
As you drill for a sample, you remember hearing from one of the scientists that the ice is very fragile this year, and then try to forget about it.
KerbinSrfSplashedWater: You scoop up a sample of the surface. It's a very humid sample, you hope the equipment has been properly waterproofed.
There seems to be a lot of a liquid substance. Surely this will be a huge advancement in science as we know it.
This material seems to take on the shape of what looks like a Kerbin container. More study is needed.
You make sure to include some plankton in your sample for the folks back at KSC.
Curious - something essential to life that isn't green, isn't explosive, and doesn't start with "K".
You put the water into your pocket. It oozes and scroogles around for a bit. It gets in your nethers. Oops.
You find your pick and shovel rather ineffective here.
"Di-hydrogen monoxide, sodium chloride... wait, did you just send us a sample of salt water?"
You somehow managed to catch a fish. Unfortunately, it doesn't fit in your pocket.
KerbinSrfSplashedGrasslands: At least this bottle of murky water will be useful for a water quality analysis.
KerbinSrfSplashedShores: You've taken a sample of the water. There are bits of vegetation in it, which strongly suggests there is land nearby.
The water has a lot of sand in it, likely related to the nearby beach.
The sharks are circling. You pointedly try to ignore this as you collect some water for quality analysis.
"Di-hydrogen monoxide, sodium chloride... wait, did you just send us a sample of salt water?"
KerbinSrfSplashedDeserts: It looks like your normal gritty water. But this is no ordinary gritty water. It's desert water, and it's worth its weight in....well, water really.
A brief chemical analysis of the water reveals high amounts of minerals associated with ancient fossil groundwater, presumably rained during wetter times aeons ago.
KerbinSrfSplashedBadlands: The water will have cooled by the time it gets back, but it's still full of silt and dust.
KerbinSrfSplashedTundra: Looks like normal water to you, perhaps with a faint peaty tinge.
KerbinSrfSplashedIceCaps: The water might be freezing, but it's still teeming with microscopic life.
KerbinSrfSplashedNorthernIceShelf: The water is salty and contains particles of ice.
KerbinSrfSplashedSouthernIceShelf: There's a large chunk of ice in it, so you have to grind it up before it fits in the tube with the water.
KerbinSrfLandedKSC*: You're sure this area is pretty well studied, but you did find a nickle.
This substance is what makes the scientists yell at us when we go into the labs without cleaning our boots first.
Dirt, dust, and burnt flecks of steel make up the sample.
Looks like dirt. And rocks. And more dirt. Why are you out here again?

Your metal detector beeps and whizzes a bit. After careful digging, you find part of the engine from Jeb's pickup truck. How it got there you don't know, but you do know why Jeb's truck isn't in the parking lot today.
You see small metal scraps from the latest rocket explosion scattered around the ground.
KerbinSrfLandedAdministration*: Your contract prohibits you from taking a sample here.
KerbinSrfLandedAstronautComplex*: The toilet seems to be the cleanest room in the complex.
KerbinSrfLandedBaikerbanur*: Shards of rusting metal are scattered all around the complex. Good to see they still blew up nicely back then.
KerbinSrfLandedBaikerbanurLaunchpad*: You prise up a piece of the disintegrating concrete as a souvenir.
KerbinSrfLandedCrawlerway*: The surface seems compressed by the immense weight of the crawler passing by.
KerbinSrfLandedFlagPole*: Realizing that this is a place of remembrance you refrain from taking a sample.
KerbinSrfLandedLaunchPad*: The surface is charred and coated with burnt rocket propellant. There are also trace amounts of a conspicuous green substance.
Is that an engine bell?
You attempt to pry off a sample, but Mission Control yells at you.
Don't we already know what this is made out of?
You pull off a loose bit of the grated metallic substance. As you stuff it in the sample bag, a couple engineers eye you angrily.
The grated area is metallic, and the concrete is very... concrete-like. The staff advise you not to remove any section of the well-engineered launchpad, because it is expensive.
Unable to sample the concrete, you pick up a quarter instead.
You find a small puddle of rocket fuel where the last rocket was. Oops...
You attempt to dig up a sample before you realizing that you are trying to dig up concrete with a shovel. You play it cool and hope no one notices.
You find the remains of an engine.
Your shovel hits the ground and... nothing. SCIENCE!
You pick up a small screw that seems to have fallen out of your rocket. You hope it's not important.
Don’t we already know what's under here?
KerbinSrfLandedMissionControl*: Despite prior assumptions, the ground here is not made of solid gold.
KerbinSrfLandedRnD*: The R&D techs whisk away your sample before you can even perform an analysis on it.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDCentralBuilding*: The soil here appears to be electrically charged from all the shuffling back and forth that the R&D personnel do.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDCornerLab*: You find high concentrations of caffeine in the soil here.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDMainBuilding*: The R&D personnel closely scrutinize your technique as you take the sample.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDObservatory*: Everyone is too busy looking at other things to notice you taking a sample.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDSideLab*: Even here in a side lab there are elevated levels of caffeine in the sample from all the spilled cups of coffee.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDSmallLab*: You take a small sample here, as a larger one would surely not go unnoticed.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDTanks*: You find traces of a whole host of chemicals in the soil. Probably from the tanks.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDWindTunnel*: You take 5 samples, all of which blow away before you can secure them. Time to go have some snacks instead.
KerbinSrfLandedRunway*: You bend down to "gather samples," but decide to make some chalk drawings instead.
Getting a sample here will be tricky. Hey look, a penny!
The sample is composed of some sort of gray rock, with one half of it nearly perfectly smooth.
Mission Control would like to remind you that, while we appreciate your efforts towards research, we already have a good understanding of the makeup of our runway.
The surface appears to be very hard, like concrete. It might actually be concrete. Could be a very good surface to land on.
After examining the asphalt, you conclude that it would be a great place to cook an egg.
KerbinSrfLandedSPH*: You find traces of jet fuel in the sample.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHMainBuilding*: You find paint chips and welding slag in the sample.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHRoundTank*: You find traces of whatever chemical used to be kept in this tank.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHTanks*: You find traces of a whole host of chemicals in the soil. Probably from the tanks.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHWaterTower*: The soil here is more moist than usual.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStation*: The soil here is completely uninteresting.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishEast*: Yep, nothing to report about this sample. As expected.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishNorth*: You contemplate whether it is even worth taking this sample.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishSouth*: You step in dog poop while taking the sample. Lucky you.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationHub*: This whole area is entirely uninteresting. As expected, the surface sample from here is as well.
KerbinSrfLandedVAB*: The sample is 80% rocket fuel and 20% dirt. Good thing there is no EPA here.
KerbinSrfLandedVABMainBuilding*: The soil sample is so volatile you are able to light it with a match.
KerbinSrfLandedVABPodMemorial*: The soil here is dark and rich. Fertile, even.
KerbinSrfLandedVABRoundTank*: You find traces of whatever chemical used to be kept in this tank.
KerbinSrfLandedVABSouthComplex*: There's a stray engine part sticking up out of the ground. Wonder what that was supposed to be fitted to.
KerbinSrfLandedVABTanks*: You find traces of a whole host of chemicals in the soil. Probably from the tanks.
KerbinSrfLandedIslandAirfield*: It's stony dirt, compacted by the aircraft wheels of yesteryear.
KerbinSrfLandedWoomerang*: Looks like normal launchpad deposits of ash, fuel residue and rocket shrapnel.

MunSrfLanded: Done and dusted.
You take a sample and confirm that the Mun is not made of cheese.
Wensleydale? Stilton? It isn't like any cheese you've tasted. You wonder if its actually cheese at all.
You suddenly realize why the physical examination screened for breathing problems.
Oddly enough, the sample smells similar to Swiss cheese. One wonders if it's edible.
The dust seems to be ionized due to long-term exposure to sunlight without an atmosphere to filter it. It sticks to your suit.
Unfortunately, it's not cheese. You double-check it, but it still isn't.
You note the sample's lack of cheese.
The surface appears to be a fine dust with a grayish tone. Seems too flaky to make snowballs with.
Finally, something that isn't dirt: a rock!
So, breccias are actually kind of boring.
Through extensive and professional testing, you find out that, contrary to popular belief, the Mun is not made of cheese. Also, you immediately regret your testing.
This rock has tiny craters on it. Neat!
The lead scientists back at KSP go against your idea to grill your sample between two pieces of bread.
MunSrfLandedMidlands: The ground appears to be made of very small, sharp, abrasive pieces of gray rock. It would probably be best if you didn't rub your space suit against the stuff.
The darker midlands surface appears to be made up of basaltic rocks.
The sample contains odd glass spherules of various colors. They're quite pretty!
MunSrfLandedNorthernBasin: The sample contains evidence of a weak, localized magnetic field.
The surface here consists of multiple types of rock. Some appear almost crystalline.
MunSrfLandedEastCrater: The sample contains a combination of breccia and melted materials.
There are high concentrations of metals in this crater.
MunSrfLandedNorthwestCrater: The ground materials appear to be a combination of basaltic rocks and breccia.
Deeper samples seems to contain more fragmented and shattered formations of rock.
MunSrfLandedSouthwestCrater: The sample appears to be radioactive.
There appears to be layers of completely different materials here. It's possible this site has had multiple impacts.
MunSrfLandedFarsideCrater: Tiny flecks of purple flesh dot the mineral deposits here. The texture of the crater looks quite puckered. Is this evidence that this was once the home of the legendary Kraken?
Samples from this location appear to be similar in makeup to the powdery surface of the Mun.
MunSrfLandedCanyons: Samples in this location are striated. It's possible that the surface has fractured here.
MunSrfLandedPolarCrater: To your surprise, you find a large lump of ice lodged in the center of the crater. Upon further analysis you find that it doesn't taste particularly well, and is too chunky for the snow-cone machine.
Samples from this location seem to show intense shock patterns.
You examine the sample you've collected, and throw it into the container for further testing.
It appears that some of the samples are magnetic and show a faint alignment.
MunSrfLandedPoles: You wonder if this would make a nice snack.
It's a cold piece of soil. As cold as ice. Wait.

You examine the rocks you have collected. Upon further investigation, you realize that there is a strange weight to one of them, it does not feel like it has the same weight as the other rocks you've collected and seems hollow. You promptly place it into a sample container.
The samples here show lower concentrations of ejecta. There appears to be some liquids in the soil that have frozen permanently.
MunSrfLandedPolarLowlands: The sample seems to contain frozen liquids... we should send them back to Kerbin for further analysis.
MunSrfLandedHighlands: You take a small rock and your nine iron, place the rock on a T, and knock it into low orbit.
The Mun dust here is light and thin... It's very poor sandcastle-building material. That doesn't stop you from making one.
The sample appears to be comprised of a large number of materials that have layered over time.
The sample has low amounts of basaltic rock. This might account for the changes in coloration we've seen.
MunSrfLandedHighlandCraters: Seems muddy, in a waterless kind of way. You find it appropriate to remove your shoes before boarding the ship again, no thought given to the problems of removing space attire while still outside.
The crater seems to contain materials from lower layers of the surface.
These samples appear to be quite old compared to others we've taken.
You sample the dark surface of the crater. This should help determine what type of object hit here.
This crater seems to filled with useful materials in high concentration. What luck!
MunSrfLandedMidlandCraters: The patterning of this surface sample is particularly interesting.
As you sample the crater you can't help but feel bad for the Mun. It's been beaten up pretty bad by these impacts.
You dig around in the surface of the crater, looking for something neat.
You find a really cool looking rock and decide to keep it. Then, you throw a less cool looking rock into the sample container.
MunSrfLandedEastFarsideCrater: This crater must have had a really nasty impact, the samples are heavier than normal.
You're glad that the Mun's gravity is so low. Digging is backbreaking work!
MunSrfLandedTwinCraters: Definitely not Cheddar. Winchester? No. Dorset Blue Vinney? Nah.
This area has a particularly high concentration of rare elements. Nice!
Continual samples of this area confirm it is rich in useful materials.
You dig around for a nice sample of the crater, carefully selecting some of everything.

MinmusSrfLanded*: As you gather up some Minmus material, you label it as "Definitely not ice".
You scoop a sample up to be analyzed back home, then smear some over your gloves to sniff when you get back in the capsule. You bet it'll smell minty and delicious.
You taste the sample, and find that it does not taste like mint at all. You wonder where the medicine cabinet went.
You are disappointed to discover that the sample does not taste like ice cream, but instead tastes more like frozen yogurt.
Mission Control tentatively asks you how it tastes.

After excavating the surface, you find a hidden ice layer. The ice appears to have a slightly greenish tinge to it. Upon further examination, you find it to have the perfect consistency for snow-cones, yet the taste leaves much to be desired.
You try to taste the sample but your helmet gets in the way.
The white mountains have a snow-like surface. The frozen lakes seem to be made of the same substance, but much smoother.
Must... not... taste...
Glad of your geology classes, you search for rocks to analyze. Finding none, you curse bitterly.
Surface samples confirm that it is not made out of mint, even though it looks and tastes like it.
You ask Mission Control if the substance could be marketed as some sort of air freshener. They consider your request.
The surface seems to consist of tiny crystal-like grains.
MinmusSrfLandedHighlands*: Ice up here has a very high reflectivity, which must significantly contribute to the high albedo of Minmus.
MinmusSrfLandedMidlands*: You realize that the ground here looks more like cookie dough then the mint ice cream on the flat lands. Your mouth starts to water.
The sample you've taken indicates that the surface is made from different types of snow and ice intricately intermixed with each other.
MinmusSrfLandedLowlands*: You take a handful of snow, ice, and dirt and put it into your sample bag. You hope the science team on Kerbin will figure out what it is and why it's not suitable for Kerbal consumption.
MinmusSrfLandedFlats*: You want to get your helmet off and taste the sample. instead, you pull yourself together and continue with the mission.
The surface is perfectly flat and smooth. You have to use your shovel to dig out some ice.
The ground appears to be made of small, cyan crystals. It doesn't look like it would be enjoyable walking barefoot here.
MinmusSrfLandedGreatFlats*: It's just ice, with occasional snowflakes here and there.
MinmusSrfLandedGreaterFlats*: The sample consists of multiple layers of very reflective greenish ice. You wonder what compound gives it such a nice colour.
MinmusSrfLandedLesserFlats*: The sample contains several distinct types of ice frozen to form a complex, multi-layered pattern.
MinmusSrfLandedPoles*: You see that The Goo has formed itself into a ice cream cone, You note how yummy it looks.
The surface here looks even tastier than everywhere else.
It has the consistency of a snowball, and is likely to be just as good at being thrown into other Kerbals' faces.
MinmusSrfLandedSlopes*: It's snow, ice, some loose dirt, and a couple of pebbles, frozen together in a strangely complicated way.

DunaSrfLanded: Different colored rocks are probably made of different stuff. You should grab as many as you can.
This red dust could be iron oxide, or possibly paprika.
Nope. Not barbecue flavored.
Yup. Dust. Are you surprised?
This sand works well for drawing pictures on your space suit.
You make a note: "Bring laser next time."
Too bad it's only red spice. It would have been much better to go to Eve.
It looks like some sort of spice.
DunaSrfLandedPoles: The outer icy crust appears to be made of an unusually dense ice. It gives off wisps of white vapour when held against your warm suit.
The ice is a mixture of dry ice and water ice. And those pesky percholorates are all over it.
On closer inspection, the ice from under the crust appears to have a jumbled hexa-cubic crystal structure rather than the standard tesselating hexagons found on Kerbin.
DunaSrfLandedMidlands: The exact chemical composition of the surface will no doubt be of interest to the scientists back home.
The winds that girdle the planet have mixed the local grains with dust from all over the surface.
It appears this soil could be used for snack production, possibly after several centuries of Kerbal-forming.
The soil sample contains percholorates. Time to find a different planet.
DunaSrfLandedLowlands: If there is evidence of ancient water here, you ain't finding it. Maybe it's buried too deep.
Digging down a couple of feet, you hit a rocky surface worn smooth by aeons of erosion by the wind.
It's very thin and fine. You imagine a rover could easily become stuck in this if it had small wheels.
DunaSrfLandedCraters: The surface here is much more solid, darker and less crumbly that other areas on Duna, indicating different localised geology.
The minerals you manage to chip off the rocks on the crater floor display oddly deformed crystal structures, as though subjected to intense heat and pressure.
You pick around, searching for a piece of the ancient meteorite that hit here. Instead, you find a piece of your ship hull that you can't quite seem to find where it came from.
DunaSrfLandedHighlands: You pick up some loose stones that look to have been part of a landslide.
The loose mountain rock you've picked up seems to contain a lot of iron (III) oxide. As such, it's not tasty, nor particularly healthy.
You chip away at the hard basalt with your rock hammer and eventually get a good sample.
The regolith is toxic with high levels of perchlorate compounds.
DunaSrfLandedMidlandSea: You dig a little and find a perfectly preserved series of ripple marks from presumably billions of years ago.
The darker ground here is much more compacted than the windblown sediments of elsewhere on Duna, as if laid down and cemented by water.
You start to dig with trepidation at the possibility of finding water before you realise you left your sample container on the ship and have to anticlimatically go and fetch it.
DunaSrfLandedNorthernBasin: The dust grains here are very large, and feel very rough as you scoop them into a container.
The tough igneous rocks here hamper your attempts to drill a sample. It would be much easier if you had a machine to help.
The local rocks look like breccia and basalt, and contain evidence of significant shocking and fracturing.
DunaSrfLandedSouthernBasin: There's a lot of frozen water in this basin, hidden under a thin layer of dust.
There's a lighter section among the basalt. It looks a bit like limestone. You consider it important and put some of it into your sample container.
Nearby rocks look consistent with flood basalts on Kerbin, suggesting this area was once a giant volcano.
DunaSrfLandedNorthernShelf: You don't even have to scoop around for a sample, just hold up a container and it blows right in and collects at the back.
The sand here is much finer from being blown around, and looks to contain traces of tiny polar ice crystals.
There appears to be traces of water in amongst the fine soil and shifting sand.
DunaSrfLandedMidlandCanyon: The dust here contains traces of ice crystals, the concentrations of which increase with depth. You also find a random, perfectly rounded pebble amongst the sand.
After an effort to dig a hole, you hit bedrock. It seems to be worn smooth, as if by water rather than dust.
The small rock you pick up seems a variety of alluvial congomerate. You wonder if there was ever a river here.
DunaSrfLandedEasternCanyon: The surface is mainly deep drifts of dust, so thick and fine that you can't dig deep enough to find solid ground. Dust will have to suffice.
You find a rare rock outcrop protruding from the dust, which is mostly basaltic and shows signs of extensive faulting.
You wonder if you need to take a sample considering there is so much dust on you that you could be considered a sample on your own right.
DunaSrfLandedWesternCanyon: The dust here appears to be comprised of rock from multiple regions, as suggested by its varying degrees of fineness.
Lots of dust, but after some digging down you still can't find any bedrock.
You find a nice metal ruler in the sand! You're ready to proclaim the discovery of an alien civilization, but then you find that the one in your suit is missing.
DunaSrfLandedPolarHighlands: The soil here is like tundra; dirt mixed with ice crystals, with a thin layer of fresh white frost on top.
Breaking through the thin crust, you find layers of dirt mixed with ice, laid down in countless cycles of thawing and freezing.
DunaSrfLandedPolarCraters: You put a sample of the crustal ice in a tube and put it in your pocket. You take it out again shortly afterwards to see a tubeful of vapour bfore the lid pops off in your face.
Collecting a sample of the less dense ice under the crust, you sneak a taste. It's ice all right, if a little off-colour and gritty.

DresSrfLanded*: You take some sand. The color will go great on your sandcastle at home.
You find traces of a large number of iron, likely deposited here by impacts over time.
The soil is very dark here. You are sure the scientists back on Kerbin will be able to find something interesting about the composition of Dres.
DresSrfLandedHighlands*: This grainy dust is not suggesting anything.
DresSrfLandedImpactCraters*: The soil is very dark here. You are sure the scientists back on Kerbin will be able to find something interesting about the composition of Dres if you can get it back to them.
DresSrfLandedImpactEjecta*: The grey metallic sand looks nice and shiny, so you make a sandcastle.

LaytheSrfLanded: You find particles of volcanic ash in the sand. Perhaps you should avoid eating them.
The soil reminds you of the sand on Kerbin's beaches, but it's less dense and darker in color.
Small pebbles you've found look to be shaped by water.
The soil contains a lot of silica and feels very rough.
You try to form a castle out of the soil. For science, of course.
The soil here contains some ores that looks like cronstedtite.
LaytheSrfLandedPoles: Sand is all over this moon. A shame it's not warmer...
The water here is different from Kerbin, it does not freeze. Maybe there is salt in the sand?
LaytheSrfLandedShores: You run the sand through your fingers and suddenly feel thirsty.
Skipping stones is easy here! They all go 20% further than on Kerbin.
LaytheSrfLandedDunes: The soil here contains no immediately identifiable organic chemicals.
LaytheSrfLandedCrescentBay: Thirsty from your long trip, you take a sip of the sample, only to find that it's the most awful water you ever tasted.
LaytheSrfLandedTheSagenSea: This sample seems to have all the basic compounds required for life.
LaytheSrfSplashed: The liquid filling these basins seems to be different than Kerbin's.
The sample sticks to your suit. You wonder if Mission Control is going to charge you for the dry cleaning.
The liquid, though definitely water, contains impurities that seem to help it maintain a liquid state at these temperatures.

You manage to take a sip of local water through the oxygen supply tube. It definitely looks like water, acts like water, smells like water and... it does not taste like water at all. You hope you have medicine on the ship.
LaytheSrfSplashedCrescentBay: You go to sneak a sip of the sample, only to be nearly overwhelmed by the unpleasant odor. You spend the next few minutes recovering, wondering what possessed you to open your helmet.
LaytheSrfSplashedTheSagenSea: You wish you had brought a boat.
You try a drink. It feels like a cold glacier and tastes like... well, tap water.
You decide to put some in your bottle but it is frozen shut.
This sample contains highly toxic levels of ammonia. Maybe you shouldn't have drank this...
You decide to cancel your proposal for a Laythe-sourced bottled water company.

VallSrfLanded*: This reminds you of Minmus, only bubblegum flavor...
You analyze the frozen surface and find some blue material.
You analyze the surface and find some beautiful green and blue crystal structures.
VallSrfLandedPoles*: After crushing some of the surface ice into a snow-cone and getting permission from mission control for an experiment, you find it doesn't taste particularly nice.
VallSrfLandedMidlands*: The icy crystals show signs of shock and sharp temperature change at some point in the past.
VallSrfLandedHighlands*: As you dig into the blue ice, a particularly nasty quake sends your shovel flying. Perhaps you shouldn't dig any deeper...
VallSrfLandedLowlands*: Strangely, some of the ice on the surface seems significantly fresher than the rest.

TyloSrfLanded: Some rock samples exhibit high heavy metal content.
The surface seems to be composed of rocks, nickel, a little bit of cobalt and ice.
TyloSrfLandedHighlands: You quickly scoop up some white dust. You ran out of condiments three months ago.
TyloSrfLandedLowlands: The rock sample is similar to what could be found on the Mun. There seems to be less evidence of volcanic activity, however.

BopSrfLanded: Just under the brown surface you analyze a black, glassy material. A volcanic mineral called obsidian.
You analyze a red-brown mineral. It appears to be hard and very moist, like rusty iron.
You pick up an orange mineral. It appears to be soft and very moist.
BopSrfLandedPeaks: Just under the brown surface you find a black, glassy material and put it in the sample bag. A volcanic residue perhaps?
BopSrfLandedRidges: The soil here is surprisingly soft, like play-koh.
BopSrfLandedPoles: As you take a sample, it begins to glow... and starts shaking. It clearly does not like being contained.
BopSrfLandedSlopes: You pick up a rock from the ground. It looks somewhat unusual so you put it into the container.
BopSrfLandedValley: The rocks lying around look very dull and in no way remind you of potatoes. You shove one into your sample container, just to pass time.

PolSrfLanded: The rock sample you take crumbles into dust in your hand. You put it into your bag.
After analyzing some samples, you found phosphoric and sulfuric compounds in the ground. If you bring this back to Kerbin, we can make some matches.
You’re fairly certain that Pol isn't, in fact, made of pollen. This will cause quite the stir back home.
PolSrfLandedLowlands: You feel slight irritation as you shove a handful of colorful sand into the storage container.
PolSrfLandedHighlands: As you pick up the sample, your eyes start to water. Thinking it would not be good to sneeze in your suit, you put the chunk of surface back down.

EelooSrfLanded*: You take a scoop of the surface, eager to drizzle chocolate sauce on it later.
You take a surface sample and put it in your soft drink.
You take a surface sample and crumple it into a snowball.
The surface is so hard that you have to use a pickaxe to get a sample.
You decide that when you get back to the ship, you will check the sample for vanilla flavoring.
The ground appears to be made up of many frozen things, including rocks, dirt, frost, water, and various metal ores.

You pick up a handful of extremely fine-grained icy material. It sublimates from the warmth of your spacesuit glove, drifting away as a short-lived wisp of vapors before refreezing and falling back to the surface.
It looks... icy. You wonder what you are doing here in the first place.
The surface reminds you of the ice lakes of Minmus, but not nearly as minty.
Ice, Ice, Baby.
It's almost, but not quite, entirely unlike Kerbin's ice caps.
You immediately find out that microscopic ice crystals orbit this body. Perhaps impacts have launched them out here.
You analyze the ground and discover that it's crystallized water.
EelooSrfLandedPoles*: The ice here is fresher than would be expected. This area was recently liquid.
EelooSrfLandedNorthernGlaciers*: The material seems very icy and brittle. You try not to think of how you landed here.
EelooSrfLandedLowlands*: This looks like the icing I had on my birthday cake!
EelooSrfLandedHighlands*: This is just ice and dirt. You question why you couldn't have gotten this from the North Pole.
EelooSrfLandedIceCanyons*: Frozen nitrogen. You might have to keep this stuff in the fridge for the return trip.
EelooSrfLandedMidlands*: It appears to be a brittle, transparent crystalline solid, quite probably hydrogen hydroxide with minor amounts of other chemicals.

temperatureScan.hints: Comments on thermal conditions, possibly with subjective reactions for added flavor or drama.

SunInSpaceLow: The temperatures are off the chart. Check that, the chart just burned up. You wonder what it said...
You look at the display and realize that you probably won't need the oven to heat your food.
It's hot. What did you expect?
You come to the shocking conclusion that temperature readings here are considerably high.
The temperature is hard to read as the paint melts from the surface of the instrument.
The weather today is warm, with a risk of heat wave in the corona. Kerbals above the age of seventy are advised to stay inside if they wish their miraculous survival streak to continue.
The recording confirms your suspicion that it is in fact, very hot.
The wires in temperature read-outs have melted, giving blatantly incorrect readings.
Wow! This place is almost as hot as the surface of the... Oh, wait.
Your thermometer appears to be stuck at "MAX".
The thermometer has exploded. You really wish you could've seen it.
I think it just broke.
It is quite hot. Mission Control asks if this experiment was necessary.
According to the thermometer, the Sun is hot. Fascinating.
I think the thermometer just exploded.
Though the thermometer has melted, you're able to take an guesstimated reading.
It seems the cookies are burning...
The thermometer doesn't look like it was constructed to handle this kind of heat.
Mission Control assured you that the extremely high temperatures are perfectly harmless.
Hot stuff!
"HOT!" Seems about right.
It's hot here. What a surprise!
The thermometer seems to have melted. You can assume that it is rather toasty outside.
It's off the scale!
The thermometer is on fire.
Mission Controls observes the readings and formally requests the crew to back off a few million meters.

MohoSrfLandedNorthernSinkhole*: The thermal gradient data forms a very pretty chart.
MohoSrfLandedMidlands*: With every fan in the pod on, you don't need a thermometer to tell you it's hot.
MohoSrfLandedCentralLowlands*: Temperature in the shadow seems to be quite low.
MohoSrfLandedHighlands*: The temperature here is so hot the thermometer has melted entirely. No matter, you send the data back anyway.
MohoSrfLandedMinorCraters*: The experiment is a success! The team predicted there would be numbers in the data!
MohoSrfLandedCanyon*: The melted thermometer makes you question the safety of this mission.
MohoInSpaceLow*: You guessed it would be at least a million billion degrees this close to the Sun... but it isn't.
This wasn't tested for such temperatures.

EveSrfLandedPoles: It's slightly cooler, but still far too hot for anything to live.
If the temperature could be cooled by sixty degrees, this might be a nice place. Ignoring the crushing atmosphere, toxic water and suspect chemicals in the dirt, that is.
EveSrfLandedMidlands: There's nothing middling about these burning temperatures at all.
The ship bakes in the heat as the air around it shimmers with mirages.
EveSrfLandedExplodiumSea: The liquid in the thermometer seems to be frothing angrily.
EveSrfLandedLowlands: Readings are off the charts, literally. Engineers in Mission Control hastily append another sheet of printer paper to them.
EveSrfLandedHighlands: The temperature is so hot that the thermometer wants to slap you in the face.
Eve's proximity to Kerbol makes any kerbiforming effort doomed to failure as the planet is constantly blasted by heat.
EveSrfLandedPeaks: Perhaps that mountain over there is slightly cooler?
A gentle wind blows up. Somehow it makes the air even hotter.
Unlike Kerbin, the temperature seems to has risen. This planet seems like a recolour of "Venus" from "Human Space Program".
EveSrfLandedImpactEjecta: That's far too pleasant to be right. Maybe if you jiggle the cord... aha! There it goes.
Blackbody radiation from the ground and the hot air means it's still extremely hot in shadow.
EveSrfLandedCraterLake: You consider dunking the thermometer in the lake to cool it off.
EveSrfLandedWesternSea: A weak sea breeze does little to alleviate the oppressive, cloying heat.
EveSrfLandedOlympus: You're not sure whether you want to go any higher, lest you be burned by the sun as well as everything else.
The temperature isn't as ludicrous all the way up here, but you're still not going outside without some ice packs.
EveSrfLandedEasternSea: You start to worry that the liquid in the thermometer might boil soon.
EveSrfLandedFoothills: You're not sure if the temperature reading is accurate or if the gravity is so strong the mercury can't rise properly.
EveSrfLandedShallows: Sun, sea and sand are all here, and the temperature is lovely and warm to boot.
EveSrfSplashedPoles: The liquid appears to be radiating heat.
EveSrfSplashedExplodiumSea: "Goooood morning ladies and gentlekerbs, it's going to be a real scorcher out at sea today, with surface highs of over a hundred and fifty degrees expected and sunny weather all day. Better take plenty of water with you when you go out, and dip to cool off. This is Jeb, for KSWN."
It's pretty hot. The high pressure is the only thing stopping this ocean from boiling away.
EveSrfSplashedLowlands: The sea is warm enough that any bathing in it is strongly discouraged.
EveSrfSplashedImpactEjecta: The crater walls radiate heat down onto the sea like a planetary solar oven.
EveSrfSplashedCraterLake: The data suggests a suitable temperature for frying kalamari.
Despite the lack of not-water interchange, the temperature in this basin sea is the same as the rest.
EveSrfSplashedWesternSea: The grape soda is hot enough that you recommend leaving it in the fridge for a while before drinking it.
EveSrfSplashedEasternSea: The temperature readings vary widely as waves roll over the sensor.
You should have brought a fishing rod, since anything you caught would be ready-cooked.
EveSrfSplashedShallows: The labels on the thermometer have dissolved, but the reading is all the way at the top anyway.
Quite how this ocean doesn't boil is lost on you, since it's well above the boiling point of.....what was the ocean made of again?
EveFlyingLowPoles: Temperatures are almost tolerable. For some people, that is. Ok, nobody.
EveFlyingLowMidlands: When life gives you lemons, send them to Eve. The heat will show those lemons who's boss.
EveFlyingLowExplodiumSea: The shiny, flat ocean surface reflects heat back into the sky, and straight at you.
EveFlyingLowLowlands: The greenhouse effect is extremely powerful on Eve, as though it was subject to a runaway warming.
EveFlyingLowHighlands: The thermometer has turned into a miniature lava lamp.
EveFlyingLowPeaks: Even above the highest mountains, it's still baking hot.
EveFlyingLowImpactEjecta: In this heat, you could just hold your koffee outside the ship to keep it piping hot.
EveFlyingLowCraterLake: If these aerial readings are anything to go by, dipping your toe in the sea would leave it charcoaled as well as dissolved and crushed.
EveFlyingLowWesternSea: A wire comes loose on the thermometer, causing it to erroneously read the temperature as something nice.
EveFlyingLowOlympus: You hope whatever part is stopping you from slamming into the mountainside doesn't melt in these temperatures.
EveFlyingLowEasternSea: The temperature is a balmy purple.
EveFlyingLowFoothills: Sluggish air currents carry heat up from the hotplate surface.
EveFlyingLowShallows: I tell you what is shallow, and that's the atmosphere temperature gradient.
EveFlyingHigh: You're nowhere near the ground, and it's warm already.
KSC assures you it's nice and cool down there. You, and the thermometer, beg to differ.
The red alcohol in the thermometer is turning purple.
EveInSpaceLow: You detect a definite heat signature from the planet below.
The planet and the atmosphere radiate heat into space.
The thermometer outputs a significantly higher reading if rotated to face the planet.
EveInSpaceHigh: The thick atmosphere acts like a mirror for heat, causing Eve to shine bright with infrared radiation.
Kerbol's rays are more powerful at this distance. It'll be hot down there.
Just looking at Eve makes you want to turn the thermostat down a notch.

GillySrfLandedMidlands: Dust from your movements has covered the thermometer. At least the dust seems friendly. You realize you want a hug.
GillySrfLandedLowlands: The temerature is about as low as your landing site.
GillySrfLandedHighlands: You were expecting Gilly to be a "Hot Potato"... You note your disappointment with the moon's cold surface.
GillyInSpaceLow: You didn't expect the temperature of space to be any different here than elsewhere. You weren't disappointed.
GillyInSpaceHigh: The glare from Kerbol almosts blinds you as it flashes off of the thermometer.

KerbinSrfLandedWater: You ensure that the thermometer is adequately sealed near the water. You wouldn't want to release mercury into the sea.
Spray from the salty water has encrusted the thermometer and you are unable to get an accurate reading. So you make something up.
KerbinSrfLandedGrasslands: The temperature here is quite pleasant. This would be a nice place to live.
You don't need any fancy measurements to tell you that it's nice outside.
The temperature here is 'normal'. Guess the calibration worked.
KerbinSrfLandedHighlands: The thermometer tube seems to have exploded in the thin atmosphere here. We really shouldn't have bought the cheapest thermometer available. Oh well, no temperature reading today.
It's quite chilly out here! The thermometer agrees.
KerbinSrfLandedShores: You wonder if there's a setting on the thermometer that allows it to warn you of sand-sharks.
Just the right temperature to go for a swim in the sea right over there.
KerbinSrfLandedMountains: The temperature reports a chilly -15 degrees. You are glad to be inside your nice warm spaceship. With snacks!
It's cold sure, but the windchill is even worse! You hope it doesn't blow you off the mountain.
KerbinSrfLandedDeserts: It's hot here. That's not a reading, you learned it in primary school.
Large plumes of heat are rising from the surface of the desert.
The temperature here is very hot.
The temperature is too hot to live here.
The thermometer quickly rises beyond the scale limit. You put it back into the shadows before it bursts.
KerbinSrfLandedBadlands: The temperature here appears to fluctuate depending on the time of day.
The temperature is bad, and it's getting worse every minute.
Man the EVA is gonna suck.
KerbinSrfLandedTundra: The thermometer reading makes you want to go home and snuggle under a blanket.
It's cold enough to keep things permanently frozen here.
KerbinSrfLandedIceCaps: If this is normal, you hate to think of how cold it can get.
Welp. That explains all the ice.
The temperature is higher than 30 years ago. You ponder why.
KerbinSrfLandedNorthernIceShelf: It's cold enough to freeze even salty sea water completely solid.
A rise in temperature would be very alarming, considering you are standing on something eminently meltable.
KerbinSrfLandedSouthernIceShelf: A note at the back of the equipment manual states "Warranty void if exposed to temperatures below 0C". You're pretty sure it's colder than that.
A thick layer of rime ice has formed on the thermometer, telling you all you need to know.
KerbinSrfSplashedWater: The engineers hadn't planned on water, so the numbers have all washed off the thermometer, making it unreadable.
The water is colder than the surrounding air.
You have to stick your head underwater in order to see what the thermometer says.
KerbinSrfSplashedGrasslands: The water cools down the temperature a bit, and it's a bit cooler than would be comfortable.
KerbinSrfSplashedShores: The water here is warmer than in the open ocean, perhaps because there's less of it for the Sun to warm up.
Just the right temperature to be a lucrative shark mating area.
KerbinSrfSplashedDeserts: The water is invitingly warm. This place would make a great inland beach resort.
KerbinSrfSplashedBadlands: It's hot enough you could reheat some snacks by dunking them in the water.
KerbinSrfSplashedTundra: It's pretty cold, but not quite cold enough to freeze the water.
KerbinSrfSplashedIceCaps: It's cold enough to freeze water solid, so why is there liquid water here?
KerbinSrfSplashedNorthernIceShelf: The water is cold as ice. In fact, it is largely ice.
KerbinSrfSplashedSouthernIceShelf: Any creatures living in this frigid water would need some serious adaptations to not freeze to death.
KerbinFlyingLowWater: The air seems to be cooled by the water, and the temperature is lower that average here.
You keep an eye out for sudden tsunamis as the thermometer takes its reading of the surrounding air.
KerbinFlyingLowGrasslands: The temperature seems cool and comfortable. It is probably due to the acres of green grass that stretch to the horizon.
As you perform temperature scans over the Grasslands, you take care not hit any trees.
KerbinFlyingLowHighlands: The snow and ice of the mountains has gotten stuck to the display of the thermometer. As you wipe it off and take a reading you are reminded of the snacks in your fridge back at home.
The brisk wind of the Highlands is making you feel colder than what the thermometer suggests.
KerbinFlyingLowShores: Yep, the ambient temperature is the definition of "beach weather".
The thermometer reads a balmy 'medium-hot'. Yep, another beautiful day at the shores. Too bad you're stuck in this rocket.
KerbinFlyingLowMountains: It's cold, OK. Just look where you're going, and PLEASE don't turn off the SAS again.
You're too busy dodging sudden mountains to pay attention to your latest temperature scan.
Glancing back and forth between the mountains beneath you and the thermometer display, you realize that you are stuck between a rock and a cold place.
KerbinFlyingLowDeserts: Even here above the surface it is hot. The thermometer reads 270 degrees. You wonder if that is in Celsius, Fahrenheit, or Kelvin. Oh well, the scientist back at KSC will figure it out.
Your temperature readings over the desert surprise exactly nobody back at KSC.
KerbinFlyingLowBadlands: It is HOT! You don't know whether it's the normal ambient temperature here or whether the spacecraft is burning up. Either way, we should have installed air conditioning.
You pour the remains of your juice pack over the engines in a frantic attempt to keep them from overheating.
KerbinFlyingLowTundra: It's normally cold at the surface, and now you're flying the wind chill just makes it even colder.
The coldness must explain why the surface below is frozen and the plants are stunted. You feel like a genius.
KerbinFlyingLowIceCaps: The temperature is a little higher than you measured last time. Is it a measurement error or a sign of kerbal warming?
As you read off the temperature to Mission Control, you are envious of how warm they all sound over the radio.
KerbinFlyingLowNorthernIceShelf: The temperature is extremely low. It has to be, to maintain such a colossal mass of ice like that below.
Wind chill from the strong winds that howl across the flats drops the temperature even lower.
KerbinFlyingLowSouthernIceShelf: The atmospheric drag causes a measurable, welcome increase in temperature.
It's many degrees below zero. No wonder this place was so hazardous to early explorers.
KerbinFlyingHigh: Let's see...temperature. Slightly cooler than the fiery pits of hell. Yep, that covers it.
It is definitely cooler here than on the surface of Kerbin.
82. Or is that 28? They never did tell you which side of this thing is up.
The thermometer is hanging on to the capsule by only one corner, and you are afraid if you touch it it will go flying off. We should have used the super-glue instead of the OK-glue.
You resist the urge to place your craft into a steep dive just to see how fast the thermometer changes.
Mission Control declares that they can't distinguish your craft from the background ambient temperature. This is a problem since your engines are currently firing...
What is it about height that makes things colder?
At this altitude, the warmth of the beach is a thing of the past.
It is chilly up here in the thin wisps of the upper atmosphere. You report this to KSC, and they laugh back something about being happy they aren't in your shoes.
The temperature reads -10, but in reality it is probably lower because that is as low as the thermometer can read.
The temperature outside is well into the negatives. You are silently thankful for you climate-controlled spacesuit.
It appears to be the perfect snack-eating temperature. You open a box just to be sure. For science.
Sitting inside your climate-controlled cockpit, you find that you don't really care what the thermometer thinks of the outside temperature.
Huh, it's pretty darn cold. Hard to believe you were just at the Grasslands.
The temperature here is higher than you expected, what with all the ice and snow. Maybe THAT's why they call it the Highlands.
The temperature seems just about perfect here. You consider doing an EVA without your spacesuit.
KerbinInSpaceLow: The hellish climate of the Badlands can't touch you up here!
The temperature scan is inconclusive, but you bet it's chilly out there. You tell KSC about your supposition.
You specifically bought one of those fancy low-gravity thermometers for this experiment.
The temperature readings fluctuates wildly as the sensor moves in and out of the shadows.
Seriously? You want to know the temperature now? We're in space; who cares!
The residual heat of the spacecraft masks the coldness of the empty vacuum of space.
Well, not much heat to measure here.
As usual the thermometer here is unreliable. You write down its reading anyway for the scientists back at KSC.
Thermometer reading? Check. Space is cold? Check. Time for a snack? Check!
Space is cold everywhere. Unless you are facing the sun or a supernova. Hey! We should go check out one of THOSE!!
Snacks fall out of your mouth and garble your speech as you try to read off the temperature. KSC just regards it as a bad radio transmission, and waits for your next report.
And the temperature is: perfect for snacking! So how about a few snacks, and then we'll send this report off to KSC.
You wonder if the fact that you're over the Grasslands will affect your temperature readings. Probably not.
The fact that you are over the Highlands does not change the thermometer's opinion on the coldness of space one bit.
KerbinInSpaceHigh: As you look down over the majestic mountains below you take yet another temperature reading. Stupid science. At least the view is nice.
In high space over the planet, thermal back radiation from the warm planet is much diminished.
The temperature is even lower than it would be if you were down in the Ice Caps. At least there's no wind chill.
"Roses are red, violets are blue (apparently), the mountains are pretty, and space is cold." Mission Control doesn't seem to appreciate your poetry.
KerbinSrfLandedKSC*: The temperature rises as an angry Gene Kerman comes over the radio and demands to know what you're still doing there.
