KerbinSrfLandedAdministration*: The temperature drops. Mortimer seems pleased with our spending of funds.
KerbinSrfLandedAstronautComplex*: The temperature here is elevated, but it's probably just due to the new recruits excitement to meet a real astronaut.
KerbinSrfLandedBaikerbanur*: The temperature is quite warm at this latitude and location.
KerbinSrfLandedBaikerbanurLaunchpad*: The concrete pad is uncomfortable warm beneath your feet from it's spell baking in the sun.
KerbinSrfLandedCrawlerway*: The thermometer gets crushed under the wheels of the crawler. Oh well, time for some snacks.
KerbinSrfLandedFlagPole*: You realize that the temperature drops the higher you climb the pole.
KerbinSrfLandedLaunchPad*: The temperature reading spikes suddenly. Did somebody start the engines?
KerbinSrfLandedMissionControl*: The temperature here is elevated from the tense atmosphere.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDCentralBuilding*: The thermometer is sucked into a wormhole by an advanced project here. You decide to write it off.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDCornerLab*: You hide the thermometer in the corner hoping no-one will find it.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDMainBuilding*: The R&D personnel disassemble your thermometer before you can take a reading.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDObservatory*: You observe the temperature. How fitting.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDSideLab*: The temperature here is elevated due to the non-stop firing of neurons in this space.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDSmallLab*: The size of the lab has no effect whatsoever on the temperature readings, contrary to your hypothesis.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDTanks*: The material from the tanks oozes out onto your thermometer and dissolves it before you can get an accurate reading.
KerbinSrfLandedRnDWindTunnel*: The thermometer reads minus one million degrees. Probably from the wind chill.
KerbinSrfLandedRunway*: The jet exhaust skews the temperature reading, and you have to throw out the results.
KerbinSrfLandedSPH*: The temperature here is cool due to the breeze blowing through the SPH.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHMainBuilding*: You are unable to concentrate on taking a reading with the screaming of jet engines in the background.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHRoundTank*: Seconds after being attached, the thermometer has frozen into place.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHTanks*: Seconds after being attached, the thermometer has frozen into place.
KerbinSrfLandedSPHWaterTower*: 3.983°C - You realize there is as much water in this tower as can be.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStation*: Temperature here is: bored. Oops, that's just you. The temperature is nominal.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishEast*: You position the thermometer in the direct center of the dish hoping for an exciting result. You are disappointed as usual.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishNorth*: In your boredom you drop the thermometer and break it. No loss, it's not like there was anything worth reporting here anyway.
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationDishSouth*: You wait in anguish for yet another temperature reading. What's the point?
KerbinSrfLandedTrackingStationHub*: You explain the use of real scientific instruments like the thermometer to the staff at the tracking station.
KerbinSrfLandedVAB*: The ground spontaneously combusts from years of rocket fuel soaking into it, and the temperature spikes.
KerbinSrfLandedVABMainBuilding*: The area here is warmer than usual due to all the nonstop activity.
KerbinSrfLandedVABPodMemorial*: The temperature reading here is inspiring. Or sad. You can't decide which.
KerbinSrfLandedVABRoundTank*: Seconds after being attached, the thermometer has frozen into place.
KerbinSrfLandedVABSouthComplex*: Something explodes in the VAB proper, and the temperature ticks up.
KerbinSrfLandedVABTanks*: Seconds after being attached, the thermometer has frozen into place.
KerbinSrfLandedIslandAirfield*: It's quite nice, and there aren't planes everywhere to mess it up.
KerbinSrfLandedWoomerang*: The mountain air is cool, and you suspect it would be quite refreshing to breathe.

MunSrfLanded: The thermometer can't seem to make up its mind whether it's hot or cold here.
MunSrfLandedMidlands: The surface here seems to reflect much of the Sun's heat. As a result, it's slightly cooler here.
MunSrfLandedNorthernBasin: The blackened rocks absorb and radiate large quantities of the Sun's heat. You briefly consider the idea of sunbathing, but realize you don't have a beach towel. Oh well...
MunSrfLandedEastCrater: The temperature fluctuates wildly with the rotation of the moon, as the crater moves from day to night.
MunSrfLandedNorthwestCrater: The inclined angle of the solar radiation at this latitude has a marked effect on the temperature, which you make note of in your report.
MunSrfLandedSouthwestCrater: The temperature here is rather boring. That's right, I said it. Boring. B-O-R-I-N-G!
MunSrfLandedFarsideCrater: Between the dusty atmosphere and the reduced light in the crater it is difficult to read the thermometer.
MunSrfLandedCanyons: The thermometer is shivering from the cold. With the high walls blocking all the sunlight, this is not surprising.
MunSrfLandedPolarCrater: It's pretty cold here. You'd almost think Kerbol hadn't shined in this crater since it was formed!
MunSrfLandedPoles: The thermometer pressure vessel seems to have sprung a leak, and all the liquid inside boiled off into space. Hope the same thing doesn't happen to the rest of the ship.
MunSrfLandedPolarLowlands: Seems like a great snack-time temperature. Let's make it happen!
MunSrfLandedHighlands: The temperature reminds you how nice it really is back on Kerbin.
MunSrfLandedHighlandCraters: The long shadows cast by the crater rims make this region colder than the surrounding highlands.
MunSrfLandedMidlandCraters: You're unsure about the surrounding area, but your temperature is definitely elevated after that landing. Wooohooo!
MunSrfLandedEastFarsideCrater: It's chilly. You want to take a walk around the canyon rim to warm up.
MunSrfLandedTwinCraters: You aren't sure which crater to take the temperature in, so you pick one at random. After all they're twins, so the readings should be the same, right?
MunInSpaceLow: While waiting for the temperature scan to finish, you sketch the patterns made by the Mun's canyons.
The temperature here is wholly uninteresting compared to the box of snacks in your hand.
You read the thermal data and sadly put away your flip-flops.
It appears to be noticeably closer on the dark side of the Mun. Nothing you aren't prepared for though.
You quickly write down your results in the knowledge that thermometers don't often respond well to lithobraking maneuvers.
Well that was quite expected. You consider throwing the thermometer into the microwave just to shake things up.
You use the thermometer to bake your snack at the perfect temperature. Then you send readings back to Mission Control just how they react.
The thermometer barely moves so you give it a good swift kick.
The temperature shifts ever so slowly as you pass sedately over the munar surface.
The temperature in orbit low around the Mun is much as that of outer space. Perhaps because technically it's outer space.
Temperatures here are well within expectations. Still, it's better to have the hard data to confirm.
You have lost the thermometer. You make up some data with impressive-looking figures to send back to KSC. Bigger numbers are better, right?
The Mun doesn't seem to retain the Sun's warmth very well. Thermometer readings on the bright side are still pretty darn cold.
The thermometer is reading the space outside as rather warm. That can't be right... Oh wait, you're sitting on it.
The temperature readings make you glad they installed a heater in this thing. You give it a few swift kicks to make sure it's still working.
The temperature in munar orbit remains fairly constant until you enter the dark side.
The temperature rises and falls as you leave and enter the Mun's shadow.

MinmusSrfLanded*: You secretly wish the temperature were hot enough to cause you to sweat. You packed extra ice cream for the occasion.
MinmusSrfLandedHighlands*: As you suspected, it's very cold here.
MinmusSrfLandedLowlands*: Temperature reads "Minty Fresh". You make an addendum requesting that all future thermometers use numbers instead of wisecracks.
MinmusSrfLandedFlats*: The thermometer suddenly reads NaN. One of the wires must have come loose again.
MinmusSrfLandedGreatFlats*: The ground seems to reflect most of the Sun's thermal energy, yet the ground temperature is much higher than it should be. This warrants further investigation.
MinmusSrfLandedGreaterFlats*: The temperature reading suggests that this area might be made of ice cream. Acquiring a surface sample is highly recommended.
MinmusSrfLandedLesserFlats*: The temperature is just cold enough for a good ice cream cone.
MinmusInSpaceLow*: Minmus is cold, ice cream is cold. Coincidence? You hope not!

DunaSrfLanded: Duna ain't the kind of place to raise your kids; in fact, it's cold as heck. And there's nobody there to raise them, if you did.
DunaSrfLandedPoles: The air is cold. The atmosphere must not support a strong greenhouse effect.
DunaSrfLandedMidlands: Duna is awfully cold for being so red. Not on fire, then.
DunaSrfLandedLowlands: Duna is colder than you expected.
It's even colder here than everywhere else at night.
DunaSrfLandedCraters: You wouldn't know it by looking, but it's actually really cold out here.
The landscape sink means the crater floor is cold. And that's Duna-brand cold.
DunaSrfLandedHighlands: The thin air is colder than anything you'd find on Kerbin.
If the thermometer liquid wasn't in a pressurised tube, it'd freeze solid.
DunaSrfLandedMidlandSea: You thought of desert worlds as hot, not places to freeze your everything off.
Another cold day on Duna. Holidaying on the shores of the Great Western Desert seems very far away now.
DunaSrfLandedNorthernBasin: Coldness is taken as a given on Duna, and this place is no exception. It's freezing.
DunaSrfLandedSouthernBasin: The slushy ice-dust mixture found here cakes to the thermometer, insulating it from the wind.
DunaSrfLandedNorthernShelf: It's Duna, it's cold and it's even colder with the wind chill. What a surprise...and now the thermometer's blown away.
These winds blow straight off the polar ice cap, bringing with them a savagely biting icy chill.
DunaSrfLandedMidlandCanyon: A gentle breeze starts up, and even that is enough to drop the temperature and remind you of how cold it is.
DunaSrfLandedEasternCanyon: The sun breaks through the thick haze, warming the thermometer enough for it to defrost and the gauge to become clearly visible again.
The cold sink nature of the canyon makes this place even more bitingly cold at nights.
DunaSrfLandedWesternCanyon: This canyon doesn't seem as cold as one would expect given its cold sink status.
It's warmer than expected in here. Either it's radiation off the walls or lack of much wind chill.
DunaSrfLandedPolarHighlands: Temperatures range between bitterly cold and cold enough to freeze the very air solid.
DunaSrfLandedPolarCraters: Temperatures fluctuate wildly as the sun changes position in the sky. The depression must act as a cold sink.
DunaFlyingLowPoles: You wish you could drink a cup of hot chocolate, but you can't take your helmet off. The thermometer seems to be frozen solid.
DunaFlyingLowMidlands: Rotating the thermometer to face the sun gives a noticeable temperature increase.
DunaFlyingLowLowlands: You wonder if the wind chill from flying here would be cold enough to freeze the ethanol in the thermometer. Sadly, no.
Temperatures increase as you drop. But not much.
DunaFlyingLowCraters: Enough ice flakes off the thermometer display to see the reading spell 'COLD'
DunaFlyingLowHighlands: The air is far too thin to conduct much heat away from the device.
Rotating the thermometer away from the sun freezes the liquid, towards the sun warms it too much. Gah!
DunaFlyingLowMidlandSea: Temperatures drop when the wind picks up. Drop more, that is.
A dust haze drifts in front of Kerbol, and it gets just that little bit colder.
DunaFlyingLowNorthernBasin: Temperatures decrease noticeably near the basin floor, indicating the presence of a layer of cold air.
DunaFlyingLowSouthernBasin: Well done, Kerlock. You figured out that Duna is cold. Woot!
DunaFlyingLowNorthernShelf: That little bit of altitude doesn't protect you from the icy canyon wind.
DunaFlyingLowMidlandCanyon: The thermometer has a bubble in it, so you gently tap it and end up making even more bubbles.
DunaFlyingLowEasternCanyon: Above the dust, you can finally see the effect sunlight has on the temperature, as though the craft is basking in the feeble warmth.
The sinking cold air is below you up here, and mixes with slightly warmer air from other zones.
DunaFlyingLowWesternCanyon: The thicker air here wicks away heat from the thermometer more effectively than elsewhere.
The canyon walls are channelling warmer air from the Midland Sea to the south, but at a relatively slow wind speed.
DunaFlyingLowPolarHighlands: A gust of even colder air buffets the craft. The temperature drops by another few degrees.
DunaFlyingLowPolarCraters: The thermometer is encrusted with ice, obscuring the display. You note down "cold" as a substitute.
DunaFlyingHigh: The temperature rise from the planet's radiation isn't as much as expected. Perhaps the greenhouse effect here is rather weak.
Turning up the thermostat in your climate controlled suit does little to keep the cold from creeping into your toes.
DunaInSpaceLow: Duna is cold, so thermal back radiation at night is limited. It's definitely there, though.
You hope the atmosphere provides a bit more insulation at the surface than you get in space.
DunaInSpaceHigh: As expected, it's cold at Duna's distance from Kerbol. By extension, Duna is cold as well.
The gauge on the thermometer sticks, so you give it a few taps. It drops another few degrees.

DresSrfLanded*: You wonder if Tanya would like it here. It's much better than Tylo.
DresSrfLandedHighlands*: You think about going home soon, before Dres decides to visit the bad parts of space again.
DresSrfLandedCanyons*: The temperature readings are as dull and unremarkable as Dres itself.

JoolFlyingLow*: Warmer gas from deep within Jool brings the surrounding temperatures up.
Temperatures appear to be quite nominal.
Atmospheric pressure seems to be heating the interior of Jool.
JoolFlyingHigh*: Temperatures are quite high.
Readings appear to be too cool for Jool.
Jool seems to be rich in very dense temperature particles.
JoolInSpaceLow*: The thermometer says the temperature is 7000, but refuses to say what units it is using.

LaytheSrfLanded: It's quite cold here. You would definitely need some warm clothes if you were ever to walk around without your spacesuit.
LaytheSrfLandedPoles: Kerbol looks smaller than you remember, it is not sending much heat to the surface. Why does the water stay liquid?
LaytheSrfLandedShores: The temperature is very similar to that of a refrigerator on steroids, lots of them.
LaytheSrfLandedDunes: Kerbol is small in the sky, but the sand is much warmer than the air. You could almost take your boots off!
LaytheSrfLandedCrescentBay: It's just above the freezing point of water here. A hot beverage would fit in well.
LaytheSrfSplashed: The temperatures here are below water's freezing point. Clearly, the water here has odd characteristics.
LaytheSrfSplashedCrescentBay: Temperatures are similar to those of Kerbin's polar regions. The water hasn't frozen, though, most probably due to the high salinity.
LaytheSrfSplashedTheSagenSea: You wonder why the water isn't frozen.
The temperature is similar to being in a freezer.
LaytheFlyingLow: It's warmer near the surface. Laythe's oceans may indeed be water.
LaytheFlyingLowPoles: Your temperature scan shows that it is too cold to pop a window open.
LaytheFlyingLowShores: Kerbol is small but bright. The cockpit glass is very cold when you touch it.
LaytheFlyingHigh: The temperature fluctuates more than expected.
The thermometer is near -40c. That's as cold as the Kerbin South Pole!
LaytheInSpaceLow: You forgot to cap your drink and it floated away. When you licked up the spill, your tongue froze to the radio panel. Mission Control asked if you were under attack by aliens.
The temperature here looks much the same as anywhere else in space, a few degrees above absolute zero.

VallSrfLanded*: After scraping flecks of ice off the meter, you confirm that yes, it is indeed very cold out.
VallSrfLandedHighlands*: There is a section below the surface that has a surprisingly high temperature for Vall. Being Vall, of course, it's still quite cold.
VallInSpaceLow*: It's incredibly cold, but every now and then the temperature rises. Plumes of heat must be coming off the moon below.

TyloSrfLanded: It reads cold until you realize that all of the liquid in the thermometer has just pooled at the bottom.
TyloInSpaceLow: Reflected light from Tylo doesn't appear to transfer much heat to the surrounding space.

BopSrfLanded: Bop is trying to mislead your temperature readings. Or maybe it's not Bop, but something that lives on Bop?
BopSrfLandedPeaks: The thermometer reads 66.6 degrees Celsius. That... that can't be right.

PolSrfLanded: Scientists have confirmed that there is no temperature present on Pol.
PolSrfLandedHighlands: The temperature varies from one rock formation to another. They must each conduct heat differently.
PolInSpaceLow: The thermometer wants to come inside because it's cold.
The temperature varies as you fly over the terrain.

EelooSrfLanded*: Its a wee bit chilly.
It's too cool for school.
The ground temperature is higher than the surrounding space by a fraction of a degree.
You watch the mercury in the thermometer break through the bottom and freeze.
The display reads "really cold". You make a note to get a less sassy thermometer.
The temperature is surprisingly hot. Perhaps there's some volcanic activity... never mind, someone put the thermometer on upside down.
The thermometer reads "Way too cold." You are beginning to question the qualifications of the science department.
Temperatures have dropped bEeloo zero.
What's colder than being cold? ICE COLD!
The thermometer picks up trace amounts of temperature.
The thermometer seems to be shivering. You give it a scarf and a mug of cocoa.
The mercury seems to have retreated into the bulb at the bottom. You peck at the bulb several times, hoping for it to reemerge. Is it really that cold?
EelooSrfLandedPoles*: The instrumentation appears to have given up.
EelooSrfLandedNorthernGlaciers*: Its cold, but you rub your hands together and think of home, and Moho.
EelooSrfLandedLowlands*: You note the temperature and add a memo that all future manned missions should pack plenty of warm clothes.
The team at Mission Control still doesn't know how this instrument works, but is fascinated by how low the numbers can go.
EelooSrfLandedIceCanyons*: Even the more pleasant parts of this planet are inhospitable.
Why did we go here? It's too cold for anything.
EelooSrfLandedCraters*: This far from the Sun, Eeloo's surface is barely affected by its radiation.
EelooSrfLandedMidlands*: After a long and thoughtful evaluation of the data using knowledge of thermodynamics and various fancy methods, you determine that Eeloo is, in fact, too cold to sensibly enjoy an ice cream cone on.
EelooInSpaceLow*: The thermometer shifts seemingly randomly between "COLD!", "really cold", "coldest", "coldestest", "-273,2 °C" and "Colder than the science department had planned for..."

deployedSeismicSensor.description: A surface-based seismic sensor that records long-term ground activity.
deployedSeismicSensor.hints: Detection of geological events, impacts, subtle vibrations, or the footsteps of an absent-minded Kerbal.

deployedWeatherReport.description: A surface weather station that records atmospheric activity over time.
deployedWeatherReport.hints: Descriptions of atmospheric phenomena such as winds, storms, pressure changes, or temperature trends.

deployedGooObservation.description: A long-term observation of the Mystery Goo after deployment, monitoring its behavior over time through a stationary camera.
deployedGooObservation.hints: Emphasize slow, evolving behavior rather than immediate reactions. The Goo adapts to its surroundings, invents routines, and occasionally shows signs of attachment, boredom, or quiet resistance. On distant or isolated bodies, it may display nostalgia, longing, or a subtle sense of farewell—remaining active, but aware that the mission has moved on without it.

deployedIONCollector.description: A surface ion collector recording long-term particle activity.
deployedIONCollector.hints: Remarks on solar wind intensity, charged particle streams, and other ionospheric or heliospheric phenomena.
